combined_pre_match_text: Argentina fall to Hagi magic. Diego Maradona, responsible for the greatest shock of the World Cup so far, turned up at the Rose Bowl yesterday to witness shock No2. Half the crowd rose in excited animation as he took his seat in the broadcasting box, little realising there would be small cause for further Argentinian excitement all day. Romania deserved their victory because they produced three superlative goals to a debatable penalty and a late consolation. In the second half especially Argentina were clearly fitter and less tired, but still had no answer to the class and penetration of Hagi and Dumitrescu. Romania are fast emerging as the surprise team of the tournament and Hagi its outstanding individual. The goal he conjured for Dumitrescu in the 17th minute was one of the best seen in this World Cup and several before it. Dumitrescu's second goal restored the lead to Romania five minutes after Argentina had equalised his first. The opening 10 minutes of the match gave no sign of what was to follow with the Romanians content to sit in their own half and contain Argentina's probing passes and runs. Again without the apparently injured Caniggia, Argentina's attack lacked some of its customary pace and verve, but Prunea in the Romanian goal still had his work cut out early on. Balbo scorned the best chance of all in the 10th minute, when Simeone dribbled the ball Maradona-style to the edge of the Romanian area, then supplied the striker in a position from where it appeared he could not miss. But, unable to believe his luck, he delayed a fraction too long and allowed Prunea to dive at his feet and beat the ball away. Seconds later, utterly against the run of play, Romania took the lead. Argentina underestimated the danger from a free-kick 35 yards out and, faced with only a three-man wall, Dumitrescu confidently curled a right-foot shot into the far corner before Islas had a chance to move. Argentina continued to press, without directly threatening Prunea until Batistuta tricked two defenders with a back-heel turn on the goal-line and received a sufficient nudge from one of them, Prodan, for the referee to point to the penalty spot. Batistuta stepped up to score his first goal since his hat-trick against Greece. If Romania were now expected to roll over and die no one told Hagi. Playing a clever one-two to buy space and time on the right wing, the Romanian playmaker slotted a wonderfully precise ball forward into the penalty area, where Dumitrescu was able to flick it past Islas at the near post without perceptibly breaking stride. Batistuta opened the second half with a blistering shot which Prunea saved, then Redondo had a strong appeal for a penalty rejected. Bold and determined, Redondo was just imposing himself when Dumitrescu broke away to set up Hagi for the killer goal. Selymes was screaming for the ball on the left but Dumitrescu waited until Hagi arrived on the right, rolling the ball before him to allow a crashing shot into the net. Ba

combined_pre_match_text: Penalties sink England again. England are out of the World Cup, for a second time their conquerors are Argentina, and for a third major tournament in the 90s English hopes have perished in a penalty shoot-out. On this occasion human frailty, rather than any Hand of God, contributed to their downfall. One flash of petulance from David Beckham, who was sent off, forced England to defend for more than an hour with 10 men. There was no golden goal and neither, for England, was there anything to be found at the end of the rainbow. England 's supporters greeted the onset of penalties with a fervent rendition of the National Anthem. The Almighty having been implored to save the Queen, David Seaman responded by saving Hernan Crespo's penalty only for Carlos Roa to keep out the next, from Paul Ince, in similar fashion, guessing right and diving left. With Argentina leading 4-3 the last of the regulation 10 penalties fell to David Batty, who had replaced Darren Anderton early in extra-time. As a marksman Batty has always fallen a mite short of William Tell and Roa kept out his poorly directed kick. So Argentina meet Holland in Marseille on Saturday in a quarter-final which will be a reprise of the 1978 final while England return home. They recovered from falling behind to an early penalty to draw level with another. Then Michael Owen gave them the lead with marvellous individualism. By half-time Javier Zanetti had brought the scores level but it was Beckham 's dismissal early in the second half, for kicking out in retaliation after being fouled by Diego Simeone, which changed the whole nature of the match. England 's rearguard action was in the best tradition of the country's stoicism under siege - Mafeking, Ladysmith, the Blitz and all that - but the fact remains they had been put in this situation by Beckham's foolishness. In fact Hoddle had warned the young Manchester United player about the consequences of such rash responses a year earlier, during the Tournoi de France. It was all the more frustrating for England because while Argentina showed their usual sound technique and passed the ball with accuracy and imagination they were not, on the night, demonstrably better. Had Beckham stayed on England might well have won, given the opposition's obvious distress in the first half whenever Owen took their defenders on for pace. To compound England's disappointment, their 10 men had what might well have been a winner disallowed nine minutes from the end of normal time. Anderton's corner reached Sol Campbell at the far post and he nodded the ball down past Roa. Several England players danced with delight off the field, not realising the Danish referee Kim Nielsen had ruled out the effort because Alan Shearer, in making the initial challenge for the corner, had used an elbow. And early in extra-time the referee failed to spot Jose Chamot's handball in Argentina 's area. So an evening which at one point had promised a settling of old scores with Argentina and

David Lacey: World Cup 2002 comment. World Cups have become as much tests of endurance as ability. The team that emerges triumphant from the 2002 tournament will have shown superior stamina as well as the prime footballing virtues of good technique and organisation. If the latest champions do not belong to that exclusive brethren of previous winners it will be a considerable surprise. So far 16 World Cups have been won by seven different countries and although the present chances of three of these - England, Germany and Uruguay - appear slim, one of the remaining four will surely be parading the Fifa trophy after the final in Yokohama on June 30. The smart money is more or less divided evenly between France, the World Cup holders and European champions, and Argentina, who have the best-looking squad. Yet there is a sneaking regard for Italy; and Brazil, for all their problems in the qualifiers, may well begin to thrive once they pick up the familiar scent of past glories. But it is going to be a slog for all who reach the later stages. Holding the tournament a fortnight earlier than usual has halved the recovery time for the European-based players who make up the bulk of the finalists. Playing it in two countries will complicate the logistics even if teams do not have to trudge back and forth across different time zones, as they did in USA 94. The bigger the World Cup has grown the more risk there has been of a final being contested by weary players who cannot wait to get home. If the final of 1998 was an improvement on 1994 and 1990, it was only because of the alacrity with which France seized the moment after Brazil's fateful change of mind over leaving out a befuddled Ronaldo. The last half-decent contest was the final of 1986 when Diego Maradona's through-pass to Jorge Burruchaga ended another German recovery to regain the World Cup for Argentina. That was also the last tournament to be dominated by the genius of a single player, hand of God notwithstanding. The World Cup is nothing if it is not remembered for great deeds of outstanding individuals, and although the frenetic speed of the modern game argues against the emergence of another Maradona, Pele or Johan Cruyff to leave his singular mark on a tournament, enough gifted footballers are lining up in Japan and South Korea to capture the imagination, even if only fleetingly. Will the world worship at the feet of Zinedine Zidane or is the Frenchman to be upstaged by Ronaldo's renaissance? Is the real Juan Sebastian Veron about to shake off his winter hibernation at Old Trafford or could Francesco Totti steal the show? All of England's woes would be forgotten were Michael Owen to strike a rich scoring vein, and Portuguese hopes will soar now that Luis Figo is said to have fully recovered from ankle trouble. World Cup in brief. · Italy and Croatia each had four players selected during training for drug tests by Fifa in the first random checks to be carried out before a World Cup. "The aim is that each team will have been tested at least once between the qualifying and the finals," Fifa's head of communications Keith Cooper said. Among the Croatian players tested was Alen Boksic who has recovered from an abdominal strain and should be fit for the start of the tournament. · Ariel Ortega will wear No10 for Argentina following Fifa's decision to bar the south Americans from retiring the shirt in honour of Diego Maradona. Argentina's third-choice goalkeeper Roberto Bonano was originally expected to take the No10 shirt but he will now wear 23. · Turkey's team manager has hit back at Brazil's Roberto Carlos for saying that Brazil would beat Turkey at 40% strength. Can Cobanoglu pointed out that Carlos was in the Real Madrid team beaten by the Turkish side Galatasaray, for whom six of the World Cup squad play, in the 2000 Super Cup. "I well remember how he cried frantically when Real Madrid lost the Super Cup," Cobanoglu said. "How many per cent of his capabilities did he use in that match? Everybody will see that no one can tease with Turkey." The sides meet in Ulsan on June 3. · Southampton's striker Agustin Delgado returned to action for Ecuador after injury but failed to score as the south Americans were held to a 1-1 draw by the J-League side Kyoto Purple Sanga. Paraguay recorded their third successive win with a 2-0 victory over Urawa Red Diamonds. · Around 10,000 tickets for the opening match between France and Senegal on Friday have been put on sale. Fifa has returned the tickets to Korean organisers because of low demand. · Cameroon have decided to spend an extra three days at their preliminary World Cup base in Nakatsue after arriving four days later than scheduled in the southern Japanese village. The African champions will now move to their next camp in Fujiyoshida, two hours south of Tokyo, after their World Cup opener against Ireland on Saturday. · Italy and Croatia each had four players selected during training for drug tests by Fifa in the first random checks to be carried out before a World Cup.

Senegal. The team Track record Runners-up at the African Cup of Nations but the big stage is new to the Dakar Lions who have never before graced the World Cup. How they qualified Unexpected winners of an African group that included previous qualifiers Morocco, Egypt and Algeria. In their last match, needing a 1-0 win against Namibia to qualify, they scored five. United or Unibond? Newcomers, but on the rise - Cheltenham, perhaps? All but three of the squad play in France, and the large contingent of Lens players will have to try to forget losing the title to Lyon on the final day of the season. Competing interests Amy Mbacke Thiam is the world 400 metres champion while DeSagana Diop, 20, is a rising US basketball star with Cleveland Cavaliers. An average of 1.4 points per game, however, suggests he has a way to go yet. Penalty pedigree Talk of shoot-outs is a no-no. El Hadji Diouf and Aliou Cisse both missed against Cameroon, costing Senegal this year's African Cup of Nations. Technical area Attack, attack, attack. Senegal are solid in the centre of midfield and pacy down the flanks but that masks a vulnerable underbelly. Rabble rouser Khalilou Fadiga earned the mantle of team head-case after enduring a month in a Belgian prison for punching a fan. Celebrity supporter Senegal-born Patrick Vieira will shrug off divided loyalties in the opening match of the tournament, while Senegal's most famous crooner Youssou N'Dour, singer of the official France 98 theme tune, considers himself the Lions' guardian angel. Wonder of the world Diouf is African footballer of the year, having scored nine times in 12 World Cup qualifying games, including two hat-tricks. Having starred for Lens, the peroxide blond intends to "set the World Cup alight". Oscar nominee Diouf is known as much for his histrionics as his goals. Dubbed "Serial Killer" by fans, he was turfed out of Rennes - via an untimely car crash - before showing signs of maturity at Lens. Then came a tirade against the "amateurish" Senegalese football federation. "Our preparation has been sabotaged," he said, pointing to the lack of World Cup warm-up games. Song for the summer Diouf should bellow Death in Vegas's Aisha at defenders, in particular the verse that begins: "I think you ought to know I'm a serial killer." David Lacey's verdict Have drawn the short straw in being pitted against the holders in the opening match although their burgeoning talents could surprise not only France but also Uruguay and Denmark. Much will depend on the creative ability of Fadiga, as well as his skill at set pieces. The coach Bruno Metsu Words of wisdom "The last thing I want is us to be the Jamaica of this World Cup. I don't want us to be the charming team nobody takes seriously." Story so far With his flowing locks, Metsu looks more ageing rocker than manager. Spells at Beauvais, Lille, Sedan, Valenciennes and Valence hardly suggest a polished pedigree, but he has inspired startling progress since assuming the internat

[ARTICLE_SEP]

France. Track record The holders have won every competition they have entered since 1996 and with a largely settled team. In short, they're magnifique. How they qualified Automatic qualification as champions but a 2-1 home defeat by Belgium and victory over Scotland by only 5-0 suggests they may yet be affected by lack of competitive match practice. United or Unibond? United, and then some. The only hope for others is if Frank Leboeuf is chosen as Marcel Desailly's defensive partner. Or if Fabien Barthez flips his lid in the oriental heat. Competing interests Also Six Nations champions in rugby - give the French a ball to kick these days and you may as well give them the winners' trophy as well. Penalty pedigree Infuriatingly, they're also pretty good at these. In 1986 they put the Brazilians out 4-3, with Italy going the same way four years ago. Only the West Germans (surprise, surprise) have prevailed (5-4) against them in the lottery and that was 20 years ago. Technical area How can you go wrong with these players at your disposal? Just put them on the pitch and let them play. Rabble rouser Lacking a Gazza but no shortage of eccentrics. For example, Emmanuel Petit on dogs. "They're nice," he says. "They can sleep anywhere, they wag their tails and on top of that they get stroked all the time." Barking mad. Celebrity supporter Johnny Depp is clearly a big admirer of French form having just had his second baby with Vanessa Paradis. Wonder of the world ZZ is still very much top. Packed as their squad is with quality, Zinedine Zidane remains the outstanding talent on offer, given a free role behind a lone striker. Excessive sweat problem aids his slipperiness. Oscar nominee The likes of David Ginola and Patrick Battiston - that was never a foul by Schumacher, honest - are long gone, with Leboeuf's incessant whingeing and occasional plummet to earth the nearest the current crop come to "doing a Jacques Cousteau". Song for the summer If they win, then Britney's Oops, I Did It Again, or maybe their own Daft Punk's One More Time. Either way they'll be mighty smug. David Lacey's verdict Still in their prime as world and European champions, the French are enjoying an esteem previously surpassed by only Brazil, Italy, Austria in the 1930s and the best Dutch and West German sides of the 70s. Zidane can keep the World Cup in France but Robert Pires is a bad loss and without Laurent Blanc the defence could be vulnerable. The coach: Roger Lemerre Words of wisdom "As soon as I heard we were to play Senegal in our first match I feared the worst." That's the world champions against a side who have never previously played in a World Cup. How to dampen expectations. Story so far As a player won six international caps, then led Sedan and Nantes to French Cup finals before joining the France Football Federation in 1986 to coach the military team. Since taking over in 1998 his main problem has been with the press - he has refused all interview requests since October

Who will you support?. If dramatists on commercial channels are irritated by commercial breaks, the real victim of this funding punctuation has been Desmond Lynam. Denied the space before and during games that made him such a deadly striker of one-liners at the 1998 World Cup, he's regarded as having struggled to justify his transfer fee to ITV. There are also questions over Des's recovery from the ego-injury caused by the peak-time failure of his main ITV series The Premiership. So the main televisual interest of the World Cup is seeing Des go head-to-head at the highest level, for the first time, with his BBC replacement Gary Lineker. Lynam gets in the first kick - as his employers have live rights to the first game, France v Senegal, in World Cup 2002 Live (Friday, 11am, ITV1). But, when the BBC starts screening live games on the Saturday, will he regret his move? And - if England prosper and matches are shown by both sides in the later stages - Lynam faces the biggest test of the quality for which ITV partly signed him. Can he reduce the natural switch towards the BBC on national occasions? What seems to be the main change from previous tournaments is an increase in the supply of comedy programmes. This is partly because the past option of patriotic fervour is both out of cultural fashion and hard to sustain after 36 years without an England victory. It's also a scheduling response to the fact that this World Cup offers no live material in peak-time. So evening surrogates include Alistair McGowan's Big World Cup (Friday, 8.30pm, BBC1). Often before a big tournament, there's a feeling that a certain player has come on form at just the right time and that's certainly true for McGowan in the sense that thin-faced men - David Beckham, Sven-Goran Eriksson and Gary Lineker - are the leading participants in 2002. Four years ago - with the chubbier Desmond Lynam and Glen Hoddle in charge - McGowan would have struggled. There's also Match Of The World Cup (Monday-Wednesday, 10.35pm, BBC1) - in which Paul Whitehouse, as Ron Manager, presents archive moments from previous cups - and Johnny Vaughan's World Cup Extra (Friday, 11.20pm, BBC1), in which another big transfer signing who hasn't so far matched achievement to fee, tries to do as a solo dribbler the kind of after-match chat and gags which Baddiel & Skinner hosted last time for ITV as a one-two. In fact, the only piece of thematic scheduling that seems to be missing is an episode of Holby City in which Mr Mayer, after retraining from cardiology to orthopaedics, miraculously heals the feet of England's absentee midfielders. McAteer given green light. Jason McAteer has been passed fit to play for the Republic of Ireland in their opening World Cup game with Cameroon tomorrow. The Sunderland midfielder was doubtful after suffering a badly bruised left knee during last weekend's friendly against Hiroshima in Izumo. But manager Mick McCarthy reported a clean bill of health for his entire squad after they trained in the magnificent Big Swan stadium here today. "Everybody is fine and fit to play - and that clears Jason," said McCarthy. "We had a few people sit out training because of blisters or minor bangs, but everybody is fit to play." McAteer is set to line up on the right-hand side of midfield, although there is speculation as to who will be on the opposite flank. McCarthy may decide to rest McAteer's Sunderland colleague Kevin Kilbane and instead push Ian Harte into midfield, with his Leeds team-mate Gary Kelly moving in at left-back. Kenny Cunningham is fit to partner Steve Staunton in the centre of defence, and Mark Kinsella will replace rebel former skipper Roy Keane in the heart of midfield. McCarthy revealed leading the team out tomorrow would be the proudest moment of his career. "As a player, playing in the 1990 World Cup was my proudest moment, but that is history and so this will take over," said McCarthy. "After everything that has gone on it will be a relief to get out there. "It is certainly a feather in my cap to have been a captain at a World Cup finals and now a manager, and when we walked out on to the pitch to train, I knew that this is what it is all about." The Republic will be under pressure to get a result against Cameroon and justify McCarthy's decision to send home Keane last week following their bust-up. But the manager has every faith in his players. He said: "The character of my squad is brilliant. We have had two defeats in 20 games, we qualified from a group with Holland and Portugal in it, and beat Iran over two legs, so I cannot believe anyone would question the character of my squad now. "They are top class, each and every one of them. "It is important not to be beaten in the first game, but sometimes you have to settle for what you get. "Who knows what will happen over three games, but to give yourself a good start you don't want to be beaten, and we'll certainly try and win it." Steve Staunton will captain the team in the absence of Keane and win his 99th cap in the process. He hailed team spirit and promised a battling performance. "The squad has been the same for the last 15 years with a few new faces coming in," said Staunton. "Team morale is the same as ever, always upbeat and we'll always give 100%. "Whether it is good enough on the day we'll have to wait and see, but we'll definitely go down fighting if we are going to go down." Jason McAteer has been passed fit to play for the Republic of Ireland in their opening World Cup game with Cameroon tomorrow.

Ireland gear up for Cameroon. Ireland manager Mick McCarthy is likely to have a fully fit squad to choose from for the side's opening World Cup match as he tries to put the Roy Keane controversy behind him. Injury concerns over midfielders Jason McAteer, Mark Kinsella and Kevin Kilbane eased today when the trio joined in full training, just two days before Ireland's first group E game against Cameroon in Niigata. The Keane saga has hit the Ireland camp hard, and McCarthy knows nothing less than a successful tournament will cement his vulnerable position. "We just want to put all the problems behind us and start winning games," he told reporters. "The players have worked hard and are concentrating on the important business." Keane, the Ireland captain, was sent home after a bust-up with McCarthy and despite speculation he could make a dramatic return, that possibility has now been ruled out. Ireland's appeal to FIFA to call up Colin Healy as a replacement for Keane was unsuccessful, leaving McCarthy with just 22 players at his disposal. McAteer will start on the right side of midfield if he can convince McCarthy he has recovered from a knee injury. "I will only pick Jason if he's 100% fit," McCarthy said. Kinsella sat out training on Wednesday to rest a knee problem but returned today and is set to take his place alongside Matt Holland as Keane's deputy in central midfield. Kilbane could play on the left of midfield after shaking off an ankle knock. Ireland, who reached the 1990 World Cup quarter-finals, also face Germany and Saudi Arabia in group E. Ireland manager Mick McCarthy is likely to have a fully fit squad to choose from for his side's opening World Cup match as he tries to put the Roy Keane controversy behind him.

Republic of Ireland. The team Track record Glory in Italia 90 when they reached the quarter-finals and 300,000 turned up to welcome them back to Dublin. Made it to 1994 as well, where they famously beat Italy, but the rest could be written on the back of a postage stamp. How they qualified Exceeded all expectations to finish as group runners-up. Started off with a 2-2 draw in Amsterdam, held the Portuguese to draws home and away, and put Holland out of the reckoning with victory at home. Comfortably won their play-off against Iran. United or Unibond? Plucky battlers punching above their weight, by reputation, though playing decent stuff too. The Charlton Athletic of Europe, if not yet the world. Competing interests All but unbeatable in the Eurovision Song Contest, but otherwise little to shout about since Sonia O'Sullivan's double world cross-country title in 1998. Even the Irish-dancing world championships are dominated by Americans and Canadians. Penalty pedigree Which Irishman can forget the shoot-out victory over Romania in 1990? Packie Bonner's heroics, David O'Leary's cool aim, Jack Charlton losing the plot. Technical area 4-4-2 with Robbie Keane and Damien Duff up front and two wide men, Kevin Kilbane and Jason McAteer. Pray that Roy Keane stays fit and bring on big Niall when all is lost. Rabble rouser Without any question, Richard Dunne. You don't earn the nickname Honey Monster without knowing how to knock back a quick pint or eight. The worst time-keeper in the world, according to Joe Royle. Celebrity supporter Larry Mullen (U2 drummer), a big mate of physiotherapist Mick Byrne, follows them round the world. Father Jack has a picture of the team in his lounge. Wonder of the world Roy Keane. Even more important for his country than he is for his club. As an Iranian journalist asked Mick McCarthy before the qualifier in Tehran: "Should you be renamed the Republic of Roy Keane?" Oscar nominee Robbie Keane - time in the Leeds reserves has allowed him to learn the art of belly-flopping over imaginary tackles. An honorary mention, as well, to Duff. Song for the summer New Order: Everything's Gone Green (B-side, Gomez: Hangover). David Lacey's verdict Already McCarthy has provided the perfect response to his Irish critics by qualifying at Holland's expense. Yet McCarthy and his players will expect to do more than just enjoy a brief World Cup and go home. In Roy Keane they have one of the game's strongest midfielders and there is enough talent in the side to go beyond the opening phase. The coach Mick McCarthy Words of wisdom "You need tough skin in this job. I guess you could say I've got a neck like a jockey's bollocks." Story so far Had a tough job following Jack Charlton and there were numerous calls for him to be sacked when Ireland failed to reach the last World Cup and two European Championships. A hero now, though, and a genuinely nice guy, too. Sobbed like a child in Tehran airport after his players had overcome Iran in the qualifyin

Cameroon. Track record Have qualified for the fifth time. Caused a shock in 1990 by beating Argentina with about six men and reaching the quarter-finals before being knocked out by England. Have been eliminated at the group stage of both subsequent tournaments. How they qualified Won their first five matches to ensure qualification from their group. They conceded only four goals throughout - two fewer than Patrick Mboma scored on his own. United or Unibond? Rising stars. The Indomitable Lions won their second successive Nations Cup in February and also hold the Olympic title. Powerful, skilful and well organised. Leicester City of two years ago but with brighter prospects. Competing interests Football is the only sport at which the country excels on the international stage but watch out for Claudine Komgang, a 400 metres runner, at this summer's Commonwealth Games. Penalty pedigree Never involved in a World Cup shoot-out but won their last two African Nations Cups and their Olympic title on penalties. Technical area Winfried Schafer can be expected to go 3-5-2 and use a passing game. There is massive physical presence through the spine and greater trickery out wide. Long throws are common to exploit the team's height. Rabble rouser Basically a sensible bunch. Rigobert Song has a wild reputation but that is for antics on the pitch. He was the first player to be sent off in two World Cups and is now on a hat-trick. Celebrity supporter Yannick Noah. The 1983 French Open winner has a Cameroonian father and Cameroonians like to think of him as one of their own. Wonder of the world Watch out for Salomon Olembe, an attacking left-sided midfielder. Small, skilful and quick, the 21-year-old played at France 98. He helped Nantes to the French title in 2001 but fell out of favour this season. Oscar nominee The problem with Cameroon is not diving but getting them to ground at all. They are a huge team, particularly through the centre - the likes of Marc-Vivien Foe should not be accused of diving if you value your life. Song for the summer Anything but The Lion Sleeps Tonight - particularly (given their height) not the version by They Might Be Giants. David Lacey's verdict Probably Africa's best chance of making serious progress in this World Cup, given Nigeria's uncertainties. Cameroon look more disciplined than in the past. Sch5fer has successfuly combined new blood with more experienced players and built a team capable of counter-attacking quickly from deep, maybe at times too deep, defensive positions. The coach: Winfried Schafer Words of wisdom "Cameroon is a wonderful team. Our objective is to go as far as possible. If we play concentrated, total football and feel positive, we should have no problem." Story so far Became Cameroon's fourth coach of 2001 last September when the sports ministry sought German discipline to complement the natural talent. Sch5fer responded by leading Cameroon to the African Nations Cup. His most recent club job was with Secon

Well, it isn't that Carr fella, and I don't see anyone else who answers to the name Steve or Steven in the Irish World Cup squad. You've obviously made him up. No, I haven't. Reid is a product of Millwall's youth system who was drafted into the Irish squad as a last minute replacement for injured winger Mark Kennedy. I see. Is he as promising as the Steven Reid that played for England under 16s a few years ago? He is the Steven Reid that played for England's under 16s a few years ago. You're kidding. So, what's he doing playing for Ireland? His late father was from Galway, which makes him eligible for the Republic. Some eye-catching performances in the Millwall side that won promotion to the First Division last season earned young Steven a call-up to the Irish Under 21s and he hasn't looked back since. He helped Millwall reach the First Division play-offs this year and won his first cap for the Irish senior team in a 2-2 draw against Croatia last August. His second appearance came against Russia in February. Ah yes, that "difficult" second appearance. How did he fare? Very well, as it happens. He scored after three minutes with a sublime 20-yard volley and Ireland eventually won 2-0. His was the name on all Irish supporters' lips, once they found out what it was. And how has he got on since? Grand. He's doubled his tally of caps without ever finishing on a losing Irish side and is reckoned to have the most artistic right foot available to Mick McCarthy. He's no David Beckham, but he can play anywhere in midfield, he's good in the air, handy at set pieces and packs a mean cross. He's quite aggressive too - only one Irish player in the English league has a worse disciplinary record than him. Don't tell me . . . Don't be so presumptious, it's not Roy Keane. It's actually some bloke by the name of Sean McCarthy who plays for Plymouth. And before you ask, he's not related to Mick. I must say, it's a good job young Reid was available to fill in for Mark Kennedy at such short notice. It could have been tricky if, say, he'd been taking his girlfriend to Barbados for a holiday when he got called up for World Cup duty. It's funny you should say that. Why? Because he was taking his girlfriend to Barbados for a holiday when he got called up for World Cup duty. Did they get the pilot to turn the plane around? Er, no. They were only on the way to the airport so they got his brother, who was giving them a lift, to turn the car around. It's not as dramatic, but it's still a good story. Hip injury hampers Ljungberg. Freddie Ljungberg faces an anxious 24-hour wait before learning whether he will be fit to play against England in Sunday's World Cup opener. The Arsenal midfielder limped out of this morning's Sweden training session after just five minutes complaining of a pain in his hip. While Sweden attempted to play down the scare to their key player Ljungberg admitted that he first felt the injury a week ago and aggravated it in Saturday's 1-1 draw against Japan. "When I first felt it a couple of days before the Japan match I didn't take much notice because I really wanted to play in that game," said Ljungberg, who spent the rest of today's session lying prostrate on his back. "It didn't happen in an accident, it just came on slowly. But I felt it again today so I stopped training. "It hurt a little bit when I ran. I tried to continue training but I decided not to risk anything. Hopefully there will be no problem but I can't say anything for sure until after 24 hours. "I'm a bit annoyed. I felt it against Japan but didn't have any treatment. I thought it would disappear after a few days. "It is hard to say now how it is but I don't think there will be a problem." It would be a major boost for England if Ljungberg was forced to pull out of the first match in Group F. The Swede was the pivot around Arsenal's Double winning run-in, his surging runs and all-round exuberance bringing him seven goals in his last eight appearances for the Gunners. In contrast, he has scored just twice for Sweden for whom he is usually employed in a much more defensive holding role. "Everyone has a responsibility in this Sweden team," Ljungberg said. "My roles for Arsenal and Sweden are very different. I have not scored many goals for the national team but I have had some good chances. It is up to the coaches to tell me how to play. "The Swedish team plays very differently from Arsenal. There is more freedom with Arsenal and they play a passing game." Ljungberg, however, is desperate to play on Sunday in Saitama, especially as he will almost certainly be pitched against David Beckham if, as expected, the England captain recovers from his broken foot. World Cup special. WOULD ARGENTINA KEEP THE TROPHY? When Brazil won the Jules Rimet trophy for the third time in 1970, they were allowed to keep it. If Argentina or Germany triumph this year, they would match that achievement, having won the new trophy for the third time. But would Fifa give them the new trophy to keep?" asks Colin Glen. No they wouldn't, Colin. All they would get - like every other World Cup winning team - would be temporary possession of the trophy for four years and a gold-plated replica. Harsh? Well, perhaps. But Fifa's position is understandable. After all, they gave away the original Jules Rimet trophy - a pure gold depiction of Nike of Samothrace designed by Abel Lafleur - to Brazil only to see it stolen in 1983 and then smelted. Another reason for their reluctance is cost. The current trophy - 5kg of solid gold and malachite - cost $50,000 to make back in 1970 and is worth several millions of pounds today. When it was made, Italian sculptor Silvio Gazzaniga described his creation in the following grandiose terms: "The lines spring out from the base, rising in spirals, stretching out to receive the world. From the remarkable dynamic tensions of the compact body of the sculpture rise the figures of two athletes at the stirring moment of victory". It might sound pretentious to you and me, but Fifa clear agree that it's something special too - which is why their regulations state the trophy will, at all times, remain theirs. United or Unibond? Winning the 1996 Olympics hinted at a move to the big time, but Kanu & Co have since produced little to cherish. An international Blackburn Rovers. Competing interests Nigeria are Africa's women's football champions. They also produced the reigning Olympic wrestling champion Daniel Igali, though he is now a Canadian citizen. Technical area Likely to play a back four but have experimented up front. Adegboyega Onigbinde generally likes to use wingers but has also tried using two link-strikers in Nwankwo Kanu and Austin "Jay-Jay" Okocha. Rabble rouser Celestine Babayaro is known to have enjoyed the odd night out in London, and has (allegedly) been involved in an orgy with Dwight Yorke. Then again, who hasn't? Celebrity supporter Sade is partly Nigerian. In the Super Eagles' case perhaps it ain't over until the thin lady sings. Wonder of the world Okocha and Kanu are frequently mentioned, but keep an eye on Julius Aghahowa, a striker with Shakhtar Donetsk of Ukraine. The youngster has pace, a strong shot and can play wide or through the centre, but it is with his back-flips that he really catches the eye. Oscar nominee Nigeria are not notorious divers, although Aghahowa's acrobatics mark him down as a candidate - he makes Lomana Lua-Lua look as flexible as Robert Maxwell. Song for the summer Fly Like an Eagle by the Steve Miller band seems the only appropriate choice - until the Super Eagles are brought down to earth. David Lacey's verdict Memories of the way Nigeria strode into the last World Cup by beating Spain before winning their group are sufficiently fresh for their chances to be talked up this time. But they did come down to earth with a wallop in the second round, losing 4-1 to Denmark, and in spite of the continued presence of Okocha and Kanu will need to show much greater consistency now. England far from favourites to win World Cup. Sven-Göran Eriksson described England as dark horses for the 2002 World Cup, and few would argue. With an uneven track record in major tournaments, no form to speak of in the past six months and a whole stable full of injuries, they will certainly not be starting among the favourites. As ever, there is a distinction to be made between teams thought capable of winning the World Cup, like Spain and England, and teams who probably will win it, like Argentina or France. The second list is never very long, it is almost exclusively drawn from previous winners within the past quarter century or so, and this year is shorter than ever because the two most historically successful nations - Brazil and Germany - are thought to be in decline. In Germany's case that is probably not a misreading, and in addition to an unconvincing qualifying campaign under Rudi Völler - almost on borrowed time with Ottmar Hitzfeld waiting in the wings - the 1990 winners have lost even more players to injury than England. Further, Group E is one of the most open and difficult to predict, and it may only take a small upset - defeat by Ireland or Cameroon, say, to send the Germans home after the first stage for a second successive competition. That said, whoever wins Group E should have a simple passage to the quarter-finals against the runners-up from Group B, so it is quite possible to envisage Germany, Cameroon or even Ireland in the last eight. Similarly, Brazil are miles behind Argentina as the leading team in South America, yet have a more favourable draw here. Whereas Argentina have to overcome Nigeria, England and Sweden, and may then face a second-round game against France, Brazil will have no trouble escaping the feeble Group C and probably will not meet a decent team until the quarter-final. Yet the reality of 32-nation World Cups is that for most leading teams the going only gets tough at the quarter-final stage. England were unlucky last time to come up against Argentina in the second round, but only had themselves to blame after losing to Romania in the group stage. They have been unlucky again this year, not only having to survive the most difficult group but ending up in the side of the draw that includes France and Brazil. Their objective must be to try and win group F, so as to avoid France in the second round, though Argentina will be pursuing exactly the same aim. It has to be conceded Marcelo Bielsa's players look the better bet. What would England give for a left-sided player like Real Madrid's Santiago Solari, one of the stars of the Spanish club's successful Champions League campaign? Solari, astonishingly, cannot even find a place in the Argentine squad, though he would walk into almost any other. Whereas England are still toying with the idea of Emile Heskey on the left of midfield Argentina can field three naturally left-sided players in Diego Placente, Juan Pablo Sorin and Kily Gonzalez. Sven: 'Beckham is ready for Sweden'. David Beckham is expected to be fit for the World Cup opener with Sweden on Sunday after coming through an intensive session of running and taking free-kicks this morning at England's training camp in Awaji. The England captain will resume full training either later today or on Wednesday, and coach Sven-Goran Eriksson is confident he will be fully recovered from his broken foot injury for the match in Saitama. "David did a session this morning and he did everything - free-kicks, corners, a lot of shooting and running and he felt no pain at all," Eriksson confirmed. "He will now hopefully join in with everyone else on Tuesday afternoon or Wednesday and, if there is not a reaction after that, then he will be ready for Sweden. "Is it a risk to play him against Sweden? I don't think so. If things go as we think they will then, yes, he will start." Eriksson added: "I do not think that tackling will be a problem although maybe on the first day he will not do that with his left foot. "But I think that will not be a problem. I think he will be able to tackle okay and he has to play normal football to be ready for Sunday." Eriksson admitted: "Of course we have a lot of options for the right-sided position. We can play other people there - but there is not another David Beckham. "What he brings to us with things like free-kicks and corners and set-pieces helps a tremendous lot." But the news is less good for Kieron Dyer, who is still struggling to prove that he can be part of Eriksson's final 23 after suffering a knee injury on the final day of the season. Eriksson confirmed Dyer was now the only person who was a significant doubt to face the Swedes and knows he has to decide soon whether to select the Newcastle player or draft in a replacement. He said: "In terms of a decision, we have to decide on Thursday or Friday to decide whether to keep him here or not." Blackburn midfielder David Dunn and Leeds striker Alan Smith, who has also been playing in a wide role, are ready to answer the call if Dyer fails to win his fitness fight. Nicky Butt (knee), David Seaman (groin) and Robbie Fowler (hip) are all set to take part in full training tomorrow afternoon. Murphy has no chance of recovering in time to play in the tournament, after fracturing the second metatarsal bone of his left foot during training earlier this week. The news, confirmed this morning after a scan of Murphy's foot at the England team's camp in Jeju, South Korea, makes him the third member of the team - after captain David Beckham and defender Gary Neville - to sustain that particular injury. Neville has already been ruled out but there are hopes Beckham will be fit to play in the tournament. Murphy follows Liverpool team-mate Steven Gerrard in being ruled out of the England squad with injury. England's latest injury blow opens the way again for West Ham's Trevor Sinclair, who is on standby. Sinclair flew home earlier this week after Eriksson picked Newcastle United's Kieron Dyer - also recovering from injury - for his squad instead. As a midfield player, Murphy's injury is of particular concern because midfielders Beckham, Nicky Butt, and Dyer are all yet to be declared fit. Manchester United star Beckham is doing light training but has not kicked a ball with his left foot since it was injured more than three weeks ago. He is doubtful for England's opening World Cup game against Sweden next Sunday. Injury farce continues as third England player sustains fractured metatarsal.

Raul told to 'step up' for Spain. When Sir Alex Ferguson was in charge of Aberdeen he once punished his team for a poor performance by making them jog up and down the city's main shopping street wearing studs. If Spain's players were required to humiliate themselves in public in similar fashion every time they flopped in a major championship, they would be almost permanently on the road, from Malaga to Bilbao. Spain confirmed they are such masters of underachievement at France 98 and Euro 2000 that they have barely merited a mention as potential winners of this World Cup. All but one of their squad plays in La Liga, widely recognised as the strongest domestic competition in Europe, and few dispute their talent. Yet backing them has become the equivalent of tipping Chelsea to win the Premiership: everyone knows they are capable but invariably they let you down. Tomorrow Jose Camacho's team intend to show they have finally discovered the right formula. The fact that their opponents are "only" Slovenia adds to the importance. Four years ago Spain's much fancied side began by losing to Nigeria and went out in the first phase. At Euro 2000 an opening defeat by Norway set the tone for a run which ended in the second round against France. Not since 1950 have they started with a win at the World Cup, failing at various times to beat South Korea, Austria and Honduras. "The first game will be key; it's almost like a final for us," said the Barcelona midfielder Luis Enrique, part of the 1994 and 1998 sides. "We haven't won our first game in 50 years. That's a negative run and now would be a perfect time to break it. We need to do something in that first game to generate enthusiasm." Camacho has confessed that Spanish players "have mentality problems if their first rival isn't a big name". With South Africa and Paraguay to come after Slovenia, this is a group Spain ought to cruise through. But everyone said that when they were drawn with Nigeria, Paraguay and Bulgaria in 1998. The players are realistic. "Comparing the situation four years ago, when people thought we could win, to the situation now, it's less egoistic," Enrique said. "Nobody here believes we can't win the World Cup but we're also aware we could exit in the first round." Spain's frailties appear to lie in the head. Against inferiors they have often been tripped by over-confidence or the burden of expectation. When faced with supposed superiors they can lack self-belief or mental strength. Italy knocked them out of USA 94 by scoring two minutes from time; at Euro 2000 Raul missed a late penalty that would have earned extra-time against France. Perhaps the relative lack of pressure this time will help. Not that pressure is a novelty to their players. Raul has thrived since bursting into the Real Madrid team as a teenager and last month collected his third Champions League winners' medal, scoring in the final. Emerson ruled out of World Cup. Brazil's World Cup hopes suffered a significant blow today when captain Emerson was ruled out of the competition with a dislocated shouulder in a bizarre training incident. The AS Roma player, entrusted by coach Luis Felipe Scolari to do the defensive work in midfield, was playing in goal in a light-hearted practice match when he fell awkwardly on his shoulder, team doctor Jose Luis Runco told reporters. Brazil, who will have to reshuffle their team when they open their campaign against Turkey on Monday, had previously been free of the injury problems which had afflicted most of their rivals. Neither Vampeta nor Gilberto Silva, Emerson's most likely replacements, have played in the World Cup before. Emerson, who was called up as a last minute replacement himself for the 1998 World Cup after an injury to striker Romario, was surrounded by his team mates and lay on the pitch for about 10 minutes before being taken off. He learned in the dressing room that his World Cup dream was over. "During the training Emerson suffered a dislocation of his right shoulder and unfortunately he needs around four weeks to be able to practise," said Runco. "Therefore his participation in the World Cup has been made impossible and we are asking Fifa if we can bring in another athlete. "He was extremely upset but understood the situation." It is not clear how Scolari will overcome the loss of his captain. He could decide that Vampeta and Gilberto Silva are too inexperienced to fulfil Emerson's role alone and opt instead to field both and sacrifice one of the attacking midfielders, most likely Juninho. Although Scolari is a great admirer of Emerson his presence in the team has been controversial. Critics say he commits too many fouls and lacks technique. Emerson, who has played 42 full internationals, made a strong impression the moment he put on a Brazil shirt against Ecuador in a friendly in 1997. The 26-year-old was twice thrown into the fray as a substitute during the 1998 World Cup and performed with great personality. He also held his nerve as he took one of the penalties and scored in the shootout at the end of the semi-final against the Netherlands. Emerson began his career under Scolari at Gremio, a club based in the southern city of Porto Alegre. He overcame a serious knee injury early in his career and went on to win the Brazilian championship in 1996 and Copa Brasil one year later. He then moved to Germany with Bayer Leverkusen, spending three seasons there before joining AS Roma where he won the Italian championship last year. Seven other upsets that rocked the World Cup. USA 1 England 0 - Brazil 1950 The most humiliating defeat in England's history. The tournament favourites dominated the start but in the 37th minute the Americans, who had warmed up for the tournament by losing 5-0 to the Turkish club side Besiktas, stunned them by taking the lead through Joe Gaetjens and England could not recover. North Korea 1 Italy 0 - England 1966 Possibly the most surprising World Cup scoreline ever. Italy were expected to thump the "Asian minnows" but the Koreans' defence held firm while the now legendary Park Doo Ik was on hand to score a scarcely believable winner just before half-time. The Italians were showered with tomatoes when they returned home. Algeria 2 West Germany 1 - Spain 1982 Lakhdar Belloumi's 69th-minute goal embarrassed the German coach Jupp Derwall, who had confidently predicted victory. "We're so strong, we'll win without problems," he said. Not so, although his side went on to reach the final. Cameroon 1 Argentina 0 - Italy 1990 Cameroon, reduced to nine men after a double sending off, inflicted on Argentina exactly the fate the French suffered yesterday in the tournament's opening game. In only Cameroon's second appearance in the finals, François Omam Biyick headed home an improbable winner. E Germany 1 W Germany 0 - West Germany 1974 The only official international ever played between the two Germanys was supposed to end in simple victory for the West German hosts against their amateur opponents, but Jürgen Sparwasser's goal in the 77th minute changed that. Costa Rica 1 Scotland 0 - Italy 1990 Not the only embarrassing result in Scotland's history, but perhaps the most humiliating. Juan Cayasso condemned the Scots to one of their darkest days with the only goal, from Claudio Jara's back-heel. It was the first time a Central American team had won a World Cup match in Europe. Republic of Ireland 1 Italy 0 - USA 1994 Still - going into this tournament- the Republic's only World Cup win in normal time, secured through Ray Houghton's dipping 25-yard volley from Franco Baresi's weak header. Thereafter Italy could not break through Ireland's centre-back pairing of Phil Babb and Paul McGrath. USA 1 England 0 - Brazil 1950

World Cup in brief. ·Argentina's plans not to use the No10 shirt in honour of their former World Cup-winning captain Diego Maradona have been scuppered by Fifa. The decision, taken by Fifa's executive committee, contradicts the recommendation of the body's World Cup organising committee, which had said it had no problem with Argentina's proposal. "Whereas yesterday the organising committee was of the opinion that there should be an exception, today the executive committee was not of this opinion," Fifa's president Sepp Blatter said, adding that the Argentinians had been told of the decision and accepted it. The Argentinian No10 shirt will probably be taken by third-choice goalkeeper Roberto Bonano, who had been expected to take No24. "It's a funny situation because Argentina's No10 will be able to use his hands this time," joked Blatter, in reference to Maradona's notorious goal against England at the 1986 World Cup. ·Turkey have accused next Monday's opponents Brazil of spying. The Turkish coach Senol Gunes brought training in Ulsan to a premature end when he was told about an intruder after the squad had finished their exercises and just begun a practice match featuring several tactical defensive formations. ·Thousands of striking workers rallied in Seoul yesterday to demand wage increases and improved working conditions. The South Korean government, meanwhile, concerned about its international image, has warned of stern measures against those organising illegal strikes in the run-up to and during the World Cup. ·Nigeria's notoriously unreliable electricity company has appealed to the nation's football-mad public not to embark on "an orgy of destruction" if power failures interrupt televised games. The National Electric and Power Authority, known by cynical Nigerian customers by an acronym Never Enough Power Anywhere, placed full-page advertisements in Sunday newspapers pleading: "Please don't take the law into your own hands. We plan to deliver." ·As England lick their wounds after two tough draws against South Korea and Cameroon, Germany had a less-than-strenuous work-out against a Japanese Under-18 side in Miyazaki. Not surprisingly, the Germans won 10-0, so no wonder their coach Rudi Völler was in bullish mood. "I am totally optimistic," he said after the one-sided game at the German training camp in the southern Japanese seaside resort. "I am convinced we will have a good World Cup." In brief: All those interesting, topical and frequently amusing pices that don't fit anywhere else.

World Cup in brief. · When England's daily press conference features an imitation black cat, an umbrella, Sol Campbell's chess club and Argentina's answer to Dennis Pennis, the spoof TV interviewer, it is clear the build-up to the World Cup has been going on too long. Diego Della Salla, allegedly a journalist for "Channel 13", bounced into the press centre on Awaji Island and offered Campbell the chance to touch the black cat he had brought with him to bring England bad luck. Superstitious Football Association officials intervened before Della Salla turned to his second prop, the umbrella. "It's unlucky to open this inside in my country," he said. Campbell's bemusement was all the more striking as the Arsenal centre-half had just surprised many by revealing he had set up a mini-chess league at the training camp. "Chess was my idea," he explained. I just got into it, it's a good way to keep your mind occupied." · With echoes of the infamous accusations against Bobby Moore before Mexico 1970, Senegal's midfielder Khalilou Fadiga was yesterday placed under investigation in South Korea on suspicion of stealing a gold necklace. Police in Taegu said Fadiga, 27, was alleged to have stolen the 18-carat trinket worth 300,000 won (£168) on Sunday during a visit to a jewellery store in the city with four team-mates and a coach. "The theft investigation on that Senegal player is almost over now. We are not planning to arrest him since the amount is small and he is foreigner," a detective in Taegu said. May 30: So where does Sol Campbell hone his tactics for the England defence? On the chess board, of course.

Technical area Hernan Dario Gomez favours 4-4-2, aims to dominate possession and has enough players with the speed and skill to make it count. The veteran midfielder Alex Aguinaga supplies the passes, usually to Ivan Kaviedes, who plays for Ecuador's version of Barcelona, and Southampton's Agustin Delgado. Ecuador can battle, too: they twice came from behind, with 10 men, in the qualifiers. Rabble rouser The fans. Ecuador's qualification ignited wild celebrations that ended with four dead and more than 70 injured. Celebrity supporter Meg Ryan "grew close" to Russell Crowe on location in Ecuador (so maybe not, then). Mrs John Wayne Bobbitt (Lorena) and Cristina Aguilera are Ecuadorian. Wonder of the world Agustin Delgado, fitness permitting. Nine goals in the qualifiers and Southampton paid £3.5m for "El Tin" and his dodgy knee. That has to tell you something. Oscar nominee Aguinaga is getting on a bit (and thankfully the ponytail has gone these days), so what else is he meant to do when he can't keep up? Song for the summer Sade's It's Never As Good As The First Time. "Such a fine time as this/ What could equal the bliss/The thrill of the first kiss/It'll blow right to you." David Lacey's verdict Qualifying ahead of Brazil means Ecuador will be expecting a modicum of success in their first World Cup. The group may be dominated by Italy but Ecuador have sufficient attacking flair to overcome Mexico and perhaps surprise Croatia. Defensively, however, they are suspect. The coach: Hernan Dario Gomez Words of wisdom "I ask my players to exhibit spontaneity but always within a tactical order. I am not in favour of winning any old how." Story so far Has experience with World Cup underdogs. Took his native Colombia to France 98 but went out in the first round. Was appointed manager of Ecuador in 1999 and in three years has earned the adulation of a nation. Popularity rating: 9/10 When "El Bolillo" (truncheon) returned to Colombia last year, after being shot in the leg for failing to select the son of former President Bucaram

World Cup diary. · A technical blip caused panic among the English media at yesterday's game between Argentina and Nigeria. With the match over, hacks were (of course) hard at work crafting finely honed prose, but some felt obliged to keep one eye on the England game, purely for research purposes you understand. Sadly, the internal TV system decided to show a repeat of Paraguay v South Africa. After some 30 minutes a member of staff was forced to re-tune every one of the media centre's 25 televisions by hand. · The great thing about having the World Cup in Asia is the opportunity it presents for others from around the world to immerse themselves in a foreign culture. Take, for example, the BBC's Chris Waddle and Jonathan Pearce. The terrible twosome strolled into the canteen at Ibaraki's Kashima Stadium yesterday, surveyed the local delicacies on offer and, simultaneously, grabbed a cheese and ham sandwich. The sweet taste of integration. · It seems there was a riot of sorts at the opening match in Seoul on Friday. Before the ceremony a group of about 30 South Korean wheelchair users attempted to storm the stadium to protest against the lack of access for people with disabilities. Though they didn't make it into the ground local riot police, trained in taekwondo in readiness for a hooligan invasion, appeared somewhat taken aback by this unexpected challenge. · Japan will surely have no excuses should they fail to beat Belgium in their opening game tomorrow after a fishing cooperative near the team's training camp in Shizuoka generously donated 100 eels to help the team's efforts. Hidetoshi Nakata's new slippery supporters are considered a source of strength and stamina and will shortly be eaten. · People may say that football is a funny old game but try saying that to the punter in Fulham who yesterday bet £11,000 on Paraguay to beat South Africa when the South American team were already winning 2-0. At odds of 1-100 the man stood to win just £110 but the Bafana Bafana's late comeback meant that he lost everything. · It isn't just the British who have failed to develop a taste for sushi. The Italian team have been banned from so much as dipping a metaphorical toenail into the giant swimming pool that is oriental cuisine. "Since coming to Japan," complained their striker Vincenzo Montella, "I've only eaten spaghetti." · Security has understandably become a priority for many teams, but Portugal seem to have taken matters a bit far. Their training base is the Korean Military Academy on the edge of Seoul, where visitors are forced to dodge the unconventional footballing obstacle of camouflaged soldiers toting machine guns through piles of sandbags. · A technical blip caused panic among the English media at yesterday's game between Argentina and Nigeria. World Cup in brief. · Chelsea's Jesper Gronkjaer and Bolton's Stig Tofting were involved in a training-ground bust-up yesterday after a bout of horseplay - which also involved Everton's Thomas Gravesen - turned sour. The scuffle started after Tofting and Gravesen jumped on Gronkjaer while the winger was stretching, spraying him with water and putting ice cubes down his shorts. It turned nasty after Gronkjaer hurt an eye, was seen by the doctor and then went to confront Tofting. Words were exchanged before the pair wrestled each other to the ground, with Tofting's hand around his team-mate's throat. · South Korea will deploy surface-to-air missiles near stadiums to thwart terrorist attacks from the air. Fighter jets will also be used, according to government defence officials, and trucks equipped to detect biochemicals will be in place. · Spain's Fernando Morientes hobbled out of training yesterday after twisting his right ankle.
