Poland. Track record This may be Poland's first appearance since 1986 but they had previously graced this stage with some class. They beat Brazil to finish third in 1974, finished ahead of the defending champions West Germany four years later and came third again in 1982. How they qualified Lost only one match in a weak group, beating Ukraine to first place. United or Unibond? A bit of a Middlesbrough. Shone in the mid-70s with a flying baldie (for David Armstrong read Grzegorz Lato), play in red and white, renowned for industrial pollution, cautiously on their way back. Perhaps. Competing interests Not great but they did win best team at the second World Air Games' glider aerobatics in Palma del Rio last year. Iwona Guzowska became kick-boxing world champion after defeating France's Valerie Rangeard three years ago, though she's apparently gone off the boil since. Penalty pedigree Yet to be tested. Technical area Rather depressingly as far as the entertainment is concerned, their strongest suit appears to be in goal. "Keeping possession has never been our strength," said the coach Jerzy Engel. "We did well from set-pieces, though." Rabble rouser They may be disappointingly dead-pan but Jacek Bak has something of a temper. His foul and subsequent fisticuffs earned him a red card against Armenia during the qualifiers. After the game both coaches insisted the opposing team had gone out deliberately to "play violent". Now now, girls. Celebrity supporter The Pope, though Dudek is probably a better bet between the posts these days than His Holiness. Wonder of the world Emanuel Olisadebe, originally a Nigerian who now plays in Greece having inspired Polonia Warsaw to a league and cup double, scored seven times in his first eight internationals. Oscar nominee All of them apparently. After seeing the Poles lose 2-1 to non-qualifiers Romania recently, the legendary Polish goalkeeper Jan Tomaszewski claimed the side played badly on purpose because the game was being observed by "spies" from South Korea, Portugal and the US. Song for the summer With a couple of Jerzys in attendance, a rework of House of Pain's Jump Around is probably in order. Failing that, anything by The Cardigans. David Lacey's verdict The prospect of meeting Italy in the next round as runners-up will surely intensify Polish determination to win this group ahead of Portugal. Under Engel the Poles have stirred memories of their outstanding team of the 70s. A workmanlike team all the same. The coach: Jerzy Engel Words of wisdom "If we win the World Cup I'll be happy to shave off all my hair, and I think all the other Polish fans will as well." Story so far Something of a nonentity as a player, the 49-year-old made his name coaching clubs in Poland and Cyprus before taking over as national coach in November 1999 and proving an instant success. Popularity rating: 5/10 Surprisingly up against it. Embarrassing friendly defeats have left the press fuming and the omission of the popular Artur Wi

World Cup bonanza for economy. World Cup fever will give Britain's economy a £500 million boost with fans emptying their wallets as they cheer their heroes in the Far East. Pubs, bookmakers, supermarkets, sportswear manufacturers and providers of corporate hospitality are the biggest beneficiaries of a spending boom sparked by England's and Ireland's presence in the finals. Over £100m of alcohol will be drunk, up to £200m wagered on matches and millions more spent on replica shirts, flags and other memorabilia. The 20,000 pubs opening earlier than usual today for England's first game against Sweden will be rewarded with an extra £20m to £30m in drink and food sales, as up to four million people crowd in to see if Sven-Goran Eriksson's England team can defeat his home country. Although sales of alcoholic drinks are expected to start off slowly because of the 10.30am kickoff, with most initially preferring tea and fruit juice to beer, British pubs' usual daily sale of 18m pints of beer is set to rise to 21m, also fuelled by jubilee festivities. Pub tills will be ringing even more loudly this Friday lunchtime during England's showdown with old rivals Argentina. Some 35,000 bars will show the match on large screens and televisions, and most will be packed for the 12.30 kickoff. Up to 6m fans are expected to consume around 25m pints in pubs during and after the game, increasing average daily pub earnings from £54m to £75m to £85m. Publicans, aware that many fans have taken the afternoon off work, hope most will stay around to either celebrate or commiserate after England's attempt to gain revenge for losing to Argentina in the last World Cup, boosting profits further. 'All the extra spending the World Cup is already generating will help offset the effects of the absenteeism and inefficiency at work which will accompany England's three games,' said Richard Greenwood of the Centre for Economics and Business Research, an economics think-tank. The Centre believes lost output caused by workers watching matches, officially or unofficially, will cost Britain about £1.3 billion. 'However, there will be around £500m extra spending and consumption, which will help reduce the loss,' said Greenwood. 'Volumes of trading in the City will be significantly lower while the England games are on, but many of those traders who would normally be doing deals will be placing bets on the outcome using their computer. That sort of increased spending will help to counterbalance the loss of productivity.' Bookies believe millions of people in the UK will stake around £200m on events in Japan and Korea - far more than at any previous World Cup. The big increase is due to the easy availability of internet betting, which is increasingly popular, especially among women. England's new red 'away' shirt has become the most popular piece of World Cup merchandise. Some shops have run out of supplies of the £35 top which, uniquely, is reversible and can be worn with either side up. Sport

[ARTICLE_SEP]

Belgium. The team Track record This is Belgium's sixth consecutive appearance in the finals, the 10th in all. Finished fourth in 1986. How they qualified Finished second to Croatia in the same group as Scotland, 1-0 play-off victories home and away against the Czech Republic securing their place. United or Unibond? Coventry City - plenty of time around the top flight without ever looking likely to become a major power. Competing interests With the president of the IOC, two top-rated women tennis players, a clutch of Olympic judo champions, some very good cyclists and the finest racing pigeons on earth, Belgium has some claim to being the world's sporting capital. Penalty pedigree The Red Devils won their only shoot-out, 5-4 against Spain in 1986. Technical area Aware that Belgium doesn't have the players for anything too fancy, Robert Waseige favours a blood-and-thunder attacking policy with full-backs and midfielders racing forward at every opportunity to join the strikers. "Explosive football" they call it. Rabble rouser With Emile Mpenza, Belgian football's leading playboy, out injured, the finger of shame points at his Schalke 04 team-mate Marc Wilmots, known in Germany as "Das Kampfschwein" - the Fighting Pig. At the age of 33 his nickname is looking increasingly accurate. Celebrity supporter The Belgian royal family will be attending some of Belgium's matches. No word on whether King Albert's illegitimate daughter, an artist who lives in London, will be there, though. Wonder of the world Wesley Sonck of Genk, a rumbustious 23-year-old striker and Woody Harrelson lookalike who gets lots of goals for his club and is mysteriously nicknamed "The Mosquito Killer". Oscar nominee The Belgians are generally too down-to-earth to go in for play-acting, though left-sided defender Nico van Kerckhoven, whose blend of arrogance and insouciance once led fans of his club side Schalke 04 to campaign for his transfer, seems most likely to give it a go. Song for the summer Self-effacing, home loving and rarely around when the real excitement begins. You'll Always Find Me in the Kitchen at Parties fits the bill nicely. David Lacey's verdict Unsung as always, Belgium ought to get past the opening phase and perhaps go further if they meet Turkey in the second round. The overlaps of Van Kerckhoven from left-back should give them important width but what the Belgians lack is a reliable goalscorer. The coach: Robert Waseige Words of wisdom "Let's be cautious. It would be premature to make predictions. It is a very varied group and meeting Japan in the first match is certainly not advantageous." Story so far The "Wizard of Rocourt" took over when Belgium were at an all-time low and when things did get better a poor Euro 2000 was quickly forgiven. Had quadruple heart bypass surgery last year. Popularity rating 4/10 The 62-year-old's stock was pretty high until recently when a poor run of form, a savage attack by Belgian coaching legend Raymond Goethals and rumours ab

Costa Rica. Track record Reached the second round in their only finals appearance in 1990, as Scotland fans may recall. How they qualified A sluggish start brought a change of coach and a dramatic turn of fortune. The campaign peaked when they became the first team to win a qualifier at Mexico's Azteca Stadium. United or Unibond? Dominant on their isthmus, overshadowed by two giant neighbours and capable of the odd cup shock, so it's Tranmere Rovers (no letters from geographical pedants, please). Competing interests Claudia Poll, Costa Rica's sports person of the year for every year since 1993, became the first Latin American to win an Olympic swimming gold, in Atlanta in 1996. Penalty pedigree No form but, unlike Glenn Hoddle's England, they've probably practised. Technical area The coach wants to play 3-5-2 with an emphasis on speed of thought and action. His team is often said to play in a Brazilian style, although that is not saying a lot these days. Rabble rouser La Nacion newspaper says right-wing-back Harold Wallace "likes salsa and reggae and enjoys sharing a joke with his team-mates". We think we can spot a euphemism when we see one. Celebrity supporter The furthest-flung fan at this World Cup will undoubtedly be Dr Franklin Chang-Diaz. The Costa Rican-born astronaut will be cheering on the Ticos from the International Space Station. Wonder of the world Paulo Cesar Wanchope. The gangling Manchester City striker may have a chequered reputation over here but last year's Copa America established him as one of Latin America's top goalscorers. Argentina's World Cup winning manager Carlos Bilardo said: "There are very few forwards like him." We think it was a compliment. Oscar nominee The flamboyant William Sunsing, with his tattoos of the crown of thorns and the nails in Jesus's hands and feet, has been booked for play-acting while with New England Revolution and got a dishonourable mention in a vote for the Major League's diver of the year. Song for the summer Do You Know the Way [Back] to San Jose? David Lacey's verdict Costa Rica have enough players of quality to reach the second round, as they did in 1990. Their strength lies in the attacking partnership of Rolando Fonseca and Paulo Wanchope, although the passing of Wilmer Lopez will be equally crucial. A weakness could be the uncertainty of their goalkeeper, Erick Lonnis, with high balls. The coach: Alexandre Guimaraes Words of wisdom "The World Cup is a party for us, a country that has such difficulty reaching the finals. We have to live it, enjoy it and take advantage of it." Story so far A Costa Rican by adoption, having arrived from his native Brazil at the age of 11, "Gumia" played in Italia 90. In Costa Rica the 41-year-old managed Saprissa to the league title three times, after spells at Belen and Herediano. Also coached in Guatemala. Was assistant to Costa Rica's Brazilian coach Gilson Nunes and took over after he quit just before a crucial qualifying play-off against Guatemala.

McCarthy tells Irish to pay Germany Klose attention. When it comes to the Germans, Mick McCarthy's policy is simple: don't mention the score. The Republic of Ireland's manager watched the video of Germany's 8-0 defeat of Saudi Arabia for the first time yesterday but refused to be consumed by dread. "Were you astonished by Germany's performance?" a journalist from the newspaper Bild asked him. "No, I was astonished by Saudi Arabia's performance," he replied. McCarthy is acutely aware that so much emphasis is being placed on Germany's weekend goal-fest there is a danger of his players being overcome by an inferiority complex before tomorrow's match in Ibaraki. He has already said that playing Germany is "not just playing 11 men, it is playing history," and he was at pains here last night to play down a result that will, inevitably, have sent a shudder throughout his squad. "I don't think there were too many tackles from Saudi Arabia, were there?" he asked. "And I can't remember the last time I saw five or six goals come from simple crosses." McCarthy will not tolerate such generosity from his own defenders, to the extent that he appeared to be advocating they resort to whatever means necessary. "We will be talking about their set-pieces and their aerial power. In circumstances like this, you look for people to take responsibility. "If someone is bigger than you and you're not going to beat him in a fair jump, then stop him jumping. It happens all the time. I will be telling my players to use their physical presence, don't let the guy head it and, if he does, make sure he has got a face full of dandruff." That strategy will be particularly useful when the Irish defence attempt to come to terms with Germany's hat-trick hero Miroslav Klose, who scored with three headers against the Saudis, taking his remarkable international tally to 11 goals in 13 matches. But McCarthy was undeterred yesterday when he said that Ireland will be going for the win and not merely attempting to contain Germany. His players will remain in Chiba, on the outskirts of Tokyo, where he will inform them of the team tonight or, as he put it, tell those he is leaving out that "they can have chips with their omelette in their pre-match meal". McCarthy is confident his goalkeeper Shay Given, who has not trained since Saturday after picking up a hip injury, will be fit, as will the defenders Gary Breen and Steve Finnan, who have had hamstring and groin problems respectively. Indeed, with all his players available, the likelihood is that he will field the same side who started against Cameroon on Saturday, with Steve Staunton becoming the first Ireland player to win 100 international caps. The exuberant performance, however, of Millwall's Steven Reid during his appearance as a substitute against Cameroon, and the fact that Jason McAteer was replaced after a quiet first half, citing a recurrence of his knee problem, has left McCarthy giving serious consideration to changing the right o

[ARTICLE_SEP]

Republic of Ireland 1 - 1 Cameroon. Mick McCarthy emerged from his lowest trough in Niigata with credit and with fighting words. He had heard about the wreath delivered to the FAI's headquarters in Dublin and about the petitions, hate mail, talk of mass protests, and Mr Angry of Dublin, who rang a radio phone-in last week to reveal that he had missed only three home internationals since 1973 but was switching his allegiance to Cameroon. At a time when Ireland's newspapers devoted almost as much to the events of May 23 in Saipan as they had September 11 in New York, a weight of foreboding had descended. "A team is greater than its individual parts," McCarthy had insisted, yet when it came to Roy Keane few believed him. Only as the swaths of green, white and orange headed contentedly away, Keane's grubby departure from international football having been expunged by the players left behind, were McCarthy's words vindicated. Any lingering doubt made way for optimism before Wednesday's game with Germany in Ibaraki. "There's an aura about playing Germany in the World Cup, like a Premiership side visiting Anfield or Old Trafford," McCarthy said last night. "Because of their reputation we will not just be playing 11 men but history as well. But we're not scared. Why should we be? I certainly don't think they are any better than Cameroon." In those goosebump moments when Matt Holland grass-cuttered in the goal of his life, a goal that maintained their hopes of qualifying for the second phase, all the spite and the acrimony that had enveloped Ireland since the Keane affair briefly gave way. Suddenly, as Holland took off, running one way then back on himself and over to the bulk of the Irish fans, seeking to share the moment with his wife Paula and their young sons Jacob and Sam, there was the reminder of what the World Cup is all about. As Ireland's team bus inched out of Tokyo's Haneda airport that night, heading for the new training base of Chiba, not even the television pictures of Germany v Saudi Arabia could dampen their spirits. "Our performance highlighted the togetherness that has come into the squad over the last two weeks," said McCarthy. Thoughts also turned back to the press conference in Izumo exactly a week earlier when the FAI's general secretary Brendan Menton, bleary-eyed and still trying to digest the ramifications of Keane's expulsion, had spoken of his hope that "new heroes would emerge". Initially it was as if the gruelling repercussions had demoralised McCarthy's players, and there was an air of inevitability when Steve Staunton was deceived by Samuel Eto'o's trickery for Patrick Mboma to open the scoring towards the end of a first half in which Ireland had been vapid beyond belief. However, Menton's heroes emerged after the interval. Holland played with such authority that McCarthy paid him the compliment that "whenever he is around and Roy Keane is absent, we have never really missed him". World Cup diary. · The stands may be half-full but no one could possibly criticise the state of the pitches in either Japan or Korea. Wherever players have run out they have been greeted by luscious carpets of turf almost supernaturally green. It turns out, however, that the grass has been genetically modified to appear greener than it should thanks to the addition of a hi-tech combination of chemicals known to scientists as paint. David Beckham was so unhappy on Sunday that his gold boots were slowly turning green that he demanded to be substituted. Apparently. · It is always nice for a nation to discover that they are being led by a man with a poor grip on rationality and a bizarre series of superstitions, so the people of Sweden are presumably delighted to discover that their prime minister Goeren Persson spent Sunday morning cowering in the corner of a deserted room while his country played England, receiving occasional text message updates from fellow politician Maud Olofsson. "I have a nasty tendency to watch games that Sweden loses," the premier explained, "so I'm being a little careful in the initial stages." · What is the best way to avoid detection by a "hoorigan"? One Japanese TV wag appeared to have found out on Sunday night. Followed at a distance by a camera in Saitama he - get this - wore an England and a Sweden shirt at the same time, so he could declare his allegiances to whichever burly foreigner walked up to him first. · Denmark's 2-1 win against Uruguay on Saturday was a magnificent result which gave the Scandinavians a great chance of qualifying from their group, and the players certainly celebrated in style and with wild abandon: they went fishing. · German fans are accusing Japanese police of being too strict on acts of harmless drunkenness after two of their number were driven away by police upon arriving by train in Ibaraki, where they play Ireland tomorrow. "They are taking us away like criminals," complained Michael Leicht. "All we were doing was lying down in front of the station." · The stands may be half-full but no one could possibly criticise the state of the pitches in either Japan or Korea. Wherever players have run out they have been greeted by luscious carpets of turf almost supernaturally green. It turns out, however, that the grass has been genetically modified to appear greener than it should thanks to the addition of a hi-tech combination of chemicals known to scientists as paint. David Beckham was so unhappy on Sunday that his gold boots were slowly turning green that he demanded to be substituted. Apparently.

Tunisia. The team Track record Shot to fame in 1978 as the first African country to win a World Cup match with a glorious 3-1 victory over Mexico. They are still looking for victory No2. How they qualified Won a relatively straightforward group with ease ahead of Ivory Coast. United or Unibond? Rushden & Diamonds. New kids on the block with plenty of money and ambition but some way from the big time. Competing interests Recently crowned African Nations handball champions for a record sixth time. Penalty pedigree Largely unimpressive - they have had shoot-outs in three of the last four African Nations Cups, losing twice. Technical area Traditionally known for their obdurate defence and frankly useless attack, the Carthage Eagles have shown few signs of bucking the trend of late. Failed to score in their first seven games of 2002 and much depends on the talented Freiburg forward Adel Sellimi. Rabble rouser Sellimi is an argumentative chap and refused to play in the African Nations Cup after falling out with the coach. Handle with care. Celebrity supporter Claudia Cardinale, one of Italy's great actresses but best known to us for starring opposite Peter Sellers in the Pink Panther series, was born in Tunisia. Her football credentials are unimpeachable: she is currently a Unesco goodwill ambassador - and so is Pele. Wonder of the world The Besiktas midfielder Zoubeir Beya is possibly the best player ever to come from Tunisia. He top-scored in qualifying with six goals, including a lob against the Democratic Republic of Congo from inside his own half which has earned him hero status. Oscar nominee The world will be waiting to see who takes over the mantle from the retired captain and record cap holder Chokri El Ouaer. In the 2000 African Champions League final, El Ouaer fled his area in howls of pain, leaving a trail of blood all the way to the halfway line. Sadly the referee had seen him cut himself. He was still voted Tunisian sportsman of the year. Song for the summer With little chance of progressing even through one of the least daunting first-round groups, it has to be Phil Collins' Against All Odds. David Lacey's verdict An awful African Nations Cup plus the sudden resignation of their French coach, Henri Michel, has not filled Tunisians with eager anticipation for the World Cup. The team will look to the creative skills of Beya to get them going but are also relying heavily on him getting the goals. The coaches Ammar Souayah and Khemais Laabidi Words of wisdom "We must improve both offensively and defensively but we will get to the second round." - Souayah Story so far The assistant coaches Souayah and Laabidi took over when Michel resigned in March following a terrible performance in the African Nations Cup, admitting he was "not a miracle worker". Popularity rating 6/10 This pair are totally unproven, and that is being treated as grounds for optimism. The Tunisian press have already hailed Souayah as a "master tactician". He will need to be.

Portugal. Track record This is only their third World Cup and their first since 1986. On both previous outings they played England - possibly a good omen, as the earliest they can meet this time is the semi. How they qualified Topped a group that, including Holland and the Republic of Ireland, appeared as hairy as the coach's upper lip. Scored 33 goals in 10 games on their way. United or Unibond? Newcastle United. Credible challengers, good to watch and influenced by Bobby Robson (during spells at Sporting and Porto). And their history isn't as glorious as you might think. Competing interests Portuguese world champions are slightly more rare than tap-dancing gibbons, but they boast the world's top two male double mini-trampoliners in Nuno Lico and Amadeu Neves. Penalty pedigree No shoot-out experience in senior competitions but half the side that won the 1991 World Youth Cup final on pens against Brazil (in front of 120,000 in Lisbon) are in the current squad. Technical area Despite the wonderful attacking talents in his squad Antonio Oliveira is instinctively cautious in his approach. Expect a 4-4-1-1, with those split strikers everyone was talking about not so long ago. Rabble rouser Lazio's flamboyantly coiffured defender Fernando Couto may have no track record of unruliness but he has been caught up in Italy's great nandrolone hunt. One to keep a close eye on. Celebrity supporter Canadian singing sensation Nelly Furtado, whose parents are Portuguese, is uniquely inspired by the music of their homeland. Wonder of the world Luis Figo has seemed destined to become one of the greats since he made his debut for Sporting Lisbon aged 16. Current world player of the year, though he was a major disappointment for Real Madrid in the Champions League final. Oscar nominee Let's just say that Mario Jardel should dedicate his European golden boot to his Sporting team-mate Joao Pinto, whose penalty-winning skills are without equal. Song for the summer Nirvana's Mr Moustache is believed to have been inspired by Portugal's hirsute coach, although the lyrics ("Yes I eat cow, I am not proud") are a little cryptic. David Lacey's verdict Worth a medium-odds bet after the way they edged out Holland in the qualifiers. Certainly the talent is there with Figo, Rui Costa and Joao Pinto still among the world's best players. Individual form has been fitful but Portugal may well win the group. The coach Antonio Oliveira Words of wisdom "Portugal are capable of beating any side, as long as they are rigorous, firm, determined and defend the game's principles at the highest level in good time. It's important not to have complexes in order to win." Story so far Oliveira's chief success came at Porto, where he won two titles. Took over the national side with a promise to "be the first instead of the best", and qualification from a tough group shows they are on their way. Popularity rating: 8/10 The presence of Porto on any manager's CV is guaranteed to make him unpopular in L

[ARTICLE_SEP]

United States of America. The team Track record The Americans were in from the very start in 1930, when they reached the semi-finals, and famously beat England 20 years later. No great history of achievement, though. How they qualified Struggled to take Concacaf's last finals spot and came dangerously close to being pipped by Honduras. United or Unibond? Huddersfield Town: full of unfulfilled potential, having won only two finals matches since 1930. Lost all three at France 98 including a galling defeat by Iran. Easy group but little hope. Competing interests World beaters at women's football, for a start. And every other global sport, such as American football. And let us not forget Chicago's Brian Cappelletto, the world scrabble champion. Penalty pedigree Got some practice in this year's Concacaf Gold Cup, cruising past Canada 4-2 in a semi-final shoot-out on their way to the title. Technical area The team plays a tight 4-4-2 with a midfielder protecting a sluggish back four, although the loss of the enforcer Chris Armas could scupper this. "We'll likely put 11 players on the field and see how that works," says Bruce Arena. Rabble rouser "There are no Gazzas on this team," a spokesman says with horror. Celebrity supporter Oscar-winning movie hero Robert Duvall, whose love of the beautiful game inspired him to make a film, A Shot at Glory, about it. Strangely, he cast Ally McCoist in a lead role. Wonder of the world Claudio Reyna, whom the former coach Steve Sampson called simply "the Man", is the midfield string-puller. Second Portugal player fails drugs test. Daniel Kenedy has been dropped from Portugal's World Cup squad after reportedly failing a drugs test. A brief statement on Wednesday by the Potuguese Football Federation said its medical team had decided to exclude the Maritimo midfielder, but gave no further details. State radio RDP said a urine sample taken from Kenedy on May 14 had tested positive for the banned diuretic furosenide. Kenedy, 28, is the second player from the Portugal squad to be dropped by coach Antonio Oliveira for failing a dope test. First-choice goalkeeper Joaquim "Quim" Silva was suspended in February after testing positive for nandrolone. Kenedy, who cried when Oliveira drafted him in last week as a late replacement to fill a gap in midfield, protested his innocence and said he had taken slimming pills. "I want to make it clear that I did nothing with intent. I took something to lose weight, as I was already on holiday and had taken a 10-day break. Those pills had a substance that showed up in the test and unfortunately that's what happened," Kenedy told RDP in Macao, where the squad is training. Diuretics can be used for slimming, but also to mask other banned substances. RDP said 19-year-old midfielder Hugo Viana, with four caps, would replace Kenedy. Viana plays for Portuguese league champions Sporting and is currently with the national squad playing in the European under-21 tournament in Switzerland. Portugal's first game in the World Cup finals will be a Group D tie against the United States on June 5. They have also been drawn against Poland and South Korea. World Cup round-up. The gloom engulfing France's defence of the World Cup deepened yesterday as the coach Roger Lemerre came to terms with the possible loss of Lilian Thuram as well Zinedine Zidane for the champions' make-or-break group game with Uruguay. The Juventus defender collided with Fabien Barthez during their final training session in Seoul ahead of last night's departure for the southern city of Busan. Though the Manchester United goalkeeper escaped with no more than a bruised foot, the 30-year-old defender damaged his shin and is rated extremely doubtful for tomorrow's match. His would be a significant loss. Employed at right-back against Senegal, he had been expected to replace the error-prone and out-of-sorts Frank Leboeuf in the centre against the South Americans. Should he be ruled out, Leboeuf would keep his place with Bayern Munich's Willy Sagnol or Roma's Vincent Candela taking his place at right-back. Zidane, crocked in training last week and badly missed in the opening game of the tournament, underwent a scan on his torn thigh muscle at a Seoul hospital yesterday but is unlikely to be fit enough to feature against Uruguay despite hopeful noises from the French camp. "The situation is evolving favourably," said the team doctor Jean-Marcel Ferret, "and the work on the ground is improving every day." Unfortunately, it is not improving quickly enough. To compound Lemerre's problems his playmaker's replacement against Senegal, Bolton's Youri Djorkaeff, is suffering from a groin injury. In his absence, Parma's Johan Micoud should become the latest lesser mortal to attempt to fill the void left by Zidane. "Compared with four years ago, or even at Euro 2000, the pressure on us is incredible," said Micoud, the 28-year-old who edged out Lyon's Eric Carrière to earn his passage to the Far East. Great World Cup shocks. United States 1 - 0 England Brazil 1950 England's most humiliating defeat, if you ignore the debaggings dished out by Hungary three years later. The joint favourites were making their first appearance at the World Cup and such were expectations (presumptions?) that a London newspaper editor famously thought the score on the wires was a misprint for 10-1. Not so - Haitian Larry Gaetjens' winner was far from the US's only chance, whilst England complained on the day of some iffy refereeing, but the Americans held out for the win on the same day as the English cricketers lost at home to the West Indies for the very first time. Bloody colonials. North Korea 1 - 0 Italy England 1966 Probably the biggest surprise in World Cup history. Italy's assistant coach, having watched Korea before the finals, had compared seeing them play to watching a physical comedy. Talk about setting yourself up for a fall. Pak Doo Ik grabbed Korea's goal just before half time, although Italy were already down to ten men after an injury to their captain, Bulgarri. There were no excuses for the Azzurri, though, and they were greeted with a torrent of tomatoes on their early return. Like the US today, Korea took a three-goal lead against Portugal in the quarter finals. This time Portugal came back. West Germany 1 - 2 Algeria Spain 1982 The shock of the 1982 finals which also saw the hosts beaten at home by Northern Ireland. Made even better by the fact that the Germans equalised only for Belloumi to immediately restore the lead claimed earlier by Madjer. Of course, Algeria's party was pooped later on by Austria and West Germany's mutually convenient final game meaning the Algerians went out on goal difference. Algeria cried foul - and who can blame them? Cameroon 1 - 0 Argentina Italy 1990 Even in light of what happened next (Cameroon also beat Romania and Colombia before scaring the willies out of England in the last eight), this was massive. Never mind the comedy antics of Zaire in 1974, this marked the arrival of sub-Saharan Africa on the football map - with a bang. The goal, Omam Biyik's header wriggling through Nery Pumpido's grasp, was not as memorable as the red cards thoroughly earned by Kana Biyik and Benjamin Massing, both for assaults on Claudio Caniggia. It was Argentina, though, so we loved them for it. Costa Rica 1 - 0 Scotland Italy 1990 As if Iran in Argentina 1978 wasn't bad enough, the Scots went one worse against Costa Rica in Genoa. Cayasso's strike just after half-time made Costa Rica the first Central American side to win a World Cup game in Europe. For Scotland, things didn't get much better, to the extent that their 3-0 defeat against Morocco doesn't even make our list. Saudi Arabia 1 - 0 Belgium USA 1994 A remarkable result as the Saudis became the first Asian side to qualify for the second round. Germany 8 - 0 Saudi Arabia. These days it is often remarked that there are no easy games in international football. As if ignorant of France's demonstration of this the night before, Rudi Voller sent his German team out to face Saudi Arabia with the remit of not only winning, but doing so "emphatically". That they did exactly that under the Sapporo Dome's roof not only silenced Germany's critics as Voller had intended. It also put Senegal's achievement against the reigning world champions into even finer focus, as if it were needed. With their towering forward line, it was clear from the very beginning that Germany's strength in the air was perfectly designed to exploit Saudi Arabia's main frailty - although it became clear they possess plenty of those. By the end, the strikers' lethal execution of their manager's directions may give cause for the world to sit up and take notice of the Germans again, even despite the paucity of the opposition. On a day when Uruguay's Dario Rodriguez volleyed an early contender for goal of the tournament, the Germans went for quantity over quality. Jancker, the initial focus of the German attack, had had a goal disallowed early on before his Polish-born strike partner took centre-stage, scoring two identical headed goals from left-wing crosses within five minutes of each other. Ballack, provider of the second, headed another carbon copy before Jancker got in on the act himself with a deftly taken shot under Al Deaya right before half-time. Not even before the Germans took the lead - as late as the 20th minute - were there any signs of the confident and ebullient football they had played in recent friendly wins over fellow finalists Uruguay and Senegal. Indeed, the ineptitude of their defending raised concerns as to whether their own record defeat may be exceded in their remaining games against Cameroon and Ireland, both of which know a bit about playing it in the air. There was an intial let up in the second half, but the Saudis looked as if they were already on the plane home. Germany started scoring again with 20 minutes left, Klose finishing his hat-trick, heading home a cross, this time from Bernd Schneider on the right, just for variety. A sixth followed immediately, Thomas Linke converting the lively Christian Ziege's corner. Klose then left the fray, replaced by Oliver Bierhoff, and within ten minutes the old master notched the goal he wanted in slightly bizarre fashion, sliding in a low toe-poke from all of 35 yards after Schneider's pass. And the Bayer Leverkusen midfielder added symmetry to the scoreline in stoppage time, curling in a freekick to cap an inventive if rather unharassed personal performance. combined_pre_match_text: Sweden's Andersson shapes up for Nigeria. Sweden's captain Patrik Andersson, who missed Sunday's draw with England due to a groin strain, may be fit to face Nigeria on Friday. The team doctor Magnus Forssblad said yesterday: "He cycled today and it was OK and tomorrow we'll let him run. On Wednesday or Thursday he should be training." Fifa has asked Nigeria to highlight the numbers and names on their shirts as they were difficult to pick out in Sunday's defeat by Argentina. France's coach Roger Lemerre may switch from 4-2-3-1 to the 4-3-3 of 1998 when they face Uruguay on Thursday as he fears that their strategy is too easily read in the absence of the injured Robert Pires and Zinedine Zidane. "It's a set-up I have used from time to time," said Lemerre, whose team have not won any of their three matches this year without Zidane. If the captain is again unfit, moreover - though his thigh injury is "evolving favourably - Johan Micoud could replace Youri Djorkaeff. Spain's Diego Tristan missed training yesterday after taking a knock on his right ankle against Slovenia. "It's a little inflamed and it's painful," said their doctor Genaro Borras. South Africa, whose first goal against Paraguay has been credited to Tebeho Mokoena rather than an own goal, have allowed their midfielder Bennett Mnguni to go home after the death of his brother. "We will leave his place open should he wish to return," said a spokesman. Belgium's defenders Nico van Kerckhoven and Glen de Boeck have leg injuries and will miss today's game with Japan. · England's draw with Sweden had an ITV audience of 14m, a British record for a Sunday-morning programme. June 4: Sweden's captain Patrik Andersson, who missed Sunday's draw with England due to a groin strain, may be fit to face Nigeria on Friday.

Spain make winning start. Spain made a winning start to a World Cup campaign for the first time in 52 years as they beat Slovenia 3-1 in their opening group B match. Raul scored the opening goal on the stroke of halftime, selling his marker with a brilliant dummy just inside the box before stabbing the ball past goalkeeper Marko Simeunovic for his 26th international goal. Juan Carlos Valeron added a second in the 74th minute, stealing in at the back post to slide a diagonal shot past the wrong-footed keeper. Substitute Sebastjan Cimirotic scored a consolation for Slovenia on 82 minutes before defender Fernando Hierro secured the win from the penalty spot five minutes later after Fernando Morientes was brought down by substitute Sasa Gajser. The match was by no means a classic - Spain seemed to labour under the weight off their dismal World Cup history while the Slovenians, appearing in the competition for the first time, rarely pushed men forward in large numbers. But the Spaniards will not care about that. They have finally broken a jinx dating back to 1950, when they beat Chile in their opening match of the tournament in Brazil. Since then they have failed to win eight World Cup openers, and have been held by the likes of Honduras and South Korea. In France four years ago they lost to Nigeria in their opening first round tie. In the early stages of the match, Slovenia looked capable of extending the Spaniards' unwanted record. Midfielder Miran Pavlin forced a point-blank save from Iker Casillas as early as the third minute and Zlatko Zahovic forced another save with a stinging left foot shot. Spain's best efforts came from a pair of long range Javi de Pedro free kicks that dipped just over the Slovenian crossbar. The Spaniards grew in confidence in the second half and Morientes came close to scoring a minute after coming on as substitute. Spain's win leaves them top of group B ahead of South Africa and Paraguay, who drew 2-2 in the earlier match, and Slovenia. Spain made a winning start to a World Cup campaign for the first time in 52 years as they beat Slovenia 3-1 in their opening group B match.

World Cup diary. · The good news just keeps on coming for the Koreans. After victory over Poland in their opening game, the team awoke yesterday to find the country's leading fortune-telling website, www.leemancy.com, saying that they are on the march to the quarter-finals. Using the Asian science of divination, the site predicts they will end honourably with a quarter-final defeat to Spain, with the victors going on to defeat Argentina in the final. For what it's worth, the site did not expect England to make it beyond the group stage. · While South Korea erupted at the victory against Poland, the local authorities, ever conscious of their image, had more pressing priorities. Rather than flash "Congratulations" across the two big screens in the Asiad stadium the ground staff opted instead for a less hearty salute: "Please pick up your rubbish from around your seat. Keep Korea tidy." ·The Shukan Post tuts at the sex ban imposed by Japan's manager Philippe Troussier on his players and even dubs him a killjoy for denying them the opportunity to watch adult channels in their hotel rooms. Naturally, the magazine's stance has nothing to do with the fact it boosts sales with "hair-nude" pictures of naked women. ·Korean television viewers must be wondering what all the fuss is about following Rivaldo's theatrics against Turkey. Highlights programmes left the incident on the cutting-room floor, so as far as the locals are aware, Rivaldo wins a (dubious) corner which he proceeds to take before the referee blows the final whistle. Clearly, the image of the game always takes priority. ·Even if they're not getting the full picture, Korean viewers can at least see some matches in 3D on giant screens. Armchair fans don special glasses to enhance their football vision. Perhaps Vladimir Fernandez, the referee for Brazil v Turkey, could be donated a pair. June 6: All the action from off the pitch.

England's past four World Cup clashes with Argentina. ENGLAND 3 ARGENTINA 1 Chile June 2, 1962, Group 4, Rancagua England led 2-0 by half-time through a Ron Flowers penalty (17min) and a Bobby Charlton shot (42). Jimmy Greaves struck (65) before Jose Francisco Sanfilippo's reply (78). England pipped Argentina to qualify - on goal difference. ENGLAND 1 ARGENTINA 0 England July 23, 1966,quarter-final, Wembley Antonio Rattin carved his name in disgrace, the Argentinian captain being sent off by German referee Rudolf Kreitlein after 36 minutes. Rattin refused to go until he was virtually dragged away eight minutes later. Argentina held out until Geoff Hurst, making his finals debut, scored with a glancing header (77). ENGLAND 1 ARGENTINA 2 Mexico June 22, 1986, quarter-final, Mexico City Revenge for Argentina in a game of unrelenting tension heightened by the Falklands War. Diego Maradona's Hand of God goal came after 51 minutes; then the Argentina captain delivered arguably the greatest individual goal ever (55). Gary Lineker's header (81) was not enough against the eventual champions. ENGLAND 2 ARGENTINA 2 (Argentina won 4-3 on pens) France June 30, 1998, second round, St Etienne Another unforgettable goal, further controversy and elimination again for England -after penalties. After an early exchange of spot-kicks Michael Owen struck scintillatingly (15) before Javier Zanetti equalised (45). Then came David Beckham's fateful sending-off (47) for kicking at Diego Simeone. A seeming eternity later David Batty failed from the spot. June 6: England's past four World Cup clashes with Argentina.

World Cup diary. · Belgium's coach Robert Waseige has lashed out at journalists who criticised the team's 2-2 draw with Japan. "We have a talent to write ourselves into the ground," he said. "You'd think we love the smell of dung." By contrast, he claimed, the foreign media had been positively gushing about Belgium: "Both the BBC and France's TF1 had very positive reactions." Which was slightly odd, since Belgium's game was shown live on ITV. Maybe he meant the radio. · You read it here first. "He's made them disciplined and, at the risk of a German stereotype, efficient as well" - ITV's Guy Mowbray on Cameroon coach Winfried Schäfer, as exclusively predicted in the Diary last week. · Sapporo sex businesses 0, Saitama rice-ball sellers 1. Brothel keepers in Sapporo's red-light district complain business is down by more than half because of the heavy police presence and the distraction of football on television. On the other hand, the Saitama Stadium branch of the Family Mart store racked up record sales when Japan played Belgium, including 3,500 balls of rice wrapped in seaweed. Coming to a Premiership ground near you soon. · Cameroon's Samuel Eto'o kept the team waiting after the Saudia Arabia game, as he struggled to provide a urine sample. Despite the best efforts of journalists outside the room "making whooshing noises" to help him, it was well after the end of France-Uruguay before the doctors, not to mention his team-mates, were finally able to take the, er, mickey. · All is not well in the Croatian camp, though the players are very reluctant to go public. "The atmosphere is horrible and awful, the situation is absolutely terrible. But it's better if I keep quiet about it," confided Alen Boksic. "I don't want to talk," added Mario Stanic in a stage whisper. "Because if I did really say what is going on, chaos would follow." Mum's the word. · World Cup glory at last for Guatemala, as referee Carlos Batres plays a blinder in Denmark v Senegal. At the match, a lone Guatemalan journalist invited other members of the media to give their views on Batres' performance live on radio at half-time, by thrusting his mobile phone into their faces. Mexico 1 - 0 Croatia. A penalty from Cuauhtemoc Blanco in the 61st minute gave Mexico a 1-0 win over Croatia in the opening Group G game of the World Cup. The penalty also resulted in the first red card of the tournament for Croatia's Boris Zivkovic, who stuck his right leg out on the advancing Blanco, bringing the attacker down while making no contact with the ball. Referee Lu Jun had no hesitation in expelling Zivkovic and pointing to the penalty spot. Blanco returned to take the shot, smashing the ball into the top lefthand corner of the net as goalkeeper Stipe Pletikosa dived the wrong way. Mexican coach Javier Aguirre said he was satisfied with the game but not complacent. "We've only won one of the three games. We still have Ecuador and Italy waiting and the next is Ecuador. So from this minute we're only thinking of Ecuador," he said. Croatia's coach, Mirko Jovic, said his team would still be fighting. "It's very bad to lose in the first game, but the players have to get over the shock. The battle is over but the war isn't," he said. The first half had been fairly even, though Mexico appeared to get the better of the game as the teams approached half-time. Croatia - the surprise third-place finishers from France '98 - had a chance in the first five minutes when Mexico goalkeeper Oscar Perez was forced to save a Josip Suminic header. In the 25th, Perez had to run to the right of his goal to dive on a loose ball. As the Mexicans started to find their form, the action switched to the Croatian half. Mexico had their best chance of the half in the 33rd minute when a cross from Ramon Morales was knocked just wide of the net by Jared Borgetti. It was at terrible miss. Croatia had another chance in the first minute of the second half when a cross from substitute Milan Rapaic found the head of Zvonimir Soldo, whose strike was touched over the bar by Perez. After the goal the Mexicans hung on to their lead but gave little indication that they might score a second, while Croatia desperately made substitutions to try to change the match. Bizarrely, they withdrew lone striker Davor Suker and ended the game with no forwards on the field. Set pieces aside, their only hopes of bagging an equaliser rested in substitute Mario Stanic's occasional forays from midfield. Mexico's best chance of doubling their advantage came with five minutes to go, but Morales' shot was knocked away by Pletikosa. Croatia stepped up the pressure in injury time with a series of attacks on the Mexican goal, but were unable to penetrate Mexico's defence. The two sides had met twice before with Croatia winning each time. A penalty from Cuauhtemoc Blanco in the 61st minute gave Mexico a 1-0 win over Croatia in the opening Group G game of the World Cup.

Slovenia coach to quit in row. Slovenia's coach Srecko Katanec will quit at the end of the World Cup after a bust-up with his highest-profile player, he said at a tearful press conference yesterday. Slovenia lost their opening Group B game 3-1 to Spain and Zlatko Zahovic was substituted in the second half to his obvious disgust. In an incident that recalled Roy Keane's row with the Republic of Ireland manager Mick McCarthy, for which Keane was sent home, he screamed abuse at Katanec after the defeat. Zahovic is believed to have said: "I can buy all of you, I can buy the whole association, I can buy Smarna Gora [Katanec's home town]. I can't stay in a team like this where you [Katanec] will substitute me in a game like this in the World Cup." The Slovenian football association intervened, along with the players and especially the captain Ales Ceh, and Zahovic, still unrepentant, was persuaded to remain with the squad. In reference to Katanec's planned resignation he said: "Whoever digs the hole often falls into it themselves." Katanec, 38, broke down in tears and before walking out of the press conference said: "I'm not used to this sort of communication. I was shocked when Zahovic attacked me. "These are my last three or four games with Slovenia; it depends how far we go." Slovenia next play South Africa on Saturday. · Meanwhile, the South Korean organisers took ticketing into their own hands on Wednesday, halting international sales through Fifa and its British agent and turning them over to the organisation that handles domestic sales. "From now on we will sell tickets through Interpark, the Kowoc tickets sale agency," said Lin Byung-taik, foreign media spokesman of Kowoc. · Fifa's anti-doping chief yesterday warned players and national teams to avoid taking muscle-building food supplements. Dr Jiri Dvorak, the chief medical officer, said many over-the-counter products did not reveal on the packaging that they contained substances such as the steroid nandrolone. "We have identified a number of products containing nandrolone which is not declared on the package and all we can do is issue a warning not to use across-the-counter food supplements as they might be contaminated," he said. Players are held responsible even if they take a banned drug unwittingly. Two players from each team, drawn at random, have urine and blood tests after every match. · Rivaldo was in unrepentant mood after Fifa's disciplinary committee fined him £4,500 with £680 costs for feigning injury during Brazil's 2-1 win over Turkey. After hearing about his punishment the Barcelona striker said he had only used his "cunning" and such incidents were commonplace. Rivaldo is the first player to be punished for simulating a foul at the World Cup - he went down clutching his face after Hakan Unsal drove the ball into his leg, an offence for which the Turk was sent off - after Fifa ordered referees to crack down on players faking injury or fouls. Italy 2 - 0 Ecuador. Italy striker Christian Vieri struck twice to give his team a comfortable 2-0 win over Ecuador in their opening World Cup group G match. The Italians showed why they are among the favourites with an efficient display against the south American side making their first appearance in the tournament. Italy top the group standings, ahead on goal difference of Mexico who beat Croatia 1-0. Vieri, who scored five goals in five games at the 1998 World Cup, quickly confirmed that he will again be one of the tournaments with a superb display. "We played a good game, we managed to score two goals, we didn't suffer that much which was important," Vieri said. "The whole team played well not just me." Italy coach Giovanni Trapattoni's decision to play Francesco Totti in an advanced role alongside Vieri paid off handsomely. The pair combined to open the scoring in the seventh minute - Totti screwing the ball back from the byline into the path of the onrushing Vieri who swept the ball past keeper Jose Cevallos with a clinical left-foot finish from 15 yards. Totti, living up to his billing as Italy's key creative force, went close to a goal of his own just four minutes later, crashing a 20-yard shot just wide after a smart turn. There was little work for the Italian defence, but when Ecuador's main marksman Agustin Delgado did found the slightest opening he was swiftly surrounded by three defenders. Vieri doubled the Italian advantage in the 27th minute, outpacing Ulises De la Cruz as he chased a long ball over the top, and although Cevallos blocked the shot with his legs, the ball span goalwards and Vieri had the simple task of knocking it into an empty net. Ecuador coach Hernan Dario Gomez brought on Carlos Tenorio at the break in a bid to bolster his attack but Italy rarely give up a two-goal lead and typically took few risks in the second half. Alessandro Del Piero came on for Totti with 16 minutes remaining to claim his 50th cap, and although Delgado wasted a good chance in the final minute to claim a consolation for his side the Italians eased to a confident win. Italy had won just one of their last five opening games at World Cups. Italy striker Christian Vieri struck twice to give his team a comfortable 2-0 win over Ecuador in their opening World Cup group G match.

World Cup diary. · Arsène Wenger is in Japan working as an analyst for a local TV station, where his only fault is that he will answer questions only in English. Unhelpful, perhaps, but how ITV must be wishing it could persuade Gazza to do the same. · The English have by and large been pretty well behaved so far, but one unruly bunch got into some trouble the other night in, of all places, a Tokyo curry house. Having staggered in after a night on the tiles, they persuaded the staff to serve them and collapsed into their seats. The waiters, however, became so upset at the constant swearing they had to act; an argument ensued and the police almost got involved. And what kind of person would besmirch the good name of England in such a way? Journalists, of course. · Perhaps the news of the journalists' drunken high-jinks has got around. It certainly seemed like it in Saitama yesterday when one Guardian correspondent on his way to the Japan game asked a local tout how much he was asking for tickets. "I hate journalists," came the reply. "Come with me where the police aren't around and I'll teach you a lesson." Needless to say, our man made himself scarce. · Because of big time differences it's hugely inconvenient for anyone in Europe or South America to watch much of the football. Lucky old Australia, on the other hand, can watch the games at their pleasure. Only so far they haven't. Only one match, France v Senegal, got into the country's top 100 programmes last week - and it was No 91. · Grand shows of public nationalism come so unnaturally to the Japanese that on the back of flags handed to their fans in Saitama yesterday were the words to the national anthem and instructions to stand up and remove their hats while it was being sung. · The decision of the Korean referee Kim Young-joo to send off two of Turkey's players and award a very suspicious penalty against them threatens to destroy relations between the two nations, best buddies since the Turks sent 15,000 troops to help out during the Korean War. "I am leaving him up to the Koreans," said the head of Turkey's football association, Haluk Ulusoy. "We sacrificed 1,000 soldiers here to defend Koreans, and one Korean killed 70 million Turks last night." June 5: Round-up of all the action off the pitch.

USA 3 - 2 Portugal. The World Cup of the Underdog may be an unfortunate label for a tournament jointly held in South Korea, but the United States sprung the second major surprise of 2002 by beating Euro 2000 semi-finalists Portugal in spectacular fashion at the Suwon stadium. After losing all three matches and scoring just one goal at France 98, the completely unfancied Ameicans raced to a three-goal lead in the first 40 minutes as they were showered with gifts by a shaky Portuguese defence. Portugal pulled one goal back on the stroke of half-time, but a resolute second-half performance saw the US concede another but finish one of the most exciting games of the tournament so far as the stronger team. Portugal's hugely talented but now ageing generation of stars creaked collectively at the back and failed to ignite up front. They will now face the suggestion that their time has passed - a charge already levelled against Croatia, whose team of '98 veterans lost disappointingly to Mexico on Monday. The US victory has turned Group D upside down, with South Korea and USA topping the group and Portugal and Poland both facing a struggle to qualify for the second round. Just as in 1950, when the US beat the mighty England 1-0, today's shock result may not convert the indifferent American public to the joys of football, but with a young and physically strong team, the US team now have every chance of reaching the second phase, as they did when hosting the tournament in 1994. In an explosive start, the US became the quickest scorers in the World Cup so far when the experienced Viktor Baia could only parry a powerful header from Brian McBride after three minutes, leaving John O'Brien to rifle the ball home from two yards. Portugal continued to look casual, sloppy and unable to cope with quick US breaks down the channels. An appalling mix-up when the Portuguese attempted to play their way out of defence saw the ball bobble to the lively Landon Donovan in an offside position. No flag came and Donovan's cross was deflected in by the hapless Jorge Costa, who later missed an excellent chance to equalise. Clearly shell-shocked, Portugal were torn apart six minutes later by the best goal of match as a sweeping move and inviting right-wing cross by DaMarcus Beasley was completed by an emphatic - and completely unchallenged - header from McBride. Portugal picked the perfect time to pull a goal back when Beto Severo slotted in on the stroke of halftime. But there was no repeat of the Euro 2000 match when Portugal recovered from conceding two early goals to England to win 3-2, or of 1966, when they were losing 3-0 to North Korea but came back to win 5-3 thanks to a Eusebio hat-trick. The much-anticipated second-half onslaught never materialised, although the US were embarrassed when a Pauleta cross was spectacularly volleyed into his own net by Jeff Agoos with twenty minutes remaining. No28: Zoubeir Beya, Tunisia. Isn't this just a waste of everybody's time? It's not like Tunisia are going to make much of an impression, is it? So dismissive. Don't you know that the beauty of the World Cup lies not in the shoot-out dramas and imaginative kit designs, but in each captivating cameo role, no matter how brief? No. Well it does, and Zoubeir is guaranteed at least a walk-on part. All right, all right. Convince me. Beya is Tunisia's highest profile export, currently playing in Turkey for Besiktas after moving from Bundesliga side Freiburg last summer. And how has he done there? He's certainly popular with the fans. He's a naturally gifted playmaker who has kept his pace despite entering his twilight years (he just turned 31). During his time in Europe he has developed as a player, adding a bit of tactical nous to his repertoire, much to the benefit of the Carthage Eagles. Who? That's the Tunisian national team to you and me. Fancy. So is he The Man as far as Tunisia's concerned? Well, they're not quite a one man team, but in a midfield already robbed of injured Osama Sellami and the drug-shamed Skander Souayah, Tunisia's creative threat rests with Beya. This is nothing new, though - he top-scored in Tunisia's qualifiers with six, including the goal of the tournent, lobbing the DR Congo goalkeeper from inside his own half. Surely Tunisian fans don't expect him to take them far, though? Actually, Tunisian expectations had been on the rise in recent years after winning back some north African attention from neighbours Morocco, Algeria and Egypt. However, they were restored to somewhere near reality by a pretty poor showing at the African Nations Cup in Mali earlier this year, and they haven't really shown any signs of recovery since. Nothing to lose for our boy Zoubeir, then? Well, you could say that, but best not to his face. He has gone to greater lengths than most to play for his country, even defying threats of being transfer-listed by Besiktas this season to go to Mali. Some good that did him Yeh, but he went right back to Turkey and promptly won his place back. Bit of a man of steel, then Something like that. The man to win back some north African credibility for Tunisia.

The World Cup giantkillers. When surprises keep happening they are no longer surprises. The number of upsets witnessed in the first six days of this World Cup has approached the total of major shocks produced by all the previous tournaments rolled together. Seen individually, the wins by Senegal over France, South Korea over Poland and the United States over Portugal stand comparison with past tales of the unexpected: the Americans beating England 1-0 in 1950, North Korea knocking out Italy in 1966, Algeria's win against West Germany in 1982 and Cameroon's victory over Argentina in the opening match of 1990. Taking all together, however, there is a certain logic to the way events are unfolding in this World Cup. In each case European teams have not only been caught on an off day but have been caught by opponents whose development at international level continues to narrow the gap between themselves and the more established powers. Nobody seriously believed that France, World Cup holders, would lose to Senegal in the opening game. Yet is it really surprising that a French team based largely outside its own country should occasionally go down to a foreign side drawn almost entirely from French league clubs and coached by a Frenchman? Bruno Metsu scoured France for talented footballers, some of whom were only vaguely aware of their Senegalese qualification. Johnny Giles adopted a similar approach when he began to find Englishmen with Irish grandmothers for the Republic of Ireland. Metsu's achievement has been to imbue his squad with a strong cadre spirit and a sense of national identity in less than 18 months. Once Senegal had reached the final of the African Nations Cup, where they lost to Cameroon on penalties, they became a World Cup shock in waiting. Being able to concentrate minds and bodies on a single aim helps. French league duties do not seem to have impeded Metsu in his task of blending the team to the point of achieving a natural understanding. A less strenuous club programme helps, as Sven-Goran Eriksson will be the first to acknowledge. Playing together as often and against a wide variety of opponents as possible has helped transform South Korea from World Cup makeweights to co-hosts with a real chance of advancing to the latter stages. Guus Hiddink, the Dutch coach, has been helped by the Korean Football Association's willingness to spend heavily on training facilities as well as friendlies in Europe, the United States and other parts of Asia. Like so many teams from the previously under-developed part of the football world Korea had potential talent awaiting a coach of Hiddink's quality and experience to turn them into a winning team. Hiddink makes the point that repeated encounters with the pace and organisation of modern opposition has made his players more worldly-wise. This was very evident in the way South Korea took the game to the slightly stunned Poles. Korea's 2-0 victory in Busan carried distant echoes of North Korea's 1-0 w

Senegal 1 - 0 France. The World Cup has lift-off and in spectacular fashion. Not since Argentina's defeat by Cameroon 12 years ago has the competition produced such a shock. As Senegal's ecstatic players danced around the pitch and hugged one another at the finish, France's trooped off knowing their aura of invincibility has been shattered. It is too early to suggest their title defence is in tatters; Argentina responded to that opening defeat in 1990 by reaching the final. But France must surely beat Uruguay and Denmark to stay in the tournament and this performance offered no guarantee of that. The absence of Zinedine Zidane and vulnerability of Frank Leboeuf were memorably exploited by the debutants of Senegal. A messy first-half goal by Pape Bouba Diop, made by the lively El Hadji Diouf, was a blow from which the champions could not recover. They had enough chances to have won, stretched Senegal's impressive goalkeeper Tony Sylva and hit the crossbar, but this was not beginners' luck. The African team defended with discipline, stifled their opponents with a 4-5-1 formation and occasionally broke with menace. France, for all their posses sion and territorial dominance, rarely rose above the ordinary. If that was in part because of the organisation and fierce tackling of Senegal, it also reflected the extent to which they rely on the inspiration of Zidane. Youri Djorkaeff and his second-half replacement Christophe Dugarry could not provide the needed element of surprise. The implications of this result will be keenly felt by Sven-Goran Eriksson. The runners-up in England's group face the winners of this section in the next round, so finishing second could become an advantage. Such thoughts, though, mattered little to Senegal. "I dreamed of this match, thought about this match and now it's come true," said Senegal's French coach Bruno Metsu. "It's a great reward for our players and for the Senegalese people. We had a tactical game plan and you have to feel satisfied when it comes off. "We left Diouf up front on his own and deliberately blocked them in midfield. When you are up against a team like France you have to adapt. I said before this tournament that we were not going to be the Jamaica of this World Cup. Today we proved we are a serious team." Diouf is expected to be in the Premiership next season with Liverpool and showed what talent he has. His fast, direct running exposed weaknesses in an ageing France defence and Marcel Desailly was embarrassed by the Lens striker before Diouf raced past Leboeuf and crossed for the winning goal. The 21-year-old was let down only by the frequency with which he was caught offside. If Diouf caught the eye, Senegal's display was notable also for its physical power. Aliou Cissé epitomised the strength and tenacity of Metsu's three-man central midfield which swallowed France's threat through the middle. Pape Malick Diop never flinched at the heart of their defence. "We played a good team who deserved to win,

Germany 1 - 1 Republic of Ireland. Somehow, it seemed inevitable that a man called Keane would have the final say. This was a headline writer's dream, compelling drama and delicious irony rolled into one to leave Ireland and Mick McCarthy planning for the World Cup's second phase with equal measures of hope and expectation here last night. The night that Robbie Keane scored an injury-time equaliser against Germany, just as it seemed McCarthy's men were heading for a defeat that might have precipitated an early flight home, will be remembered as one of the finest moments of Irish sporting history. After 90 minutes of unremitting toil, perspiration and scant regard for loftier reputations, blame was starting to be apportioned. Thoughts were turning to the man formerly known as Ireland's captain, the man whose name now appears on Fifa's list as Roy Keane (A), a man whose blowtorch exhortations in Saipan two weeks ago had, everyone assumed, caused irreparable damage to Ireland's chances of emerging from Group E. And then came the moment, a goal that will be replayed in Ireland over the next few years as often, if not more often, than Packie Bonner saving Daniel Timofte's penalty in Genoa in 1990 and Ray Houghton shinning the winner against Italy in New Jersey in 1994. Pandemonium ensued. Steve Finnan chipped a ball into the penalty area, Niall Quinn leapt highest to nod down and there was Keane, controlling the ball on his chest, evading Carsten Ramelow and lashing in a rising shot that flicked off Oliver Kahn's glove, ricocheting off the post and, gloriously, into the roof of the net. "I enjoyed beating Holland in the qualifiers, I really enjoyed going to Iran in the play-offs, but this is the best of all," said McCarthy. "After everything that has happened, I dread to think what would been written about us if we had lost 1-0. But we don't have to think about that now. I've had some great moments with Ireland and I feel like a very lucky man tonight," said Niall Quinn. "After 92 minutes Robbie had every right to be shattered. But he's an absolute gem. He's burst through and created a piece of history." Quinn's role could not be understated. Until his introduction, 17 minutes from the end, Ireland's breathless endeavour had been matched by an incisive edge only infrequently. Seldom had they threatened to get behind a robotic and robust German defence, rarely had they shown the penetration with which Michael Ballack and Miroslav Klose had combined to open the scoring after 19 minutes. Yet in the short time Quinn had on the pitch, a team containing not a single player from the Premiership's top three clubs started to exude an air of authority that the three-time World Cup winners could never have anticipated. McCarthy, the man with the Mission Impossible ring-tone, has never made a better decision during his six years as manager than to change from 4-4-2 to 3-4-3. World Cup Diary. · Eamon Dunphy was substituted early on Sunday and is probably out of the World Cup - to the delight of the timeservers of the Football Association of Ireland. The author, columnist and former Irish international (think Julie Burchill, only with a higher media profile) made a "brief" appearance on RTE's World Cup panel of experts before being removed and suspended for, ahem, being "unable to fulfil the terms of his contract". "I was not actually drunk - I had been," said the long-term critic of Jack Charlton, Mick McCarthy, the FAI etc (think A A Gill, only with lots more column inches). "I had not been terribly drunk, but I had not slept or been to bed." · Junichi Inamoto and Arsène Wenger were brought together by Japanese television after the game in Yokohama, to the discomfort of both. "It was the moment I was dreading most," said the Japanese midfielder, who is emerging as one of the stars of the tournament after playing no part in Arsenal's Double triumph last season. And the ever urbane Wenger may even have been a little disturbed. "It's not a good time to talk about whether he's leaving Arsenal or staying," said his column in Sankei Sports. "I haven't decided yet." This turns out to be a lie - or, as we say in the media business, a transmission error. Arsenal have already told Inamoto that he can leave and they are so anxious to be rid of him they are ready to write off all the £4m they paid Gamba Osaka last year. · Fearing that Robbie Keane's single-somersault celebration may have seemed a little anticlimactic after Julius Aghahowa's seven back flips, some of Ireland's slimmer and more athletic players have resolved to do better. Oh, and "Richard Dunne reckons he could do four", says Alan Kelly. · Heard about World Cup Benders, the sausages that replicate the curve in David Beckham's free-kick against Greece that clinched England's place in the World Cup finals? You ain't seen nothing yet. "Sausage technology is not quite as developed as aerodynamics, so the curve of this sausage is only in two dimensions," the designer, Dr Bill Crowther, tells the Leicester Mercury. "Ultimately we will be looking at creating a sausage curved in 3D." · Faced with the task of summarising 44 years of World Cup rivalry in a 30-second preview slot, the Korean television station showing the England-Argentina game chose footage of David Batty missing that penalty, Diego Maradona scoring that goal, Antonio Rattin taking that long walk, and British jets prosecuting that war. · Japan's victory over Russia sparked frenzy in the streets and newspapers and came close to setting a Japanese record for television audience share. But it still failed to topple Japan's all-time TV favourite - the 1964 Olympic women's volleyball final. · Yet this World Cup has already set at least one record. Nigeria leave cleaner to carry the can. Nigeria, as if eager to play to the stereotype of an unpredictable nation prone to damaging introspection, cancelled their training session here yesterday without warning. Reporters and photographers told to be on Mashiami Island at 3.30pm turned up to find the place deserted except for a cleaning lady who informed them that Nigeria, England's final Group F opponents on Wednesday, had decided not to bother. The scheduled Nigeria training ground is beside one of Osaka's numerous baseball pitches and the feeling that the Super Eagles, already eliminated from the World Cup, have mentally done a home run was not contradicted by the image of empty stands. No reason was given for the cancellation, although Sven-Goran Eriksson will not want his England players to take it as a sign of Nigerian apathy, especially as the England team also had a day off yesterday. Yet it is beyond dispute that certain Nigerian players have lost their focus. The captain Jay-Jay Okocha, for example, has irked his manager Adegboyega Onigbinde by announcing that, when the game against England ends on Wednesday, he may, at 28, have played his last match for Nigeria. "I think this may be my last tournament for Nigeria," Okocha said. "I think it is time to give others a chance to play for the national team. One cannot be there for ever. There are some leaving the scene and we hope that those coming after us will do better than we have done." Okocha is also leaving his club Paris St-Germain following a dispute over wages and a new contract. He wants to go to England but his £25,000 a week at PSG is likely to deter a number of clubs. While not accepting Okocha's international retirement, Onigbinde faces a task coping without him or, indeed, persuading him to stay on. "Jay-Jay and all the others believe that everyone is useful for the team but his experience, technical ability and discipline stand out," said Onigbinde. "He is the captain and he has carried that role very well in terms of both leadership and performance. Jay-Jay means a lot to the Nigeria team." June 10: Nigeria, already elminated from the World Cup, cancelled their traning session yesterday without warning.

Spain drools over 'Real Madrid's World Cup'. Two matches, two victories and six goals. It's little wonder that Spain's ever-rational media is queuing up to declare the selección certain World Cup finalists. A questionnaire in yesterday's Marca revealed that, from Antena 3 to Vía Digital, via Cope Radio, El País and the rest, media faith in the national team has never been higher. Of the 18 journalists asked, nine thought Spain would get to the final, five said the semis - the country's best ever finish - and four opted for the quarters. No one entertained the possibility that Spain could go out in the second round - Cameroon and "the British team" - that's Ireland by the way - are considered easy opponents. The way the press so spectacularly underestimates the Irish and Cameroon (both of whom could cause Spain's defence serious problems), the way they have turned two good performances into "brilliant" ones, and the way they have refused to accept that Paraguay and Slovenia are frankly rubbish has again demonstrated that some things never change - opening day victory or not. One thing, though, proves the point more than any other - the press's continued obsession with Real Madrid and Barcelona. Even with World Cup success a genuine possibility, the country's sports dailies have refused to hide their club allegiances. After the Slovenia match, El Mundo Deportivo and Sport, the two Catalan dailies, inevitably picked out Barcelona's Luis Enrique as Man of the Match. They were the only ones to do so and have carried on where they left off, showing more interest in Brazil's Rivaldo and Ronaldo than in the Spanish team. That, though, makes sense - after all, these are pro-Barça, Catalan dailies, and proud of it. Why should they care about the Spanish national team? It's a rather different story for Marca and AS. The country's Madrid-based best sellers laughably claim objectivity. AS, in particular, gleefully jumped up and down clapping when national manager José Antonio Camacho declared it "time the national team, not the clubs, was No.1". "At last!" they said, "a manager who really cares about Spain!". World Cup success was now a foregone conclusion - after all, an obsession with the clubs was the "main reason" why the national team had failed so often. As if to prove the point, Atlético Madrid fan and television presenter José Ribagorda wrote in yesterday's AS that "I never thought I'd celebrate goals from Raúl, Hierro and Morientes, but I've forgotten their white shirts - they're all property of the national team now. We're all in this together". But while the sentiment was perfectly justifiable - if a bit bloody obvious - Ribagorda's point was completely undone by the rest of the day's paper. Flick to the front cover and there's Roberto Carlos, announcing not only that Brazil "will be waiting for Spain in the final" but also that "Real Madrid should be proud of their World Cup players".
