South Africa end Slovenia's misery. Slovenia's nightmare week ended in depressing fashion as they were kicked out in a turgid match with South Africa in Daegu. The question before was whether Slovenia's coach Srecko Katanec could rally his side following the expulsion of star player Zlatko Zahovic after his open criticism of his manager. The similarity with Ireland's situation with Roy Keane was impossible to ignore; the outcome couldn't have been more different. Far from united in the face of adversity, Slovenia seemed to carry their differences with them onto the pitch, and played accordingly. Katanec himself ended the game in the stands after the fourth official banished him for his "indiscrete" behaviour on the touchline. He won't have liked the view from there any better. South Africa weren't much better, but once they had taken an early lead it was they that dictated any play. The goal befitted such a disjointed game, Siyabonga Nomvethe leaping to head Quinton Fortune's fourth minute free-kick but making do with his thigh to bundle it past a helpless Marko Simeunovic. By half time, Slovenia had yet to test Andre Arendse in South Africa's goal, their only effort from open play ending in the crowd after new Spurs signing Milenko Acimovic fancied his chances from 25 yards. South Africa, with Fortune going through the paces and Benni McCarthy finding space, if not always the right choice of shot, made passing resemblances to cohesion, but never really looked like killing the game off. Slovenia showed signs of punishing McCarthy's wastefulness late on as their World Cup fate loomed large, but still failed to craft a clear opening. It was the Africans who came closest again, substitue Delron Buckley's shot glancing wide off Simeunovic after being put through by the imaginative Sibusiso Zuma. South Africa's win, their first in the World Cup finals and only their second ever against European opposition (to add to the scalp of Malta), leaves them within reach of the second round. Slovenia, meanwhile, will go home, as from Euro 2000, having made a mark of sorts. This time, though, it was the wrong sort. Slovenia's nightmare week ended in depressing fashion as they were kicked out in a turgid match with South Africa in Daegu.

England players can't afford to bask in glory. It was our turn and the nation deserved it. After '86, '98, David Beckham and all that, England in general - and the captain in particular - were due a win. Watching it brought back a host of memories of four years ago and I have to admit that I would have loved to have been in the middle of it again, not only to rectify a wrong but also because, as a defender, to have held out for those last 15 minutes under siege would have been so satisfying. People have told me how nail-biting it was, how every minute seemed like an hour, with Argentina putting together wave after wave of attacks. I have to say I didn't see it like that. Mentally it was a tough time for the defence, yes, with concentration stretched to the limit, but physically it was not that difficult. I felt that England had the situation well under control. It has been an enjoyable World Cup and to see English defenders in their element gave me more pleasure. Sol Campbell and Rio Ferdinand had great protection in front of them, notably from Nicky Butt, and Argentina played to English, and not their own, strengths. It was a Premiership game for our back four. Neither could I see 'Old Safe Hands', the unflappable David Seaman, being beaten. I was never out of my seat the way I was when Robbie Keane scored for Ireland in the last minute of their game against Germany the previous day. Now comes the dangerous bit. It was England's cup final, for historical reasons a match the country so wanted to win, and in terms of qualifying for the last 16, needed to win. One win does not make a World Cup, however, even if it was against the competition's favourites and one of our biggest footballing enemies. It is a weekend for celebration but not to go mad. Euro 2000 springs to mind. The 1-0 win over Germany papered over many cracks and led to false expectations. There are still cracks in this England team. If we are not careful, we will believe the hard work has been done, believe that we can now win the tournament. The best teams go on from a result like that. We don't want to come home thinking that at least we beat Argentina. That will mean instead that at most we beat Argentina and we cling to it as consolation. Nigeria may be out but they will still present a tricky task in Osaka on Wednesday. Sapporo was an emotional occasion but what I like about Sven-Göran Eriksson is that he takes the heat out of games. Telling the team to forget about the Falklands or revenge for '98 was exactly the right approach, the same one he adopted for the game against Germany in Munich last September when there was also cultural baggage to be carried. We have needed to get away from all that nonsense and concentrate on the football for a while now. I remain less sure about the manager's approach to games. Boban: Italy could crash out of uncertain World Cup. In only the first ten days, this World Cup has proven to be one of real shocks. From Senegal to, well, England, there has been no end of upsets and Group G is no exception. Croatia came from behind to beat Italy 2-1 in Ibaraki yesterday and are now level on points with the Azzurri. And according to former Croatian international Zvonimir Boban, the bookmaker's favourites may yet fall at the first hurdle. Boban, who captained Croatia to their third place finish at France 98, was a key player for AC Milan throughout the 90s and is no stranger to Italian football. But, speaking exclusively to Guardian Unlimited Football, he insisted that Giovanni Trappatoni's side must improve the level of their performance if they are to qualify for the second round. "I really don't know if Italy will qualify," he said after Mexico had beaten Ecuador 2-1 to move to first place in the group. "Croatia were much the better side last night, and they deserved their victory. "If Italy play like Italy can, then I would expect them to beat Mexico. But they didn't play like Italy last night, and you never know what will happen in football." Mexico currently lead Group G on six points, with both Italy and Croatia behind them on three. Ecuador, who have no points, could yet still qualify if they beat Croatia by two goals. If they win and Mexico also beat Italy by a sufficient margin then the south Americans would also go through in what would be one of the biggest shocks in World Cup history. Javier Aguirre's Mexico dominated today's match in Miyagi, but still only won the match by a single goal. "Mexico will cause some trouble to the Italians with their approach play," he said, "but Christian Vieri will enjoy playing against the Mexican defence. Ecuador succumb to Mexican waves. Mexico went to the top of Group G after fighting back to dump Ecuador out of the World Cup. They had gone behind in the fifth minute when Agustin Delgado scored Ecuador's first goal at a World Cup. But Jared Borgetti cancelled that out and Gerardo Torrado hit the winner. Italy's shock defeat to Croatia yesterday opened the way for Mexico, and they eventually grabbed their second win in two games despite the early setback. Delgado headed home the opener from Ulises De La Cruz's cross, his effort proving too strong for goalkeeper Oscar Perez, who got a hand to the ball but failed to keep it out. Mexico immediately returned down the other end in search of an equaliser with Braulio Luna dragging a shot wide from 20 yards. Although the Central Americans were having the better of the possession, their final ball was disappointing. The main flashpoint after the goal came when Mexico midfielder Joahan Rodriguez gently pushed away Ecuador's Raul Guerron in the 16th minute. The defender slumped to the ground, however referee Mourad Daami was unimpressed by his histrionics. Mexico did finally find their range with their crossing in the 28th minute, and they were rewarded with the equaliser. Ramon Morales put the ball in from the left and Borgetti nipped in at the near post to flick the ball across keeper Jose Cevallos and into the net. Cuauhtemoc Blanco swung a free-kick dangerously into the six-yard area, but straight into the arms of Cevallos moments later as Mexico picked up the slack. The 31-year-old keeper was again alert to smother Blanco's shot from the edge of the area, although the forward would have been disappointed not to get more power on his effort. Mexico then deservedly went in front in the 57th minute through Gerardo Torrado, the midfielder's 20-yard left-footed strike arrowing past the despairing dive of Cevallos. Ecuador, though, signalled their intention to fight until the end with Guerron drilling a long-range free-kick straight into the midrift of Perez in the 65th minute. Borgetti almost grabbed his second to make the game safe moments later, but his strike from outside the area rebounded off the post. But they could not finish Ecuador off and had to endure a nervous final 10 minutes. First it needed a great reflex stop from Perez, with his right leg, to deny Carlos Tenorio after he burst into the box. The South Americans went close again five minutes later, Marlon Ayovi putting Alex Aguinaga's pull-back inches wide of the post from 20 yards out. But the Mexicans held on, and Blanco almost made it three for them in injury time, only to be denied by Cevallos's full-length save. June 9: Ecuador notched their first World Cup finals goal, but Mexico came back to steal the initiative in Group G.

Italy stunned by Croatia. Two late goals in four minutes gave Croatia a stunning 2-1 win over Italy in World Cup group G on Saturday - the Italians' first defeat in normal time in the finals since 1994. Italy thought they had grabbed a freakish injury time equaliser when a hopeful long ball from inside his own half by Materazzi somehow bypassed Vieri, Inzaghi and goalkeeper Pletikosa to find its way into the Croatian net. Unfortunately for Italy, the bizarre strike was ruled out by a linesman, apparently for shirt pulling by Filippo Inzaghi. The new cup favourites had led through a Christian Vieri header in the 55th minute and looked to be dawdling towards their second straight win in the finals before a stirring, late Croat revival. Substitute Ivica Olic pulled the Croats level in the 73rd minute before Milan Rapaic stunned the Italians four minutes later, throwing group G wide open. Italy, Mexico and Croatia, third in the 1998 World Cup, all have three points ahead of Sunday's game between Mexico and Ecuador. The last time Italy lost in normal time in the finals was their first game of the 1994 World Cup when they were beaten 1-0 by Ireland. Despite this setback, they still went on to reach the final where they lost on penalties to Brazil. Italy coach Giovanni Trapattoni was forced to reorganise when central defender Alessandro Nesta suffered what appeared to be a foot injury in the 24th minute and was replaced by Marco Materazzi. The Croats took advantage of the change to gain the initiative with Zvonimir Soldo going close with a low drive. Just two minutes after coming on Materazzi was then forced to clear off the line after Italy keeper Gianluigi Buffon could only get one hand on a shot from Davor Vugrinec who had linked up well with Milan Rapaic. Vugrinec and Rapaic, drafted in at the expense of Davor Suker and Robert Prosinecki after Croatia's poor showing in their 1-0 opening game defeat to Mexico, were begining to cause problems for the Italians and Rapaic forced another save out of Buffon just before the break. But the Italians reasserted themselves after the break with Vieri claiming his eighth goal in seven matches in the finals outjumping Josip Simunic to head home at the back post. But this time the Italians failed to defend their lead. Substitute Olic brought things level when he slid in a cross from Robert Jarni. Then Rapaic beat Materazzi and Paolo Maldini and fired in a shot which was headed out by Christian Panucci - the ball was played back into Rapaic whose hooked shot took a slight deflection and beat a stranded Buffon. Three minutes from time Francesco Totti rapped the inside of the post from a 25-metre free kick but the ball ran across the Croatian goal before being cleared. June 8: Croatia came from behind to beat Italy, but not without controversy as Group G was thrown wide open.

Late goal earns Costa Rica draw. Brazil were the main beneficiaries of this tense draw between Turkey and Costa Rica, with the result meaning the South Americans qualify for the second round of the World Cup. Emre Belozoglu's goal early in the second half separated the sides until substitute Winston Parks slammed in a late equaliser for Costa Rica. The second qualifying place remains delicately poised between today's contestants, with Costa Rica needing a result against Brazil, and Turkey having to score a hatful against China to make sure they progress instead. The 50,000-capacity stadium at Incheon was filled to the roof that crowns this spectacular ground, a significant number of seats having been given as rewards to local volunteers. As it was they witnessed a match that did not catch fire until the dying minutes. It was 10 minutes before there was an effort on goal of note, Paulo Wanchope volleying a bouncing ball a yard over the bar. Umit Ozat was similarly close from a long-range free-kick, his effort whizzing past an upright, before Erick Lonnis fielded Hasan Sas' dipping shot. Sas nearly broke through the Costa Rican ranks after a clever free-kick routine but Gilberto Martinez flung himself in front of the Turkish midfielder's low drive. Shortly after the half-hour, Costa Rica put together an excellent break but when Ronald Gomez sent Walter Centeno through, the midfielder blasted over with only keeper Rustu Recber to beat. The central Americans looked to have plenty of skill, but the slow pace to their game played into Turkey's hands, and each pass was a vital second too late to cause danger. Thje Turks, on the other hand, had the necessary extra quality and in Sas a player of real verve. Early in the second half he weaved through the defence and earned a corner, from which Hakan Sukur sent a flicked header past the far-post. Hakan Sukur then had an even better opportunity when Tugay's ball over the defence put him clear, but he sliced his shot badly wide. Missing the target became contagious, and Costa Rica picked up the bug when a drive by Gomez from 18 yards flashed a couple of yards the wrong side of the post. Ten minutes into the second half, Turkey managed to get their aim right and took the lead through an excellent individual goal by Emre. Sas chested down the ball to Emre on the edge of the area, the little midfielder feinted to shoot, cut into the box and though Martinez managed to block his first effort he tucked the rebound past Lonnis at the near-post. Costa Rica at least came out and had a go but were guilty of two awful misses, Wilmer Lopez and Mauricio Solis both blasting into the stratosphere after some attractive build-up play by their team-mates. Manchester City striker Wanchope then ran at the Turkish defence and slipped Lopez through but he could not beat Rustu from a narrow angle, while Winston Parks goal-bound strike was blocked by the forceful Umit. With four minutes left, Parks did manage to force the equaliser.

World Cup Diary. South American fans are famous for their fervour, so we should hardly be surprised that a dispute between fans of Brazil and Argentina led to a pitched battle fought with stones and iron bars in which 10 supporters were injured. The only surprise was the location: Badanpur in Bangladesh where villagers fought over which flag - Brazil's or Argentina's - should fly from the tallest coconut tree in the village. Two Bangladeshi Argentina supporters have already died in flag-related incidents in this World Cup. · The rise of Senegal has taken everyone by surprise, not least the three English journalists who interviewed the defender Ferdinand Coly after yesterday's 3-3 draw with Uruguay. After respectfully calling him Ferdinand throughout, the hacks retired to write their prose - only for a Portuguese writer to overhear them and interject: "By the way, Ferdinand Coly is the one standing over there, with Coly on the back of his shirt." Thankfully she was also able to point out they had been speaking to Alassane Ndour. · South Africa's striker Benni McCarthy went into a fit of laughter when asked to comment on rumours that his side inject the blood of wild animals into their legs to make them run faster. McCarthy finally replied: "Don't worry. South Africa don't have anything to do with that. We practise fair play and I think we do the same as any European country does. We don't do no voodoo stuff." · Belgium forgot its cultural differences yesterday as French and Dutch speakers united to rubbish the Red Devils' uninspired 1-1 draw with Tunisia. "Pitiful" fumed the Flemish daily Het Laatste Nieuws. The French-language La Dernière Heure was less polite, screaming "This is crap, Robert", accusing the veteran coach Robert Waseige, who had already announced he will quit after the finals. For him the last hour could well be Friday's final group game with Russia. · Gone but not forgotten... A Seoul artist was left with the tricky task of picking three players at the 2002 World Cup to star in a 20ft high mural marking the tournament. Naturally he settled on Shearer, Jürgen Klinsmann and Colombia's Carlos Valderrama. June 12: All the news from off the pitch.

Italy 1 - 2 Croatia. The Japanese city of Ibaraki might assume a place in Italian football mythology alongside Middlesbrough, where Italy lost to North Korea in 1966. Their defeat was almost as much of a shock, and means that the first three favourites for this World Cup might fall in the first round. The Italians must beat Mexico in their final game on Thursday or they could find themselves arriving home on Friday, possibly just after France and Argentina. Croatia might yet repeat their heroics of 1998, when they reached the semi-finals. In this World Cup, who knows? The game had long seemed headed for the most predictable of outcomes, until a linesman from Denmark intervened, twice invoking the wrath of the Italians by prompting the referee Graham Poll to disallow goals. 'I don't blame the referee, but that assistant should only work in amateur matches,' said Christian Vieri. Italy had been masterful in their opening match, defeating Ecuador 2-0 almost in neutral gear. A woeful Croatia had lost 1-0 to Mexico. Yet the Italians, predictably, were taking no risks. Vieri was again the sole out-and-out striker, with Francesco Totti just behind. Croatia had dropped its old men Davor Suker and Robert Prosinecki, both embarrassingly off the pace against Mexico. The 32-year-old Alen Boksic was retained, which may have been a mistake. Yet after Alessandro Nesta limped off on 23 minutes, to be replaced by Marco Materazzi, strange hints began emerging that this Italian defence might be penetrable. The change to this most settled of defences produced disarray. On 25 minutes, Zvonimir Soldo fired only just wide from 20 yards after a long build-up, and a minute later the men in tablecloth shirts should have scored. Milan Rapaic suddenly found himself in the unfamiliar position of staring straight at an Italian goalkeeper from 12 yards . Instead of shooting, he passed to Davor Vugrinec, whose shot was palmed away by Gianluigi Buffon. Rapaic had unforgivably been omitted from Croatia's opening game, in favour of the ageing Prosinecki. Yesterday the 28-year-old Fenerbahce man flitted from wing to wing, giving the runaround to Christian Panucci and Paolo Maldini. Italy's best chance fell to Cristiano Doni, who was well blocked by Stipe Pletikosa. But otherwise it was a dull half. Many of the Japanese spectators watching solemnly in Italian face-paint must have wondered what the world saw in this sport. We have seen Italy play first halves like this before: the opponent is given some fun before having its head chopped off. That moment seemed to have arrived five minutes after the break, when Vieri headed home from point-blank range, but the Danish linesman, Jens Larsen judged him to be offside. In the 56th minute, Vieri did get his third goal of this competition, and his eighth in seven career World Cup games. Tunisia push Belgium towards exit. Tunisia found an unlikely World Cup lifeline and plunged Belgium into trouble as the north Africans came from behind to earn a deserved 1-1 draw in their group H match. Another defeat would have eliminated Tunisia but, playing with new-found spirit, they impressed throughout and were rewarded by Raouf Bouzaiane's 17th-minute equaliser. Bouzaiane curled home a marvellous free kick just four minutes after Belgium captain Marc Wilmots had given his side the lead. Belgium were left to settle for their fifth World Cup finals draw in succession and, after their poor performance, they must now secure victory against Russia in their last group game on Friday to stand any hope of progressing to the second round. Tunisia also have to win their last group match, against Japan on Friday, while improving their goal difference at the same time - but the players rescued a great deal of pride from this performance and will still hope to reach the last 16 for the first time. Japan, the co-hosts, lead the section with four points. Russia are second with three, followed by Belgium - who drew 2-2 with Japan in their opener - with two and Tunisia, who lost their opening game 2-0 to Russia, on one. Wilmots wasted little time in underlining his importance to Belgium's cause when the midfielder - playing as a makeshift forward - pounced on a 13th minute knock down from Branko Strupar to sweep his side into the lead from close range. It should have ushered in a period of dominance for the Red Devils, but with Tunisia's Hassen Gabsi and Slim Ben Achour linking well with lone frontman Ziad Jaziri, Belgium quickly relinquished the initiative. Just four minutes after falling behind, Bouzaiane stepped up to curl his left-footed free kick past Belgium keeper Geert de Vlieger from 20 yards. That came just moments after Khaled Badra had seen his excellent long-range strike ruled out for the infringement which led to Bouzaiane's free kick. Belgium coach Robert Waseige's disgust was evident in his half-time changes, for he introduced 2001 Belgian player of the year Wesley Sonck and Sven Vermant. But Tunisia again began brightly in the second period, with Kais Ghodhbane fizzing a 25-yard volley narrowly wide. Sonck should have connected with a Yves Vanderhaeghe cross after 56 minutes and Wilmots was denied by the legs of goalkeeper Ali Boumnijel. Tunisia then found a second wind in the last five minutes and went close when another Ghodhbane drive was beaten out by de Vlieger before Sonck blew Belgium's final chance, blasting wide from inside the area. The result leaves Belgium still waiting for their first World Cup finals victory since the 1994 tournament. Tunisia grab a World Cup lifeline and plunge Belgium into trouble as the north Africans come from behind to earn a deserved 1-1 draw in group H.

World Cup diary. Since glitzy new stadiums are two a penny, host cities have had to be inventive to stand out from the crowd. None more so than Suwon, just south of Seoul, which has carved a niche for itself with some unique sanitation facilities. Lavatories that have been designated Beautiful Toilets are even marked on the map ahead of major landmarks. Obviously a man with a clear idea of where the seat of affections truly rests, the mayor's belief was that "if tourists feel comfortable in the toilet they will want to come back to Korea". · England's draw with Nigeria was so dull that fans found alternative entertainment by watching one of the stewards fall asleep on his feet before falling over. Now there's a job Emile Heskey could do. · The World Cup, as we all know, can make it tricky to concentrate on what you are meant to be doing during the day. So it is not surprising to learn that crime has dropped dramatically in South Korea since the start of the tournament. Murderers have apparently been most distracted with a 67% reduction in homicides. Great to know that the World Cup is bringing football to the ordinary people. · Never mind the perilous business of trying to back a favourite in this turbulent market. Shrewd punters know the bet to be on is Victor Chandler's offer of 4-1 that Don't Cry For Me Argentina is re-released. Who cares if it doesn't pay out, just think of the emotional satisfaction. · Japanese fans brought the country's largest mobile phone company to a halt this week when 100 million callers tried to buy the 750 tickets that had been made available for Japan's game against Russia. A spokesperson for NTT DoCoMo blamed full-throttle use of the automatic redial button and announced that a recorded "sold out" message will greet callers looking to buy tickets for today's encounter between Japan and Tunisia, which should calm things down no end. June 13: All the news from off the pitch.

World Cup let downs. England 1950 England's disastrous World Cup debut is usually remembered for how a line-up which reads like a winning hand at cigarette card top trumps was turned over by the United States. What is forgotten about England's short-lived participation in Brazil is that it owed as much to the affects of climate change and long-haul travel which took a far greater toll back then. Far from excusing England, though, this illuminates the FA's palpable lack of preparation. Their other victors, Spain, certainly coped okay - until the hosts beat them 6-1 in the final stage, that is. Still, it took a few more years before England stopped making excuses and realised that their failure was not simply a freak occurence. Italy 1966 Pak Doo Ik's winner for North Korea finally relieved England of the ignominy of their defeat in 1950, knocking out an Italian team made up largely of Inter's European Cup winning sides. Italy had started the game needing just a point to qualify for the quarter-finals, and had considered the Koreans a joke beforehand. History forgets that Italy spent an hour down to ten men, although the supporters awaiting them on their return weren't especially sympathetic, either. To put Korea's achievement in perspective, Italy won the European Championship two years later. Scotland 1978 Scotland's pre-tournament bluster owed as much to their having knocked out European champions Czechoslovakia in the qualifiers as to England's notable absence. Ally MacLeod's side were no mugs, either, with Kenny Dalglish, John Robertson, Willie Johnston and Archie Gemmil providing the creativity to complement the grit. Of course, things couldn't have gone more awry. In declaring Scotland world beaters, MacLeod had reckoned without Cubillas's Peru and his own side's ineptitude against Iran. Beating the Dutch by three was always beyond them, but only just in the end. Scotland, though, learned never to expect anything again. Spain 1982 The hosts started badly, hinted at recovery and then just disintegrated. An opening draw with Honduras (honestly) was not the ideal grounding, and although they rallied against Yugoslavia, it was the defeat to Billy Bingham's Northern Ireland which really ballsed it up for Spain, casting them into a second round group with England and West Germany. There they lost to the eventual runners-up and came within the depth of Kevin Keegan's perm of doing the same against England. Spain hadn't truly expected their team to win the thing, but it rankles that it stands as the most disappointing World Cup performance by a host nation in Europe. Colombia 1994 Pele said they could win in the United States, and the world listened. Their 5-0 victory in Argentina during the qualifiers had made ripples, and a skilful team boasted the talents of Carlos Valderrama, Adolfo Valencia and Faustino Asprilla, while sweeper-keeper Rene Higuitta was replaced by the more conventional Oscar Cordoba. World Cup entertainers. Hungary 1954 The statistics paint the most accurate picture. In 30 games leading up to the 1954 finals, and with an approach to unbridled attacking football that would give Kevin Keegan ideas, the Magyars weighed in with 121 goals against many of Europe's tidiest sides - swaggering past Czechoslovakia (5-0 and 5-1), Italy (3-0) and, most famously, England in their own back garden (6-3). Fuelled by the portly probings of PuskÀs and a bagful of innovations - the deep-lying centre forward, near-post corners and a pretty system based on moving triangles - they headed for Switzerland as overwhelming favourites. Having averaged more than six goals a game on their way to the final - including an 8-3 mauling of the German hosts - Hungarian confidence was understandably high. The West Germans, however, had their own ideas come their rematch in the final, and, alas, defeat by the odd goal in five against their physical opponents sent Hungary scuttling home with a title they hold to this day: the best team never to have won the World Cup. Holland 1974 Built on the elastic brilliance of Johan Cruyff and his all-conquering Ajax colleagues, the Dutch took Total Football to the finals in '74. They toyed with opponents like a playboy matador, safe in the knowledge that if the killer blow was required, they could supply it. Arrogance? The Dutch? Surely not. The formula took them within touching distance of the Big Cup, where only those West Germans, the hosts again, stood in their path. And then indecision crept in. Moving quickly through the gears against the footballing minnows of New Zealand and usual suspects Scotland, they appeared to have found their rhythm at the perfect moment. It still rates as the World Cup's finest hour (and a half), and it's how they would have wanted to go. No frills England book Denmark meeting. It wasn't the prettiest or most fluent of performances, but bleary-eyed England fans hip-hopping their way into work this morning won't care a jot. This 0-0 draw against Nigeria, coupled with Sweden's draw over Argentina, means that England are through to a last 16 meeting with Denmark on Saturday morning. England fans will expect victory, as always, but judging from this performance their team shouldn't be overwhelming favourites to progress. For today we saw Ye Olde England - sporadically inventive and largely unconvincing - rather than the gun-slinging, Argentina-slaying colossus of last Friday. They really should have swatted aside an inexperienced Nigeria team that had nothing to play for, but were happy instead to saunter to a draw. Critics will argue that Sven-Goran Eriksson's side never really looked like losing. True - but apart from a glorious Teddy Sheringham chance 15 minutes from time apart - they never looked like winning either. The match had a lethargic, mid-afternoon-in-summer feel to it from the start: Nigeria content to dominate possession, England equally content to let them. For the first half-hour England's threat came from an opponent: Nigeria's teenage goalkeeper Vincent Enyeama. Every time David Beckham whipped in a corner, Enyeama - the adrenaline no doubt racing - came. And flapped. Horribly. England, however, couldn't take an advantage. Finally, as half-time approached, England roused. Owen had a shot on the run deflected away and another charged down, while Scholes' venomous 25-yard volley was brilliantly tipped onto the post by Enyeama. Second half, same story. Nigeria occasionally threatened through the speed of Julius Aghahowa and the skill of Jay Jay Okocha, but Rio Ferdinand - again superb in defence - put out the fires early. England could - should - have scored twice. First, Michael Owen's outstretched boot just failed to connect with a driven cross from Emile Heskey. An even better chance then fell to substitute Teddy Sheringham on 75 minutes - Ashley Cole's cross deflected off Owen's thigh and into the Tottenham striker's path, 10 yards out, but leaning back he fired well over. After that, England seemed happy to make a non-aggression pact. And with the temperature in the 90s, the overwhelming humidity alien, and a place in the second round assured, you could hardly blame them. But in the final 25 minutes substitutes Nelson Cuevas scored twice and Jorge Campos once to see the Paraguayans finish above South Africa on goals scored and so claim a place in the last 16. Paraguay went close with a glancing header from Jose Cardozo before they suffering a serious blow midway through the first half when they were reduced to 10 men. Paredes, who had been cautioned after just four minutes, caught Milenko Acimovic in the face with his right elbow to earn his second booking of the match and a red card. Slovenia, who had already been eliminated, managed their first shot on target when Miran Pavlin's curling effort was comfortably saved by Chilavert. These were worrying times for Paraguay and the impressive Acimovic shot against the crossbar with Chilavert completely beaten before the Strasbourg goalkeeper parried Cimirotic's drive. But then the game turned on its head in the 65th minute when Paraguay equalised through Cuevas, who had only been on the pitch for four minutes after replacing Cardozo. Beckham eyes Brazil. David Beckham is hoping to beat Brazil at their own game should the South Americans be England's next opponents at the World Cup. The England captain admits he would relish scoring direct from a free-kick against a country which virtually invented the art of curling and swerving dead balls to lethal effect. But he also insisted that England's players weren't getting carried away despite the massive level of expectancy back home and among the fans out in Japan. He told Sky Sports News: "It would be nice to get any goal in that game, we'll see. "If a free-kick's there I'll be ready, I've not really had one close enough to goal yet. "We're not allowing ourselves to get carried away, we've done well." Beckham, who has already received a Father's Day card from his son Brooklyn, said he had also spoken to him about the game. "He's very happy obviously, he watched most of the game and then went to a dog show, so he wasn't getting carried away either." The Manchester United star acknowledged that a quarter-final meeting with Brazil was a mouth-watering prospect, but Ronaldo and co still have to overcome Belgium in their last-16 clash. "Obviously the people's choice would be Brazil," said Beckham. "The last time we played them at the World Cup was in 1970, I remember seeing the pictures of (Bobby) Moore changing shirts with Pele; two great people like that and it's two great nations." Asked if he thought this England team were making history themselves, he replied: "Not yet. We are three games closer to the final but we can't let ourselves get carried away. We know people are getting excited at home, and so they should be." Beckham was glad he was able to help ward off the only threat of indiscipline yesterday when he stepped in around right-back Danny Mills when he appeared set for a scuffle. "When situations happen like that you have to get round your team-mates. It was important I was closest to him and it was important I got round him and calmed the situation down," Beckham added. David Beckham is hoping to beat Brazil at their own game should the South Americans be England's next opponents at the World Cup.

Cold War in Japan. On first impressions, today's Group H match between Japan and Russia does not conjure up images of a local derby, but it very definitely is. Only 43 kilometres separate Japan and the large Russian island of Sakhalin, and on a clear day from Hokkaido, Japan's northernmost island, it is even possible to see one of the Russian-administered Kuril Islands. The Kuril Islands are a source of tension between the two nations. Japan maintains that these islands are Japanese sovereign territory. The two countries still technically remain at war, as no peace treaty has ever been signed from the Second World War. The words 'the day the four islands come back is the day of peace' are written above the entrance to the Hokkaido prefectural office in Sapporo. Despite this, contact and trade between the Russian Far East and Hokkaido has been increasing year upon year since the break-up of the Soviet Union. In the port of Otaru, 30 minutes from Sapporo, Russian ships can be seen sitting dockside, unloading cargoes of timber and fish, while secondhand Japanese cars and electrical products wait for the return voyage. This increase in the number of Russian seamen arriving in Japan has not been without problems - particualrly so in that most relaxing of Japanese traditions, onsen (hot spring baths). The 1990s saw a number of onsen in Otaru put up notices that forbade Russians and foreigners to enter. The exclusion of foreign bathers was seized upon by a number of foreign residents of Hokkaido, who seek to document instances of racial discrimination in Japan. This fight against discrimination has resulted in a lawsuit being filed against one Otaru onsen and the City Government of Otaru for not upholding certain national treatises. Arudou Debito, a naturalised Japanese citizen, formerly David Aldwinckle of the USA, is one of the three people taking out this civil action. Arudou sees this discrimination as a legacy of the Second World War. 'A lot of people living in Otaru are veterans of Sakhalin. It used to be Japanese but was taken over by Russia in the closing days of war. They had to live under the Russians until the San Francisco Treaty, when they were repatriated.' Overall, it is estimated that up to 700,000 Japanese were taken prisoner of war by the Russians and it was not until 1958 that repatriation was practically completed. Despite the removal of the signs, foreigners can still have problems, but it must be made clear that exclusion of foreigners and World Cup supporters is rare and isolated. The most common comment from foreign World Cup supporters about their experiences in Japan to date would have to be: 'The Japanese are so friendly and helpful.' Where shall I be watching the Japan-Russia match? It will be difficult to pass up the invitation from Anatoly, a Russian seaman, to join him and the 28 other Russian crew members onboard Chendensky , a Russian ship waiting to load a cargo of secondhand cars. Sweden 1 - 2 Senegal. Victory over the French cockerel prompted a mass cooking of chickens in Dakar, and the Senegalese must have been boiling root vegetables yesterday after Bruno Metsu's side snatched a golden-goal victory over the Swedes to enter the quarter-finals of the World Cup at their first attempt. If the celebrations on the pitch following Henri Camara's winner were anything to go by, flowering plants may be in danger too. First the entire team bundled on top of each other, then Camara was chaired across the pitch by his team-mates. Finally a clutch of players - led by El Hadji Diouf - performed a line dance for each of the four stands in turn. Though Diouf failed to get on the scoresheet again, he almost single-handedly led the team in the Big Eye stadium: his ability to retain possession, and push the ball forward through the smallest of openings was invaluable. The 21-year-old is not unaware of his own talents and neither was he shy of proclaiming the importance of his team's win. "Today we were representing Senegal, we were representing Africa, but we were also representing France", he said, his designer sunglasses sparkling in the flashlights. "It was a very difficult match, and both teams tried hard to win. But we showed that we were not just friends but brothers and we were determined to make the quarter-finals." That determination was called upon after only 11 minutes, when a mistake by Tony Sylva cost them an early goal. A corner from Anders Svensson should have been claimed by Monaco's reserve keeper, but the ball slipped through his fingers and Henrik Larsson headed it low into the net. It was to be the last time that Larsson would appear in a Sweden shirt - he announced his international retirement after the match. But Senegal deserved an equaliser, and it came before half-time. Diouf's looped pass reached Camara 20 yards from goal, and he produced one shimmy then another before darting past Teddy Lucic and shooting low inside Magnus Hedman's far post. Sweden grabbed the initiative once more in the second half and perhaps should have scored as Senegal were the first to tire. However, the 90 minutes elapsed without score and the game went into extra-time. There is much debate about the golden goal and its tendency to induce defensive play, but the extra period only incited both sides into adventure yesterday. With one sublime piece of skill, Sweden almost snatched it. Svensson, now known as the Cannonball Kid after his free kick against Argentina, took a Larsson knockdown and spun on the ball beautifully to leave Ferdinand Coly flailing. He composed himself, shot fiercely, and saw the ball come back off the post. A few minutes later the game was over. A back-heel from Pape Thiaw found Camara, who attacked the Swedish defence as he had for his first goal and hit the ball hard and low inside the same post. Senegal snatch golden goal. Henri Camara struck twice and snatched only the second golden goal winner in World Cup history to earn Senegal a quarter-final World Cup place after being held 1-1 by Sweden at the end of normal time. Sweden took the lead through Henrik Larsson's 11th-minute header only for World Cup debutants Senegal, who stunned holders France 1-0 in the opening game of the tournament, to equalise through Camara eight minutes before half-time. Camara then struck his second 104 minutes into the match with a low drive into the bottom left-hand corner of the net off the post. The only previous occasion when a World Cup tie was settled by a golden goal was four years ago when France beat Paraguay 1-0 in the second round, with central defender Laurent Blanc grabbing the all-important strike on the way to their eventual title win. Senegal are only the second African nation, after Cameroon in 1990, to reach the last eight. Like Cameroon who beat world champions Argentina in the opening match in 1990, Senegal upset the previous holders in the opening game of the tournament. The winners will now play Japan or Turkey in the quarter-finals. Whatever the result, a team which has never reached the semi-final of the World Cup will qualify for the last four here. Lars Lagerback's Sweden had come through a desperately difficult group F involving England and Argentina undefeated and went ahead when prolific Celtic striker Larsson headed home an Anders Svensson corner. It was the Swede's 24th goal in 72 internationals but after that the 1994 semi-finalists lost their early momentum. Senegal, Africa's only representatives in the last 16, deservedly equalised eight minutes before the interval. Camara sidestepped Johan Mjallby's challenge and drilled the ball low into the bottom corner to send the rhythmic rattle of the drummers among Senegal's fans into ecstatic overdrive in the sweltering conditions at the Big Eye stadium in western Japan. Senegal also had a Pape Boupa Diop effort ruled out by a tight offside decision as Sweden, without injured midfielder Fredrik Ljungberg who failed a late fitness test, visibly wilted in the heat as the first half wore on. Sweden improved at the start of the second period and both Marcus Allback and Anders Svensson threatening to restore their advantage. Substitute Andreas Andersson also fired wastefully over for the Swedes after a good link up with Larsson, while Senegal's impressive El Hadji Diouf tested Sweden goalkeeper Magnus Hedman with a curling free kick. With 10 minutes to go, Sweden substitute Zlatan Ibrahimovic looked likely to break the deadlock, cutting inside two defenders and unleashing a powerful shot which was well saved. But with so much at stake and the hot conditions making it slow going for both sides, neither goalkeeper was overly bothered and golden goal extra time became inevitable. The worst defences of the World Cup... ever!. The definition of a true champion, it is said, is the capacity to come back and win whatever it is they've won already again and again. There are, meanwhile, those champions who have come back and made a real pig's ear of it. In 1934, Uruguay didn't even turn up to defend their title, probably to get the Italian hosts back for not taking part in the inaugural competition in South America. At least France, like the teams below, had the grace to turn up and bugger it up in person. Brazil 1966 They were the holders and favourites, but came to England with an in-between squad of players who had already seen plenty of glory at the previous two finals (four of their starting line-up were over 34), and a handful whose time would come four years later (Jairzinho, Tostao, Edu). Pele was still El Rey, though, and scored the first in their opening 2-0 win over Bulgaria, but he got a taste of the brutal man-marking which saw to his and, so it is remembered, Brazil's participation. He was rested in the next game against Hungary, but one wonders whether he would have made much difference as the Magyars, with echoes of their great 50s team, took the South Americans apart. There were shades of Zizou's limping return as Pele was brought back for the crucial last group game with Portugal, but the Portuguese were far less hospitable than the Danes today. Pele was kicked about inelegantly all afternoon, and finally left the field with a coat round his shoulders, vowing never to play in the World Cup again. Luckily for football history, he was just in a huff. Brazil 1974 Having won the Jules Rimet trophy outright in 1970, the Brazillians famously had it nicked before they had a chance to start their bid for the new bit of goldware we know and love today. The thieves who did away with the cup showed a cynical lack of appreciation for the beautiful game which, nonetheless, characterised Brazil's approach to their 1974 defence. Coach Mario Zagallo felt that the skills and showmanship exhibited by his 1970 side would never survive against the physical game of the strongest Europeans. Instead he sought to match them with an athletic but far from watchable side with really bad hair, even for the time. They became the only Brazilian side not to outclass Scotland in the finals with a 0-0 draw to follow up another against Yugoslavia in the first round, and only progressed from there thanks to beating mighty Zaire. They won untidily against Argentina before facing Holland, handing them their reputation for the most finely choreographed football, only for the Dutch to go and forget to win anything with it. Damn. West Germany 1978 The holders got off to an iffy start with a goalless draw with Poland on a potato patch in Buenos Aires, but it was hard to tell which of their 6-0 win over Mexico and another dreary 0-0 with Tunisia were the real West Germany. World Cup diary. · Giovanni Trapattoni was furious that his Italy team were eliminated, especially after an offside flag denied Damiano Tommasi what would have been a golden-goal winner. "These linesmen are incapable," he fumed. However, those with a historical bent will recall that the last two times Italy won the World Cup they were waved on their way by obliging linesmen. In the first round in 1938 the Italians were on the rack for virtually 90 minutes with Norway's Arne Brustad seeing a goal disallowed for offside. Hamann injury doubt for Germany. Germany midfielder Dietmar Hamann remains a doubtful starter for tomorrow's World Cup semi-final against South Korea because of a knee injury, coach Rudi Voeller has said. "He was able to train lightly yesterday (Sunday) but a final decision on whether he will play will not be made until shortly before the game," Voeller said of Hamann, who twisted a ligament on his right knee in Friday's 1-0 quarter-final win over the United States. "He is extremely important to the team and it would be a big blow if he couldn't make it but he will only play if he's 100 percent fit," the Germany coach added just before the triple world champions left the holiday island of Cheju to fly to Seoul, where they will meet the co-hosts for a place in the final. The 28-year-old Hamann has started all five of Germany's matches in the tournament. If he does not recover in time, Voeller will probably hand his defensive central midfield role to Jens Jeremies. Is there a World Cup conspiracy?. Our mailbag is heaving. Talk of conspiracy hangs heavy in the air. Some say the World Cup is fixed. Others, that certain match officials have been 'got at'. And one enterprising soul even reckons that if Fifa lob him some readies, he'll give them the solution to their problems. Truly, today, we've heard it all. But enough frippery - to the conspiracy talk. "I am one the many Italians who support the 'conspiracy' theory," writes Luciano Zamponi, immediately raising eyebrows on the sports desk with his implication that there might be Italians who do not support it. "In Italy we believe that referees can make mistakes, like anyone else. "At the same time, even statistically, these mistakes SHOULD NOT HAVE ALWAYS GONE IN ONE DIRECTION!" Ooh, angry capital letters. That's told us. Luciano goes on to cite - among what he sees as other aberrations - Italy's five disallowed goals, along with the controversial dismissal of Francesco Totti as proof positive that something is indeed rotten in the states of Japan and, particularly, South Korea. "I predict a Brazil vs Germany final, with Brazil the winner," he concludes. "Adidas and Nike, who sponsor Germany and Brazil will be very happy. Brazil will win and sport will lose." One man who's infinitely more tactful in the conspiracy-mongering stakes is Björn Sveinsson: "Spain were robbed, plain and simple. I wonder how much the Koreans had pay for the results against Italy and Spain?" he enquires, before suggesting the notion of a conspiracy will always be credible when applied to organisations led by leaders with the public image of people "people like" Sepp Blatter. Meanwhile Iain Mclatchey reckons that all this conspiracy talk is nothing new. "Have the Spanish conveniently forgotten the blatant 'favouritism' shown by referees towards their team when they were hosts of the World Cup in 1982?" he asks. "Everything possible was done to get their team through the group stage, They were not complaining then were they?" They certainly were not, Iain. However not everyone agrees that there is a conspiracy. Andy Best reckons the reaction in Spain and Italy "stinks of bad tempers and sore losers more than pay outs and back handers," he explains before shrewdly observing that "the biggest complainers are those traditionally associated with cheating themselves." Mani Sancho-Ortiz is of a similar mind. Or is he? We can't really tell: "Conspiracy? Probably not," he declares, before giving what seems to be a damn good evidence that such chicanery has taken place before: "That businessmen in the Far East have been involved in bribing match-officials in the past is well-documented. Without a doubt what has happened has been bad for football. We should all be sad about that." Now, here's one for the books - an Italian who thinks the Azzurri have nobody to blame for their humiliating exit but themselves. "I think we deserved to leave the World Cup. The referee's mistakes? It's not his fault i

Leader: England win World Cup. Oh well: it was great while it lasted. A team, once in danger of not even qualifying, producing - at best - performances far above expectations; the flags in the streets and the mood of gregarious celebration; England football supporters behaving themselves, and the Japanese taking not just the England team but the England fans to their hearts; many supporters of Scotland and Wales swallowing their old alienation and coming to do that too; that very occasional smile on the inscrutable face of Sven-Goran Eriksson; Alan Hansen saying "awesome", and Rio Ferdinand, David Seaman and others truly deserving it. And even great former stars of Brazil like Pele conceding that England might very well win, since though the Brazilians had flair, England had something still better: resolute organisation. And then, a moment or two before half time, the script began to unravel. Flair came into its own. The team that could not defend, defended. That fear of failure, of letting the country down, which the England coach had identified when he took over as England's most serious weakness, fatally reasserted itself. Just as England had done best in a game - against Argentina - where defeat would have brought no feeling of shame, so it was again yesterday. The team's self-belief was so much greater against 11 Brazilians than it was when expectations of victory should have been highest, against only 10. "It's over now," Gary Lineker, who has tasted such disappointments himself, said lugubriously at the end. But it isn't over, by any means. England may have departed, the flags may have gone back in the cupboard, but the great international festival which has caught world attention like none before it is still very much on. Unpredicted successes like Senegal and South Korea are still in there with a chance, as well as Brazil. That alone is a reason for swallowing disappointment and staying with it, right through to the final whistle. Well, nearly; and it's not over yet.

Italy's Collina to referee World Cup final. Italian referee Pierluigi Collina will take charge of the World Cup final between Brazil and Germany on Sunday. Collina was described by David Beckham as the "best referee in the world" before officiating in England's group match against Argentina earlier in the tournament. Ronaldo, who has been dogged by injury, grabbed the vital goal in Saitama in the 49th minute. It now means that Brazil, who last won the trophy in 1994, will face Germany in the final on Sunday. Runners-up to France four years ago, the South Americans dominated the game after almost going behind in the 19th minute. Goalkeeper Marcos did well to push away a header from Aston Villa defender Alpay after a fine cross from the right by Fatih Akyel. Play switched down the other end and Brazil came close to scoring twice in two minutes. Cafu was released by Ronaldo, only to see his shot pushed over the top by Rustu. Then Roberto Carlos skipped away from Akyel but sent his effort into the side netting. The four-times World Cup winners kept up the pressure and Rivaldo tested Rustu twice in quick succession. Turkey were being pinned in their own half but the pacey Akyel broke away to pick out Hakan Sukur. However Cafu read the situation superbly to intercept. Rivaldo was seeing plenty of the ball but in the first half Rustu was equal to it, tipping away a shot from 20 yards. The Barcelona striker then dragged a shot wide after being set up by Edilson. Ronaldo stunned Turkey four minutes after the break after being released by Gilberto Silva, who had surged down the left. Ronaldo rode one challenge before toe-poking the ball into the corner, although Rustu got a hand to it. It was his sixth goal in the tournament - more than anyone else - and put Brazil in the driving seat. Leicester midfielder Muzzy Izzet made his first appearance in the finals when he replaced Umit Davala in the 74th minute. Turkey stepped up the pace and Hakan Sukur saw his clever close range shot turned away superbly by Marcos. They couldn't get that vital strike and Brazil, who struggled to qualify from the South American group, clinched a place in the final. Ballack to miss opener. Germany's captain Michael Ballack is in danger of missing the World Cup curtainraiser against Costa Rica tomorrow after failing to complete a training session yesterday afternoon. He has been struggling to get over a strain to his right calf since last Friday's friendly against Colombia and, although he returned to training yesterday, the German football association said the muscle was not strong enough for him to continue. "The medical department will continue to work on the injury intensively over the coming hours," the DFB said. Ballack missed the final of the last World Cup through suspension and the German media are so concerned about his prospects they have started referring to the injury as "the country's calf muscle". The absence of the 29-year-old would be keenly felt by Jürgen Klinsmann's relatively young team, particularly if he were also to miss the group games against Poland and Ecuador. "You can replace a player like him for one game," said the goalkeeper Jens Lehmann. "It would be terrible if he was out for two or three games. It would be tragic." Ballack, who joins Chelsea from Bayern Munich next season, was Germany's best outfield player four years ago, leading an unfancied team all the way to the final, although he was unable to play after committing a professional foul in the semifinal against South Korea, preventing a clear chance but earning a yellow card and a suspension. His importance has only increased and his record now stands at 31 goals in 65 appearances for his country, a remarkable rate for a midfielder. World Cup 2006: Snow falls on England's camp. The following correction was printed in the Guardian's Corrections and clarifications column, Tuesday June 6 2006 In our report below, we placed Bühlerhöhe, the England football team's base for the World Cup, in Bavaria. Bühlerhöhe is actually in Baden-Württemberg. England's players are likely to face many tricky challenges over the next month. But one thing they would not have anticipated was the snow that fell on Thursday at the Bavarian mountain-top hotel where the squad will be based for the World Cup. The 55-strong party of players, support staff and officials are due to check in to the baroque Schlosshotel in Bühlerhöhe on Monday evening. Staff confirmed that there had been a flurry on Thursday afternoon, after the coldest June 1 in Germany for decades. "It was snowing during the day. It only lasted a couple of minutes, but the English are used to bad weather, aren't they?" the spokeswoman for the hotel said yesterday. Olaf Pels-Leusden of the Deutscher Wetterdienst (German weather service) estimated temperatures on the mountain 900 metres above Baden-Baden - the Black Forest spa town where the England players' wives and girlfriends will be based - reached just about 3C. The hotel was selected by Sven-Goran Eriksson in December from a long list of 25 assessed as options by the Football Association. He felt that the medical centre was "one of the best in Europe" and made it "by far the best" choice. It is not clear whether the chilly snap will affect England's pre-tournament plans. Although warmer temperatures are expected next week, the squad's training HQ is also high in the mountains in the town of Bühlertal, a 20-minute drive away from the hotel down winding, extremely narrow roads. Across Germany yesterday morning temperatures reached record lows, with some of the coldest temperatures ever recorded for June. However, forecasters are predicting sunshine for the opening match of the tournament between the hosts Germany and Costa Rica next Friday afternoon in Munich. The following correction was printed in the Guardian's Corrections and clarifications column, Tuesday June 6 2006 In our report below, we placed Bühlerhöhe, the England football team's base for the World Cup, in Bavaria. Bühlerhöhe is actually in Baden-Württemberg.

World Cup 2006: Carragher: England will be a contender. Depite the absence of Wayne Rooney for the group phase of the World Cup, Liverpool defender Jamie Carragher believes England will be among the serious contenders when the tournament begins next month. 'Everyone always mentions Brazil and Argentina. But it's like the Champions League, when something can always go against the big teams, whether it's a refereeing decision or a man getting sent off. In knockout football, it's one bad game and you're out. The game's about small details - there's not much to choose between the top teams at this level. 'It will all be very tight. I would think other teams would worry about playing us, because we play a different way. Look at howLiverpool played against AC Milan and how Middlesbrough have come back in Uefa Cup games this year. There are not many countries where teams can do that.' Carragher's appearance for the full 90 minutes in the B international against Belarus on Thursday was his sixty-second game of a season that began 11 months ago against TNS in a Champions League qualifier. Yet he believes the warm weather training in Portugal last month with England aided him and his team-mates. 'It's been a long season, but that's what you're in the game for. We want to play a full year, July to July, because that will mean we've been successful. I feel fine - we had the break in Portugal so there's no problem. And there are a lot of players are just coming back to fitness like Ashley Cole, Sol Campbell and Michael Owen.' The Newcastle United striker is confident he will recover full sharpness for Germany, following his long-term metatarsal injury. 'I know I can perform well when it comes to playing in front of thousands of people in a World Cup,' Owen said. 'Normally when you come off after your first game or two, you feel a bit stiff, but I was quite pleased with how I felt after the Belarus game. I'm looking forward to the next one and hopefully I can play a bit more. Goals are a part of my game and it would be great to score in one of these warm-up games.' Owen is 15 short of breaking Bobby Charlton's record 49 goals for England. 'I'm not thinking about it. My only thoughts are coming back from Germany having had a good tournament.' Further injury to Owen added to Rooney's lay-off would leave England's attacking options thin, with Theo Walcott the only other recognised striker in Sven-Goran Eriksson's squad who could support Peter Crouch. Carragher, though, believes that the 17-year-old, who has yet to feature for Arsenal, will be able if called upon. 'He did very well when he came on against Belarus. The opposition know nothing about him and he's got that great pace. It will be difficult for him to do what Michael did in 1998, but he's very similar. He's definitely not afraid.' Tuesday v Hungary. Eriksson: Rooney is injury free. Sven-Goran Eriksson today insisted Wayne Rooney is "injury free" and now just needs to obtain match fitness before he plays in the World Cup. However, Eriksson said today: "The good news from yesterday evening and today is Rooney has no more injury. Eriksson read out a prepared statement at this morning's press conference and then refused to answer any more questions on the subject from the assembled media. "Professor Wallace assured me this morning that he will be here when we think he is match fit," added Eriksson. "I'm prepared to listen to everyone and discuss with them about Wayne Rooney but the last say in this story is Rooney's and mine. World Cup 2006: Lennon gives Eriksson an option. Sven-Goran Eriksson will arrive at Old Trafford on Tuesday for England's match against Hungary conscious that his five-year reign as head coach has entered its critical, end-game phase. The World Cup is less than two weeks away and Eriksson's tenure will be judged on how England perform in Germany. At the crucial moment during quarter-finals against Brazil and Portugal in his two tournaments in charge, the 2002 World Cup and Euro 2004, Eriksson's tactical decisions failed to change the game and England crashed out. This time the Swede's major dilemma is how he deals with the loss of Wayne Rooney for at least the group stages in Germany. Having fielded a team of hopefuls and senior players needing match fitness in Thursday's B international against Belarus, the experimentation now stops. Eriksson has taken few of his 27 friendlies very seriously, but there is little point in making mass substitutions for the games against Hungary, on Tuesday, and Jamaica, on Saturday. These are the last two warm-up games before the real thing, England v Paraguay in Frankfurt on 10 June. 'I'm going to start the best XI and then see how things go,' he said. While the 2-1 loss to Belarus was disappointing, there was good news for Eriksson from the game when Michael Owen, Ashley Cole and Sol Campbell, all of whom have had long injury lay-offs, emerged unscathed. Owen's 61 minutes featured the header that rebounded off the bar to set up Jermaine Jenas's goal, albeit with the striker's run having started from an offside position. In Rooney's absence, the Newcastle man's injury-free night was of even greater relief to Eriksson and prompted him to be bullish. 'I shouldn't be surprised if Michael is 110 per cent against Paraguay,' said Eriksson. 'He's extremely focused on that.' The foot injury suffered in a collision with England's Tottenham goalkeeper Paul Robinson has allowed him just 29 minutes of competitive action since Boxing Day and the Swede was unconcerned that Owen looked off the pace. 'You know he's not playing for a long time,' he said, before claiming that 'he looks very sharp. Two more games and no, I'm not worried about Michael Owen. In the past he's been afraid to miss the World Cup and that's normal. Once it starts that's completely over.' The striker's probable partner against Hungary, Jamaica and Paraguay is Peter Crouch, who had a goal disallowed for offside (rightly) against Belarus. 'I think they can do well together,' Eriksson said, although he refused to confirm the Liverpool man will start on Tuesday. 'Could be,' he said. 'Let's wait.' He ruled out 4-5-1 and 4-3-3 unless Rooney is fit. The only options other than Crouch in Eriksson's preferred 4-4-2, or in the diamond formation that allows a holding midfielder against the stronger teams, are Steven Gerrard and Joe Cole. Neither the Liverpool skipper nor the Chelsea man has ever played further forward for England than their usual midfield positions.

It may look complicated, but this is the mathematical formula that promises to take the frustration, misjudgment and wasted bets out of predicting football results. The good news is that punters should now be able to win a fortune. Decision Technology, a firm of prediction experts who claim to be the best in the business, has invented a computer program that boasts a better record than any bookmaker, pundit or sports tipster. While most bookmakers rank England as second favourites behind Brazil to land football's greatest prize, Dectech ranks Sven-Goran Eriksson's side ninth in the list it has produced to estimate the chance each of the 32 teams has of lifting the trophy. Brazil have the highest probability at 13.1 per cent. In a statement that will not readily make the first line of a new football chant, Dr Henry Stott, firm director and visiting fellow at Warwick University, said: 'Our modelling technique involves maximum likelihood estimation and a kind of rational probabilistic analysis to predict what the outcome of a match will be.' The computer has studied the scorelines of 4,500 games between 200 countries since 2002 and come up with forecasts for every match at the initial group stage. 'England have an easy group and so an 80 per cent chance of reaching the knockout stages. After that the games against tough opposition such as Germany and Argentina come thick and fast. That's why we have made them only ninth favourites to win. With or without Wayne Rooney,' said Stott. If the system is as accurate as its inventors claim - it has correctly called 53 per cent of Premiership matches since 2002, better than anyone else - fans can stop relying on hunches, advice from friends and listening, for example, to newspaper tipsters, who score a measly 43 per cent. Stott says anyone seeking to make a profit on events in Germany should back France, Holland or the Czech Republic, whose chances, he says, have been underestimated. According to Stott and his colleagues, a combination of science and mathematics shows that, after Brazil, the next nearest favourites are France, who did not score a goal at the last World Cup, Germany, who even Germans admit are an unexceptional team, and Holland. World Cup special: part two. Which team would top a World Cup table that gives three points for a win and one for a draw, for all matches played at the finals over the years?" muses Luke Kelly. Well Luke, the straightforward answer to your question, as you could probably have guessed, is Brazil: holders, five-time winners and the only team to have qualified for all 17 tournaments so far. A dedicated tallying effort here tallied up 189 points for the South Americans (58 wins, 15 draws), giving them a 24-point lead over nearest rivals Germany (if you include West Germany's results). Our method, which counted games settled in extra-time or on penalties as draws and did include results from third and fourth play-off fixtures, had England ranked in at a perfectly respectable fifth place, and came up with the following top 10: 1 Brazil - 189 points (58 wins, 15 draws) 2 Germany (including West Germany) - 165 (48 wins, 21 draws) 3 Italy - 124 (34 wins, 22 draws) 4 Argentina - 98 (29 wins, 11 draws) 5 England - 76 (19 wins, 19 draws) 6 France - 69 (20 wins, 9 draws) 7 Spain - 67 (18 wins, 13 draws) 8 Sweden - 57 (15 wins, 12 draws) 9 Uruguay - 54 (14 wins, 12 draws) 10= Russia (including USSR) - 53 (15 wins, 8 draws) 10= Holland - 53 (14 wins, 11 draws) 10= Yugoslavia - 53 (15 wins, 8 draws) Also worth noting is Brazil's phenomenal positive goal difference of 109 over the tournament's history (191 scored and 82 conceded), which comfortably outstrips nearest rivals Germany by 47. Only Brazil, Germany (171), Italy (111) and Argentina (102) have scored more than 100 goals at World Cup finals, while England trail some way behind with just 68 goals for and 48 against. A complete World Cup wins and losses table can be seen here, though the results differ slightly from those shown above due to a different counting system used for drawn and replayed results. World Cup 2006: Beckham hopes for peace on field. David Beckham made what could easily be described as a schoolboy error this week. He started feeling a bit claustrophobic at England's hotel and decided to go for a walk around Manchester's designer stores. The mistake he made was not to go for the A-listers' traditional disguise of baseball cap, pulled low, and dark sunglasses. By the time he had got to King Street he had been mobbed. On Market Street Beckham suddenly had the strange sensation that his buttocks were being grabbed. Schoolgirls were pulling and prodding and squeezing and screaming. His beanie hat was pulled from his head and, finally, police were called to rescue him. It is a snapshot into the madness of Planet Beckham, the type of scene that has not been seen in the North-west since the days of Ringo, Paul, George and John. Beckham, of course, is accustomed to hysteria but even he seemed genuinely taken aback. "It still surprises me what happens when I go out, even now," he said yesterday, flashing those Tippex-ed teeth and showing off a diamond-encrusted watch the size of a saucer. "You turn up at a shop and suddenly there are 2,000 people going mad. It amazes me. It's the same in London as well. I went there a couple of weeks ago and I couldn't get out of the shop. It took six policemen to get me out." It might be that Beckham under-estimates the frenzy that accompanies the build-up to a World Cup but, then again, perhaps he was entitled to believe he could have an afternoon walk without it turning into a 999 ordeal. Beckham, after all, has had a pretty quiet time of it recently. While a nation's attention has been fixed to Wayne Rooney and Theo Walcott, the England captain has quietly prepared for his third World Cup. "I've been through tournaments where the spotlight has permanently been on me," he says. "This time it's not been there as much but the thing is, it's never changed the way I train or play anyway. As soon as I get on that football field I forget everything else. It's where I feel happiest. It's where I feel safest." He demonstrated as much in the game against Hungary, not just with his dead-ball expertise but because it was him, not Steven Gerrard, Frank Lampard or anyone else, who lifted England when it was threatening to become a flat performance. "There was a headline the other day that said I'd promised to have my best World Cup. Well, I don't think I can actually promise that, but I will promise to give everything in every game. I feel great, my fitness levels have never been better and I just want to get it started now. I'm determined to have a great World Cup." One is overdue given his sending-off against Argentina in 1998 and his diminished fitness four years ago. Beckham's unhappy record extends to Euro 2004, culminating in his botched penalty against Portugal, but it is a measure of the man that he has volunteered to be one of the takers should the same situation arise. Angola out for revenge against Portugal. Before boarding the team bus after training, one Angolan official was forthright. "This match will be taken by the people as a chance for a challenge, for revenge," he said, looking to tomorrow's game against Portugal. The sides' last meeting, in 2001, was abandoned 20 minutes from time with Angola having collected four red cards and losing 5-1. The only other head-to-head brought a 6-0 Portugal victory, yet when retribution is mentioned by Angolans, it is not just sport they have in mind. Angola lived under Portugal's colonial rule for almost 500 years until independence in 1975 and an element of bitterness is said to remain in a love-hate relationship. That may be unspoken by this squad and not universally shared, at the very least by the sizeable segment who grew up or earn a living in Portugal. But every player knows what is at stake in Cologne. Angola have a chance to humble their former masters before the eyes of the world, just as Senegal did memorably to France four years ago. The odds are stacked far more heavily against Angola, whose goalkeeper Joao Ricardo has spent the past year with no club after being released by Portugal's Moreirense, and whose outfield players are drawn from less glamorous teams than Senegal's were. Of the likely starting line-up, four played last season in Angola, three in Portugal's second division and one in Kuwait. Even the captain Fabrice Akwa, who counts Benfica among his former clubs, has not played competitively since February when his Qatar contract was cancelled for disciplinary reasons. Angola's motivation could scarcely be higher, though, and they will recall how Portugal lost their opening match of the last World Cup to the United States and went out in the first round. The defender Marco Abreu, one of five players from Portuguese clubs outside the top division, summed up the emotions. "Even in Portugal they have a lot of Angolan people and they are saying to me: 'When you go to the World Cup I hope you beat Portugal. You can lose all the other games but you have to win against Portugal.'" Eyes hidden behind sunglasses, he dismissed as irrelevant the two previous mismatches and the indiscipline that brought Angola red cards for bad tackles and dissent five years ago. "The players will be more concentrated," he said. "We are ambitious and have a lot of young players and if they want a career in Europe, this is the game." Poor warm-up results and failure at this year's African Nations Cup do not bode well but Akwa insists a shock is possible. "They have the big names, good players," he said of Portugal, "but names never win a game." The softly spoken striker described Senegal as an inspiration and is attuned to feelings back home. "The players know the big responsibility we have and we hope we can give this big gift to our people." World Cup 2006: Ballack's challenge works. Michael Ballack publicly challenged Germany's leaky defence to keep a clean sheet and they immediately obliged against Colombia last night as Chelsea's new signing scored the headed goal that set them on the way to a 3-0 victory. The German coach Jürgen Klinsmann has experimented with 20 different defensive alignments over two years but seems to have found a back four to open the World Cup for them in Munich this Friday. Klinsmann showed support for Ballack's comments, though. "It's absolutely a good thing when the players express their opinions," he said, before expressing some sympathy for his defenders. "Our problem has not been the defence per se but the transition back to defence from up front and from midfield." The left-back Phillip Lahm returned to the team and, together with the right-back Arne Friedrich, helped stabilise the German rearguard after a weak effort during the 2-2 draw against Japan on Tuesday. Japan could have won emphatically had Arsenal's Jens Lehmann not played so well on that occasion and memories of losing 4-1 against Italy in March were fresh too. Per Mertesacker and Christoph Metzelder joined Lahm and Friedrich in the heart of defence. "We've faced a lot of criticism, some of which was justified," said Metzelder. Bastian Schweinsteiger seemed to benefit as well from the return of Lahm behind him on the left. Ballack's goal came after he outjumped two Colombia defenders to head in a Schweinsteiger free-kick from the left in the 21st minute and Schweinsteiger scored from long range 16 minutes later from another. Tim Borowski made it 3-0 in the 69th minute with one more strike from distance. Germany's forwards Miroslav Klose and Lukas Podolski wasted a number of chances, though, especially in the first half when Klose twice missed an empty net. After the game the players unfurled a sign to the home fans which read "Together with you, a dream: July 9 2006" - the date of the World Cup final. Germany 3-0 Colombia: Ballack's challenge for a tighter defence works as Germany sweep aside Colombia.

World Cup 2006: Italy draw with Ukraine. Italy are unbeaten in 18 matches after their 0-0 draw with Ukraine in Lausanne yesterday but the side lacked the inspiration that they showed in recent friendly wins over Holland and Germany. The Ukranians, who will be taking part in their first World Cup this month, concentrated on defence. Italy started with a three-prong attack of Alessandro Del Piero, Alberto Gilardino and Luca Toni but with half an hour left Marcello Lippi, their coach, sent on Francesco Totti and Filippo Inzaghi. Totti was making his third competitive appearance since injuring his ankle in February and lacked sharpness. "My players haven't played much competitive football since the Serie A season finished on May 14," said Lippi. "It's only natural they are going to take time to get into the game. As far as this evening goes, I am happy."
