My World Cup outsiders? Switzerland. "The Swiss are on a roll" is a line you're likely to get sick of hearing over the coming weeks. Unless, of course, you do what I did and stick a fiver on Kobi Kuhn's crew making it to the semi-finals. At 16/1, Switzerland are great value. 1990 was the only one of the last five World Cups where the semi-finals featured four of the game's big guns (Germany, Argentina, Italy and England). In 1982, Poland made it to the last quartet. In 1986, Belgium crept in. At USA 94, Sweden and Bulgaria contested the third place play-off and in France 98, Croatia were the surprise package. Four years ago in Asia, Turkey and South Korea nearly went all the way. With the exception of Poland and Bulgaria, none of those giant-killers had stand-out stars: rather, they were well-drilled collectives of tidy, tough and tricky players who gathered momentum as they progressed. Switzerland fit that bill perfectly. Their deftness of touch and thought is central to their style of play, which is all about retaining possession, not in ponderous Italian fashion but as part of zippy, slick moves that probe quickly and directly. Their fluidity and flair made them top scorers (and unbeaten) in a tight qualifying group, and they showed they had steel to go with their style by surviving a literally riotous play-off with Turkey. They may not have made much impression at Euro 2004 but the fact is Switzerland have been building up to something big for the last four years. The squad features some solid old stagers such as captain Johan Vogel and Hamburg enforcer Raphael Wicky but the bulk of it is made up of gifted youngsters who first hinted at a gilded destiny in 2002. That year Switzerland won the U-17 European championship with Philippe Senderos as captain, and came third in the U-21 tournament, where the excellent Alexander Frei was top scorer. Since graduating to the senior team, Frei has hit 23 goals in 43 matches (he's 150/1 to pick up the golden boot in Germany - well worth another fiver). The draw has smiled on the Swiss. They should demolish Togo and are more lively than the predictable South Korea. Which only leaves France, a fading force who can't get the best out of Thierry Henry and have a midfield of jaded veterans who won't admit their past it. Count on the Swiss to prove it to them - they almost did so in the qualifers, when the French were fortunate to escape with draws from both their meetings. Indeed, in the run-up to their latest encounter, oddball French boss Raymond Domenech has taken the characteristically unusual step of admitting: "the Swiss game gives me nightmares - they know how to beat us." In the second round, Switzerland will likely face Spain, Ukraine or Tunisia, all of whom are more beatable than insolent eggs. Brazil or Italy could await in the quarters - and that's when the young Swiss will seal their new stardom. And you'll collect a handsome profit on your fiver. Pfister's Togo turnaround. Two days after resigning as manager of Togo, Otto Pfister has announced that he's changed his mind and will take charge of the Hawks' World Cup opener against South Korea tomorrow after all. The German had quit after complaining about the long-running dispute between players and officials over bonsuses, but today explained he had been persuaded to return. However, he declined to specify whether it was just for one match or whether, as widely reported, former Cameroon manager Winfried Schäfer will take the reins for the remainder of the tournament. "The players intervened massively," said Pfister today. "I received a fax from the Togolese football association president and I will sit on the bench as coach of the team tomorrow." Pfister was only appointed manager three months ago after Stephen Keshi, who had guided the team to their first ever World Cup qualification, was sacked following a row with star player Emmanuel Adebayor during Togo's dismal African Cup of Nations campaign in February. All change in the Togo camp. Togo assistant coach Kodjovi Mawuena has taken over first-team duties after Otto Pfister stormed out of the team's hotel in Wangen due to a pay dispute between officials and the squad. Spokesman Messan Attolou said: "Mr Mawuena is the new coach and he is already conducting the training with the team this morning." According to the team physio, Joachim Schubert, 68-year-old Pfister and his assistant Piet Hamberg walked away from the Africans' camp last night over the ongoing dispute between the players and the national football federation over bonuses. The players have asked for £80,000 each to play in the World Cup, plus an additional £20,000 each per win and £10,000 per draw. "Otto Pfister left the hotel with assistant coach Piet Hamberg at 22.20 (on Friday)," Schubert told Kicker . "It's a shame that it's all fallen to pieces because Togo and Pfister had a good relationship, but the situation had become unbearable for the coach." Pfister explained: "I have resigned without notice. The players boycotted (three closed) training sessions and with that my basis to work has been removed. It has destroyed a dream of my life." He told Tagesspiegel: "The players are angry, unmotivated. They are striking - and we are supposed to play a successful World Cup?...I cannot do my job professionally if the federation does not even begin to work professionally." Togo prime minister Edem Kodjo flew to Germany late on Friday night in a bid to resolve the dispute, which is threatening to overshadow the nation's first appearance in the World Cup. Kodjovi Mawuena has taken over as Togo coach after Otto Pfister did one on the eve of their first World Cup match.

Brazil legend sees frailties in class of 2006. They are the most sublime team on earth, boast some magical talents, and are everyone's favourites to win the World Cup. But some of the stars who have helped Brazil win the trophy five times before believe Ronaldinho and his teammates are overrated, have a suspect defence and will face tougher challenges in Germany than most people expect. 'The team have an incredible potential,' said Tostao, who helped Brazil win their third World Cup in 1970 in Mexico. 'But right now, they're not that much better than the other teams. It wouldn't be a surprise if they were eliminated.' 'I still see some things that need to be adjusted, especially in the defensive sector,' added Tostao, who is now a columnist for Brazilian newspapers. He used to partner Pele in Brazil's attack, and scored three goals at the 1966 and 1970 World Cups. 'The differential for this team is that they have three spectacular players in Ronaldinho, Kaka and Ronaldo,' Tostao said. 'But if they have bad days, the rest of the team are normal - the same standard as the other teams.' Falcao, a star at the 1982 and 1986 World Cups, also agreed Brazil still have to improve before being labelled 'better than the others'. 'Brazil still haven't been able to put a team together,' Falcao said. 'They are working towards that goal and there are still ten days to do it.' Falcao, a former midfielder for Brazil's Internacional and Italy's AS Roma, said Brazil will have to work hard to play up to expectations. 'Our goal will be to improve a little bit more,' Parreira said. 'New Zealand have been playing a lot recently and certainly will be a tougher test for us.' Brazil romped to an 8-0 victory last Tuesday against FC Lucerne, who have just returned to the Swiss First Division and hardly posed a stern test for the five-time world champions. Atmosphere heats up in Sweden camp. Sweden officials have played down the confrontation between Olof Mellberg and Freddy Ljungberg following the goalless draw with Trinidad & Tobago. The duo were involved in an altercation in the Sweden dressing room minutes after the final whistle on Saturday, a scene which recalled the pair's training-ground bust-up during the 2002 World Cup. That incident saw the players grappling on the ground after Ljungberg was floored by a hefty challenge from Mellberg. However, a spokesman played down the significance of the latest incident: "It was not a fight, but a short, but hot, dispute between some players. Two of those players were Mellberg and Ljungberg. It was a typical dressing-room situation, nothing serious." Sweden have come under fire from both their fans and the Swedish media following the shock draw with Trinidad & Tobago. Lars Lagerback's team failed to impress in their World Cup warm-up games and that form continued into the tournament last weekend. Their hopes of reaching the knockout stages took a serious blow with the result and it appears nothing less than a victory will suffice in their second group game, against Paraguay on Thursday. Their final match in the pool is against England on June 20. One of the biggest criticisms levelled at Lagerback has been his decision to opt for Anders Svensson in the centre of midfield ahead of playmaker Kim Kallstrom. Svensson had a poor game on Saturday, and was replaced by Marcus Allback in the second half. Kallstrom, who came off the bench with 11 minutes remaining, impressed in the little time he had. Both the press and the fans want Lagerback to go with Kallstrom for the Paraguay clash. The coach, however, is leaving his options open: "It was not individual mistakes that lay behind the draw," he said. "We played well but we didn't have any luck. We are all looking forward to the game with Paraguay, either with Svensson or Kallstrom - or maybe both - from the start." Svensson, the former Southampton midfielder who now plays for Elfsborg, does not know where he stands in Lagerback's thinking. "It's frustrating not to know from game to game if you have the coach's trust," he said. "After the game against Trinidad & Tobago, it seemed like it was my fault that we didn't win. At the moment, I don't feel very well." Olof Mellberg and Freddy Ljungberg picked up where they left off in 2002 and engaged in a spot of fisticuffs after Saturday's draw with Trinidad & Tobago.

Chris Birchall: Four years ago I was down the pub watching England. Chris Birchall has experienced World Cup fever before. Four years ago he squeezed into the Crown and Anchor pub in Stone in Staffordshire and, with his local swollen to bursting point, rejoiced as David Beckham deflated Argentina, then winced as Ronaldinho's cross-shot arced cruelly over David Seaman. "It was heaving, but the whole town had been buzzing and getting behind England," he recalled. "I can imagine what it'll be like this summer, too, but they've got another team to support down there too this time." Birchall, a regular for Port Vale in League One these days, will face up to Steven Gerrard and Frank Lampard in Nuremberg attempting to undermine England's challenge and propel Trinidad & Tobago to one of the greatest World Cup upsets. More used to scuttling up the flank at Vale Park, the 22-year-old will busy himself in central midfield on Thursday attempting to stifle his idols. He might just catch his breath to pinch himself. The Soca Warriors have already caught the imagination, Saturday's spirited goalless draw with Sweden applauded by the England camp as it was celebrated deliriously back in the Caribbean, and more than 3,000 locals have taken to watching the squad train in the tiny town of Rotenburg, just east of Bremen, with the players pursued by squeals of teenage girls. The high-pitched clamour has surrounded Birchall more than most. The first white player to feature for the Caribbean side in 60 years, he qualified because his mother was born in Port of Spain. "If you'd told me four years ago, back when I was just breaking into the Vale set-up, that I'd be playing in the next World Cup I'd have assumed it would have been for England, perhaps after playing well for Vale and earning myself a move," he said. "Actually, I wouldn't have believed you. But the last 12 months have been amazing. "It all started towards the end of the year before last when my agent told the Trinidad and Tobago Football Federation I was eligible. Vale played Wrexham towards the end of that season, the ball went out for a throw-in and Dennis Lawrence came charging up to me. When a 6ft 7in opponent comes at you, normally the first thing you think is 'Uh-oh, what have I done wrong here? Did I foul him earlier or something?' But he just asked whether I had any Trini blood in me and wanted to have a chat after the game. A few weeks later I was on the plane. "I'd never even been to Trinidad before. There was a big crowd for my first game - I was a bit of a novelty, the first white player for so long - and they wanted to see how good I was, wondering: 'Who is this guy playing for us who's never been here before, and a white lad too?' But it's not an issue any more. I've taken to the country and they've taken to me. I'm proud to be wearing the shirt and I'm as patriotic as anyone else in the team." World Cup 2006: Richard Williams on England's experience. This, we have so often been told, is England's golden generation. Never before has the country assembled such a gifted group of footballers, whose presence in a single squad represents a phenomenon that may never be repeated. A sceptic would suggest that the word "gifted" might be replaced by "highly paid" or even "over-publicised" but that is hardly the players' fault. And whatever they may lack as they attempt to live up to their billing and satisfy a nation's expectations, it will not be experience of major tournaments. Take Owen Hargreaves, for instance. In the public's mind the Bayern Munich player retains the status of a rather dodgy experiment. But in the last week of May 2002 he started as the defensive midfield player in England's two warm-up matches against South Korea and Cameroon, lasting 90 minutes in both games. Sven-Goran Eriksson may be willing to take a chance on including a handful of untested teenagers in a squad whose efforts will ultimately determine history's judgment on his tenure as England's head coach but in other areas he has shown a notable, and sometimes contentious, love of stability. As a result Hargreaves is surrounded by familiar faces. Eriksson's first gift to the squad was continuity, and the 23-strong party that embarks for Germany on Monday will contain eight other men who were present at the last World Cup finals: David Beckham, Michael Owen, Rio Ferdinand, Ashley Cole, Sol Campbell, Wayne Bridge, Joe Cole and David James. It can reasonably be expected that those players will share with more recent arrivals a core knowledge of what a World Cup campaign entails. Missing from the 2002 party, and much regretted by Eriksson in their enforced absence, were Gary Neville and Steven Gerrard. Both returned to the squad in time for Euro 2004, and joining them in Portugal were five other members of this weekend's group: Frank Lampard, Paul Robinson, John Terry, Jamie Carragher and Wayne Rooney. Absent, thanks to the ban imposed for his missed dope test, was Ferdinand. And so once again it is possible to discern the steady growth of a squad that has as near to a feeling of permanence as any group of footballers could ever possess. In the view of some critics Eriksson has over-indulged the more famous names, and one man in particular. Beckham is far from the ideal choice to captain the side on the pitch, although he commands the respect of his team-mates. Purely in playing terms, however, his contribution to Tuesday's victory over Hungary confirmed the opinion of those who have paid attention to his performances with Real Madrid, which is that his right foot remains a weapon that any team in the world would covet, particularly now that he seems to have grown out of the urge to direct the match from the centre of midfield. Few would dispute the presence of the remaining old-stagers. Even Owen, who (like Beckham, Neville, Ferdinand and Campbell) goes all the way back

Czech Republic 3 - 0 USA. Czech Republic fans will not have been alone in raucously celebrating the two excellent goals Tomas Rosicky scored yesterday. A great deal of pleasure will also have been taken in parts of north London at his contribution. The midfielder was signed by Arsenal from Borussia Dortmund for £6.8m last month and here was a clear indication of why Arsène Wenger paid that money as the Czechs gave a statement of intent in this World Cup. Rosicky may have been quiet for long periods of this impressive victory, and some of his intended through-passes may have failed to find their targets, but three thrilling moments in particular underlined his ability. The swerving long-range shot with which he put his country 2-0 ahead and the coolly taken finish he added in the second half sandwiched an effort struck from outside the box which dipped on to the bar. The Premiership should look forward to seeing such talent next season, and has Michael Ballack and Andriy Shevchenko arriving as well. Rosicky received resounding applause from the neutrals as well as Czech fans when he was substituted in the closing minutes, having provided periodic thrusts to which a vulnerable, outclassed US team could offer no answer. "I would like to congratulate Arsenal for buying such a wonderful player," the Czech coach Karel Bruckner said. Rosicky is capable of sharper distribution but it would be ridiculous to pull him up for that in a Czech display reminiscent of their progress to the Euro 2004 semi-final, a run they hope to at least match here. This part of Germany has been Rosicky's home for the five years he spent at Dortmund and he gave a reminder to the locals of what he can offer. The 25-year-old said that sealing his move to England had been beneficial to his performance. "I think that the transfer to Arsenal did help me because I really feel relaxed that I don't have to think about the future too much," he said. His first goal was all the more significant for coming eight minutes after Claudio Reyna, the one US player to emerge with any credit, struck a post in pursuit of an equaliser. Bruce Arena's players were comfortably dominating possession at that point but Reyna's effort was a lone threat from a team who lacked a cutting edge. The Czech goals showed how brutally they can pick off opponents. There was a further boost for Bruckner last night when tests showed a hamstring problem suffered by Jan Koller was not serious. The centre-forward departed on a stretcher just before half-time and the initial prognosis had been that he would take no further part in the tournament. The towering striker is vital in linking his team's play and as a goalscorer, and he had put the Czechs ahead. He was hardly missed here but his replacement, Vratislav Lokvenc, missed a presentable chance. Portugal. WORLD RANKING: 7 ODDS: 25-1 WORLD CUP RECORD? With the exception of 1966, when a Eusebio-inspired team knocked out Brazil en route to a semi-final defeat by England, the Portuguese are perennial underachievers, undermined by abject inconsistency. In 2002, they went out at the group stage, thumping Poland 4-0 but losing to South Korea and the USA. FINEST HOUR? In 1966 Portugal wowed England with the best football of the tournament. Their finest hour came, literally, in the quarter-final against North Korea when, 3-0 down after just 25 minutes, a rampant Eusebio scored four to lead a 5-3 comeback win. WHO'S THE GAFFER? Luis Felipe Scolari, the man who famously quit the England job before he had even taken it in April. Big Phil may have handled the intense pressure that goes with coaching Brazil, but the thought of waking up to a gang of English hacks every morning was just too much. Even the reported £5m a year didn't tempt him. What should not be in doubt are Scolari's credentials. After masterminding Brazil's 2002 World Cup success he guided Portugal to the final of Euro 2004 and in both tournaments he out-thought Sven-Goran Eriksson and eliminated England at the quarter-final stage. Scolari has mastered the art of incorporating flair individuals into teams with a rock-solid work ethic and has achieved the same with the current Portugal side. GOLDEN BOOT? Pauleta is Portugal's record scorer and a prolific striker for Paris St-Germain. He has been criticised for bottling it in major tournaments but goes to Germany off the back of an excellent domestic season and 11 goals in qualifying. LOOSE CANNON? When facing defeat, self-discipline disappears quite dramatically. At the European Championship in 2000, protests after a late disputed penalty in the semi-final against France saw three players - Nuno Gomes, Abel Xavier and Paulo Bento - receive cumulative bans of two years after the referee was 'pushed and intimidated'. Two years later Joao Pinto went even further, punching Argentinian referee Angel Sanchez after he sent him off against South Korea. Of the miscreant foursome, only Nuno Gomes is still involved. WEAKEST LINK? Portugal are solid defensively but lack a genuinely world-class frontman. If Pauleta hits form they could go all the way, but if he doesn't they will look good without actually punishing teams. UNLIKELY HERO? Goalkeeper Ricardo is better known for his surges off his goalline and dodgy positioning than his safe hands, but at Euro 2004 he was the toast of the country after the shootout victory against England. MOST LIKELY TO BE IN 'HELLO!'? As if film-star looks, a genius for football and a personal fortune of some £50m weren't enough, Luis Figo has a Swedish supermodel wife, Helen Svedin. The sod. World Cup 2006: Kewell backs Aussie teamwork. Harry Kewell believes teamwork and collective responsibility will provide Brazil with a unique Australian challenge in Munich tomorrow and is confident that defeat is not the Socceroos' only option. "We are playing football and it is a team game," Kewell said. "It is not an individual game. At moments there is individual brilliance but we're capable of going out there together, doing our job and making Australia proud. That is the Australian way. There is no point in wasting energy." The Liverpool winger said Australia would treat Brazil no differently to any other opponent despite worries over picking up suspensions for a potentially decisive final group fixture against Croatia. "You're not going to go into a game thinking you'll lose. You are going to go into a game thinking you can win it. I couldn't care what the Brazilians think of us. Not too many other people think much about us, so why would Brazil be any different? "Every game is the most important of your life. The next game is the most important. It doesn't matter who you play. Playing Brazil is no different to any other team, whether it is Liechtenstein or Japan or England or Argentina. You go in with the same mind." Mark Viduka echoed Kewell's relaxed confidence and suggested that, rather than a policy of containment, Australia could surprise Brazil by attacking the South Americans from the whistle. "I think we have to go for it," Viduka said. "Every game is a crunch game at this World Cup. You saw against Japan that it was end-to-end stuff, it is not like a league. You have to go for it in this tournament and that means Australia has to go for it. Brazil are not superheroes. They are people like us. So why not?" With all his players fit, Guus Hiddink's only selection conundrum is how to juggle the ones on yellow cards. Tim Cahill, John Aloisi, Vince Grella and Craig Moore all collected cautions against Japan and the Australia coach wants to ensure his team faces Croatia next week with all the key personnel available. "The yellow cards are a bit of a concern for us because a few of them were given out quite lightly against Japan," Viduka said. Sven-Goran Eriksson may consider playing David Beckham as an attacking right-back in England's final group game against Sweden after being impressed with the relative success of the captain's redeployment in a more defensive role. Beckham was shifted to the right side of England's defence to accommodate Aaron Lennon against Trinidad & Tobago as Eriksson sought a way of breathing life into a laboured display. Lennon's pace discomfited T&T in Nuremberg, and with England dominating possession Beckham was able to take up attacking positions similar to those he assumed earlier in the more rigid 4-4-2. It was from just such a position that Beckham provided Peter Crouch with the opener. The move is tempting to Eriksson in the absence of Gary Neville because it allows the coach to keep his captain in the side and incorporate Lennon's match-turning abilities. Jamie Carragher, Neville's replacement on Thursday, is dependable but as a converted centre-half does not offer the same overlapping qualities as the Manchester United player. Neville is likely to be missing again against Sweden and it remains to be seen if England work on the formation when training resumes today. It would be a bold move and one likely to attract criticism because of the coach's apparently slavish devotion to keeping Beckham in the side. His performances thus far do not justify omission however - in the absence of fluency from the midfield quartet the captain's right foot has been the likeliest source of goals. Sven-Goran Eriksson was so impressed by Aaron Lennon that he may play Beckham in defence in Gary Neville's absence.

World Cup 2006: Well behaved fans are rewarded. German and British police expressed satisfaction yesterday at the behaviour of the 60,000 English fans who gathered in this city for England's second World Cup match. Despite the number of supporters there were only 28 arrests, most for low-level offences. Assistant chief constable Stephen Thomas, gold commander of the British policing operation in Germany, will spend the weekend liaising with the police authorities in Cologne, the venue for England's third group match against Sweden on Tuesday night. The city council has already reassessed its plans because of the size of England's travelling support. The prediction had been that 100,000 fans would be in Germany at some point in the tournament but that now seems a huge underestimate - 70,000 were in Frankfurt and only 10,000 fewer in Nuremberg. A new public viewing arena with big screens will be built purely for England fans in Cologne, with a capacity of 30,000. About half of the 35,000 saleable seats in the stadium are likely to be occupied by England fans and there is another official giant Fan Fest park in the city. Of the 28 arrests in Nuremberg, 16 were at the stadium, some for climbing over security fences. In the city centre there were 12 arrests, including six for assaults, one for being drunk, two for touting tickets and one for selling forged tickets. Most were released and none is likely to face proceedings in Germany. Although England's noisy celebrations went on past 2am the city was quieter than Frankfurt had been and singing was confined to football songs - mainly We're Not Going Home" - rather than the anti-German war songs favoured in Frankfurt. Bars in the centre began emptying after 10.30pm as fans caught trains back to Frankfurt, where many are based, and the day-trippers took chartered flights home. England's fans have been praised by Wolfgang Niersbach, vice-president of the German organising committee, who said their behaviour was one of the highlights of the World Cup so far. "By our estimates there were 60,000 or 70,000 English fans in Nuremberg for the match against Trinidad & Tobago and their exemplary behaviour was being discussed by many people last night," he said. "We would like to say we have tremendous praise for the way the English fans are behaving." Both the German and British police say the England fans behaved themselves after Thursday's game.

World Cup 2006: Crespo hails 'the most beautiful goal'. Hernán Crespo last night described Argentina's second goal as the most beautiful he has ever been involved in after the thumping victory over Serbia & Montenegro. The move involved 24 passes and culminated in Crespo backheeling a return pass into the path of Esteban Cambiasso who fired past Dragoslav Jevric in the Serbian goal. "I thought at the time it was a beautiful goal," said the striker. "Now I want to go and watch it on the TV just to see how magical it really was. I have never been involved in a more beautiful goal than that. The result was easy because we played so well. Normally when you play at such a level it doesn't matter who the defence is. "I'm very happy with our form and everyone is playing very well. There are a few teams who can challenge Brazil and we are one of them. It's a great group of players and we have a lot of talent waiting to come off the bench." That was a reference to, among others, Lionel Messi, who made his comeback from a thigh injury by entering as a 74th-minute substitute for his first competitive game since March, and so became the youngest player to represent his country at a World Cup. In that short spell he showcased his talent. "I think Messi did a good job today," said the Argentinian midfielder Juan Román Riquelme. "I'm glad he didn't suffer pains, because we knew he had an injured leg. Every time he comes on to the pitch we enjoy watching him play." The coach José Pekerman added: "These few minutes on the pitch were a joy for all of us." Riquelme believes Argentina can get better still. They are likely to face Portugal, Mexico or Iran in the second round. "We are very happy with the match we played and we have the intention of improving further," he said. "I think we are now keeping a rather high level." The only downside for Argentina, who complete the group against Holland on Wednesday, was a muscle injury to the midfielder Luis González. For Serbia & Montenegro, positives were non-existent as they head towards the exit. "This is the worst result in the history of Yugoslavian football," said the coach Ilija Petkovic, "but I have to stand by the team. It's not the players' fault, it's my fault." Hernán Crespo says he can't stop watching Argentina's "magical" 24 pass goal.

World Cup 2006: US troops boost American tream. After a demoralising defeat in their opening game, the United States have had their flagging morale boosted by the support of American troops stationed in Ramstein, who have given them enthusiastic backing before tomorrow's crucial clash with Italy. The US must win at Kaiserslautern's Fritz-Walter-Stadion to give themselves a realistic chance of progressing to the next stage after their first match ended in a 3-0 defeat by the Czech Republic, and their captain Claudio Reyna said the troops have inspired them. "We had a great reception," the Manchester City midfielder said. "They are all very excited for us and it was very nice for us to be among Americans. "Seeing and speaking to some of the troops that have just arrived from Iraq, it really put things into perspective with all the things that are happening in the world. After all, this is just a game of football." Expectations were high for this US side after an impressiove performance in the World Cup four years ago when they upset Portugal and Mexico before falling to Germany in the quarter-finals. But the US simply never provided the type of challenge many expected against the Czechs, with the coach Bruce Arena complaining that some of his players lacked aggression. "This game is a great challenge for us," Reyna said. "We face a nation that has an amazing football tradition and we need to show what we are made of. We need to be brave and show as a team that we want to go through to the next stage." Reyna says his team-mates have already put behind them their disappointing performance against the Czechs. "Regardless of the result in the opening game, we knew that we would need to get a result against Italy," he said. "After our defeat against the Czechs we were disappointed but the very next day we got back to work. We now know what we need to get and that is a positive result and that urgency could help us." The 32-year-old believes he and his team-mates have learned their lessons from their bad start. "We know we have to remain concentrated and focused from the very first minute until the last," he said. "We cannot make the same mistake of giving away a goal early on." Reyna is aware his team will face a difficult challenge to stop the advances of the Italian strikers Luca Toni and Alberto Gilardino, who failed to score in their opening 2-0 win against Ghana. "Toni and Gilardino are hungry for goals," said Reyna. "They are predators and if we give them a chance they will put it away." A chat with US troops based in Ramstein has given the beleaguered Americans all the inspiration they need.

World Cup 2006: Imperious Argentina give Serbia six of the best. Dumped out of the last World Cup at the group stage after a disastrous campaign in which they scored just twice, both times from set pieces, Argentina are clearly in for the long haul this time. Yesterday's magnificent performance humiliated Serbia & Montenegro and produced a series of stunning goals, the second of which could prove the tournament's finest team effort. It said everything that the move was finished by Esteban Cambiasso, a defensive midfielder. Diego Maradona roared his approval at what his countrymen served up. The combination of silk and steel - when required - was marvellous, and the margin of victory could have been greater. Argentina's players ended by twirling shirts above their heads in front of their celebrating fans to mark what must rank among the finest performances of any World Cup. Serbia & Montenegro are competing as a unit for the final time in Germany, and this Argentina display felt good enough to beat two countries. Talk of them taking Brazil's crown is now inevitable. With Javier Saviola sharp in movement and possession, Maxi Rodríguez bursting from midfield to score twice, and Juan Román Riquelme decorating proceedings with a number of fine touches, Argentina were 3-0 ahead by half-time. As if to tease every other country with the depth of their attacking talent, the South Americans then brought on Lionel Messi and Carlos Tévez. The electric Messi set up a goal and scored another on his long-awaited return from injury and Tévez also found the net. For all the talk of Brazil's resources up front, Argentina can match that. Although the final three strikes came after a flailing Serbia had been reduced to 10 men by the 65th-minute sending-off of Mateja Kezman, that should not detract from Argentina's showing. They demonstrated their ability to hold possession and suddenly quicken the pace of the game with phenomenal thrusts, and their fluid interplay featured countless excellent flicks, passes and movements as they showed they share a wavelength and touched the heights. In the second half they were content to play in bursts, rather than at full throttle, but what bursts! This victory and a win over a tough Ivory Coast have them galloping towards the second round and their run will surely go much further. Serbia had conceded only two goals in their previous 12 competitive matches and got to this tournament by winning a qualifying group that included Spain, who scored against them once. Admittedly, Serbia were weakened here without Nemanja Vidic and their left-back Ivica Dragutinovic. With Riquelme moved into a central position, having played the previous game from the left, Argentina would surely have been 4-0 up by half-time had Hernán Crespo not been wrongly flagged offside. Gary Younge's ethical World Cup: Mexico v Angola. The impact of international football on nationalism is clear; it inflames it. Its impact on domestic politics is less so. Victory should favour the incumbents. But in Mexico, where the incumbent is resigning and leftwing presidential candidate Andrés Manuel López Obrador leads in the polls, it is difficult to predict whom a Mexican victory would benefit. As members of the UN security council both Mexico and Angola stood firm against US pressure to endorse the war. Safest, then, to back the Angolans, emerging from years of civil war with a life expectancy of 41 - just a year older than the tournament's oldest player. Verdict: Angola, by a nose! The impact of international football on nationalism is clear; it inflames it.

Nigeria themselves became the highest-ranked non-qualifier in 2006, at 11th, making this the first time all top-10 ranked teams have qualified for the World Cup." World Cup 2006: Italians determined to focus for USA game. Italy know they must not underestimate the United States in Kaiserslautern today, having been beaten by unfancied opponents in the last World Cup. Italy won their opening Group E game against Ghana 2-0 on Monday and, while Bruce Arena's side suffered a 3-0 loss to the Czech Republic in theirs, the Azzurri are taking nothing for granted. "We can't undervalue the importance of this game," said the Italy forward Alberto Gilardino. "For the United States it will be the game of their lives given that they lost their opening match against the Czechs and they know that if they mess up again they will be out. "From our point of view, we have to be focused and very solid in order to get another three points." Gilardino was one of the few disappointments in Italy's win over Ghana but is likely to keep his place despite the good form of Vincenzo Iaquinta who scored their second goal. The only change the Italy coach Marcello Lippi is expected to make will be to bring in the fit-again defender Gianluca Zambrotta at right-back in place of Cristian Zaccardo. The US reached the quarter-finals four years ago but need at least a point against Italy, who were knocked out by South Korea in the second round, to have any chance of advancing this time. "We're not in a coma yet," said Arena, "We're not going to go with the 3-0 loss game plan. We threw that page away after the [Czech] game and we'll try to come up with a new one." Reading's Bobby Convey was in no doubt about what is needed against the Italians. "We definitely need to create more chances against Italy than we did against the Czechs. It's going to be difficult. Obviously they're a hard team to break down. When we get a chance, we have to score." Among Arena's options would be to bring in the forward Eddie Johnson and midfielder John O'Brien, who played the second half against the Czechs. The coach has also settled his dispute with the PSV Eindhoven midfielder DaMarcus Beasley after they exchanged harsh words. Arena's squad prepared for the game at the vast US air base at Ramstein and there was a discernably military tone to the build-up, with Johnson talking of his experience of meeting veterans of the war in Iraq. "We're representing our country," he said. "We know [the soldiers] are watching us and following us." Gilardino followed suit, saying Italy were also playing for their troops. "We know they are watching and we want to put on a good show for them as well. They're fighting every day for Italy." AP As the last World Cup proved, Italy know all about being beaten by underdogs so they're having a good, hard look at the Americans.

Injury forces Owen out of World Cup. Michael Owen's tournament was today brought to a premature end after he was ruled out of the finals by injury. A scan on the Newcastle and England striker's right knee confirmed a rupture of his anterior cruciate ligament, forcing Owen to fly home from Germany and leave the prospect of him missing the start of next season. "The injury will prevent Michael playing any further part in the World Cup and he will return to England immediately," read a Football Association statement. "Newcastle United have been kept fully informed of the facts, and Michael's medical care will now be managed by the club. Any further comment regarding details of the injury or further treatment will be made by Newcastle United." Newcastle chairman Freddy Shepherd added: "We've been informed by the FA that Michael has suffered a ruptured anterior cruciate ligament, but at this stage it's too early to say how long he will be out for, although it is a serious injury. He'll come home and back into our care and will be assessed by our club medical staff at some point in the next couple of days following his return." Owen, who missed the second half of last season with a metatarsal injury, flew back to the team hotel in Baden-Baden this morning and underwent an MRI scan at the Max Grundig hospital at Buhlertal. He suffered the injury in the first minute of England's 2-2 draw with Sweden last night and immediately signalled his distress. "Obviously it's a massive blow to suffer the injury - and be out of the World Cup," said Owen today. "As soon as it happened I knew I was in trouble. It was a major disappointment to pick up the injury so early in the game. I'll go back home now and work with the Newcastle medical staff to put a recovery programme in place. "I'm clearly sorry to be leaving the lads in Germany, but I'm convinced they can go on and achieve success. I'll be following all the matches very closely and giving them my full support, hopefully all the way to Berlin. My main objective is now to get fit as quickly as possible for Newcastle United." England manager Sven-Goran Eriksson offered his sympathy to the injured striker. "Michael has had terrible luck with injuries since Christmas.I feel very sorry for him to miss out on the rest of the World Cup - it's blow to him and a big loss for the team," said Eriksson. "However I am sure Michael will work very hard to get back as quickly as possible for Newcastle and England. He is a true professional and a very mentally strong person. I hope the team can now go on and achieve success in Germany for Michael." Eriksson did at least receive an encouraging update on Rio Ferdinand and Gary Neville's injuries. The Manchester United pair both had scans with much more promising results than Owen. FA director of communications Adrian Bevington said a scan on Ferdinand's groin problem showed no damage at all and that the centre-half has a chance of playing against Ecuador on Sunday. World Cup: Italy 1 - 1 United States. Is this the group nobody wants to win? Welcome to the group of fear, where at various stages so far all four teams have lost their nerve. The capacity for Italy and the USA to shoot themselves in the foot was liberally exposed here. The Italians started it with a gauche own goal and a nasty red card, only for the Americans to prick their own balloon with a dismissal either side of half time. More of the game was played with 10 men against nine than any other numerical formula and it made for an intense, overstretched encounter which was strangely compelling. Particularly as the USA were the more ambitious, more enthusiastic team by far. Having started the tournament in impressive fashion, Italy's temperament let them down badly. They were playing with an extra man for almost the entire second half, yet wanted for creativity, for zest, for killer instinct. They now go into the group of fear's deciding day in Hamburg on Thursday requiring a draw against the Czech Republic to guarantee qualification into the knockout phase. Meanwhile the USA are by no means out, should they beat Ghana and the result in Hamburg go for them. American coach Bruce Arena had admitted he was struggling to identify any weakness in an Italy team that had looked supremely powerful against Ghana. Was this just some crafty psychology? His team displayed no such awe for their opponents. They might have arrived demoralised by their 3-0 battering by the Czechs, but they responded with a fusion of concentrated aggression and willingness to play football which caught Italy by surprise. The USA snapped and hassled, denying the Italians space to weave their passing and surging forward in numbers. Pressing higher and higher up the pitch, they forced Italy onto the back foot. For 20 minutes the famously solid defence marshalled by Fabio Cannavaro was stretched dangerously out of shape. Embarrassed, even. Bobby Convey's nimble feet served warning that Arena's team were in no mood to roll over. Alessandro Nesta scythed through the Reading winger on the edge of the box before Convey found more space to lift a shot over the bar. Then Clint Dempsey dragged a chance narrowly wide. All the while Landon Donovan probed and prompted with touches of class. The Italian attack earned their team no respite. When Eddie Pope conceded a free kick on Italy's right flank, the Azzurri were grateful for the chance to relieve the pressure in the best way possible. Andrea Pirlo's piercingly perfect free kick arrowed in front of the USA defence and Alberto Gilardino stooped to steer a firm header past Kasey Keller. Classic sucker-punch stuff. Back came the USA with one of their own. Five minutes later they were rewarded for their gameness when Convey's floated free kick was diverted past Gianluigi Buffon courtesy of Cristian Zaccardo's comically sliced clearance. Ukraine off the mark. Croaking frogs today seemed a distant memory to the Ukraine, who looked considerably more bright-eyed than during last week's 4-0 defeat to Spain as they swept Saudi Arabia aside in their penultimate group match. On this showing, it would appear the vociferous amphibians instead turned their attentions to disturbing the Arabian team, keeping them awake with their ribbits and catalysing a 4-0 demolition. The rout began with Andriy Rusol, who shinned Maxim Kalinichenko's corner through goalkeeper Mabrouk Zaid's legs after three minutes to claim his side's first-ever goal in the World Cup finals. Andriy Shevchenko nearly doubled the lead shortly after but his powerful header from an identical corner was cleared off the line. The ball pinged back to Serhiy Rebrov, who blasted the ball skywards when it appeared far easier to hit the target. Then Rebrov showed a glimpse of what once made him one of Europe's most sought-after strikers with a sublime finish to double the lead. Thirty-five yards from goal, with the Saudi defence backing away, the diminutive Dinamo Kiev forward struck the ball so sweetly that it left Zaid floundering. Rebrov turned to his team-mates in flummoxed celebration, the surprised expression on his face probably corresponding with those of any watching Tottenham fans, who saw him only as an £11m flop. The Saudi manager, Brazilian Marcos Paqueta, spent much of the game perplexingly shifting the chips on a magnetic tactical drafts board. But it was to no avail. Seconds into the second half and Shevchenko, the star of Ukrainian football, scored his first and his side's third goal. Maxim Kalinichenko floated a free-kick into the box towards the £30m Chelsea signing, who showed his calibre by directing a clean, powerful header into the back of the net. No stunned celebrations after that one. Saudi scoring opportunities were few and inconsequential. Mohammed Noor supplied Malek Mouath with a pass that sent Olexandr Shovkovskiy flailing out of his goal. Mouath scooped the ball back from the touchline and took a shot, but the angle was too tight and the Ukraine defence recovered. The match soon became a Saudi surrender, the Arabian team bearing all the hallmarks of the side that lost 8-0 in their opening match against Germany in 2002. Ukraine continued to attack with all the ease of a side enjoying a training-ground exhibition. Kalinichenko, who deserved a goal for his assured performance, swept the ball with his left foot and sent it flying goalwards, but the crossbar spoiled the effect and prevented the goal. Finally, however, Kalinichenko was rewarded. Seven minutes from time Rebrov unselfishly slipped the ball across the box to the Ukrainian midfielder, who smacked it past Zaid from close range. Their confidence restored, victory over Tunisia on Friday will put Oleg Blokhin's side through to the second round. Gary Younge's ethical World Cup: Spain v Tunisia. Tunisia squander their early lead courtesy of a developing world advantage (the average Tunisian earns about a third of their Spanish counterpart and lives seven years less) with a spate of wanton suppression of dissidents, including reports of torture of political prisoners. Spain pulls back with an impressive run up the left-wing. In the two years since their election, the Spanish socialists have withdrawn troops from Iraq, opened talks with ETA, legalised gay marriage, provided amnesty for 800,000 undocumented migrants and introduced a tough law against gender-based violence. A rare victory for Europe over Africa in an era of global disparity and client regimes. Verdict: ¡Support Spain! World Cup 2010: France can go all the way, says Patrice Evra. Patrice Evra has claimed that France can once again go all the way to the World Cup final, as he looked ahead to setting a positive tone from the outset in tomorrow night's opening group tie against Uruguay. "I am very confident and I have trust in the squad," said Evra, the newly appointed captain. "I think we can go right to the end [of the competition]. Anything can happen in football but that is what drives us. We are in very good health both on and off the field and that is what we need." Evra admitted his "pride" at being handed the captaincy by the manager, Raymond Domenech, in the wake of Patrick Vieira's omission and Thierry Henry's being sidelined. Henry has been a substitute in all three of France's warm‑up fixtures and, despite Nicolas Anelka's recent struggle for international form, Henry will not start against Uruguay. For Evra, the elevation reflects his burgeoning status in the game and it completes a remarkable personal journey from his early days at Manchester United when, for six months, he struggled to justify his move from Monaco. "It's difficult to talk about one's self but I have put in some hard work to this point," he said. "I have not had any gifts but I am determined. My first six months in Manchester were difficult but my psychology has helped me get this far." Domenech was in typically enigmatic mood at the pre-match press conference here in Cape Town, as he began his final weeks in the job, hoping to emulate the achievement of 2006 when he guided Les Bleus to the final, where they lost on penalties to Italy. "I don't prepare games with any certainty," he said. "I also may seem calm but underneath there is something of a storm going on and we need to manage that. There are 23 of us who are ready and determined. I would not say the word is 'calm'. We are determined, ready, perhaps a bit aggressive and impatient but calm is not the word I would use. "We have done everything to be ready for D-day. All the coaches try to do their best but this is not an exact science. We will try to be ready but we can't always guarantee we will be ready. Some teams are ready early, some later. If it were an exact science, we would not be involved in football." Domenech shrugged off the question of whether France deserved to be among the finalists, in light of the controversial nature of their play-off victory over the Republic of Ireland, when Henry clearly handled as he set up the decisive goal for William Gallas. "We finished second in our group and we won the play-off as, incidentally, Uruguay did," he said. "Other teams won and qualified. There are 32 who deserved to have qualified. That's football. The people who are here deserve to be here and the team that wins will deserve to do so and not the others." Patrice Evra has said he believes France can go all the way to the World Cup final

They won the tournament on their first two appearances, in 1930 and 1950, and Obdulio Varela, their indomitable captain during the latter triumph, has a strong case for being the greatest leader in the tournament's history. Yet of the seven past World Cup winners, they are the only ones who are never seen as contenders each time it rolls around. That, in part, is because a team drawn from a population that is now around three and a half million struggled to qualify, reaching only four of the last nine tournaments, struggling like an author with writer's block to replicate earlier successes, and in those four they did not go beyond the last 16. Their most memorable recent contribution was to be described as the "scum of world football" by Ernie Walker, the then chief executive of the Scottish Football Association, after Uruguay kicked everything that moved during their notorious match in 1986. Oscar Tabárez's side have a puncher's chance of becoming the first South American team other than Brazil or Argentina to reach the last eight since Peru in 1978. With Uruguay, however, things are rarely so simple. Newly appointed skipper Steven Gerrard, Frank Lampard and Aaron Lennon should complete a strong midfield quartet, with Lennon's pace making him a potential match-winner. Michael Carrick, James Millner and Joe Cole are more than adequate alternatives, although the completely useless Shaun Wright-Phillips can count himself lucky to make the squad ahead of the nearly-always useless Theo Walcott. World Cup 2010: Nigeria, Ghana, Ivory Coast, Cameroon and South Africa - my boys. On a humid Lagos night in July 1996, my family gathered around the television, all of us armed with hope: Nigeria was playing Argentina in the final game of the Atlanta Olympics. We shouted – "Go now, go go!" "What is he doing?" "Look at this stupid boy!"– and fell silent, by turns; we hoped and despaired; and we threw Igbo and English insults at the bald referee who we were convinced was biased against Nigeria. The score was 3-2 for Nigeria and there were a few minutes left to go and I could no longer breathe properly. I wanted to fast-forward time, to leap into our victory because I feared that the longer we waited, the less likely it would come. But it came. The shrill pee-pee-pee whistle went off. We had won gold. I remember feeling very light, hugging everyone, laughing, repeating the same things that somebody else had just said. We hugged neighbours we did not like. We offered drinks and relived the game over and over. From the streets came the sounds of car horns, of shouting, of singing. What happened that night was an explosion of nationalism of a certain kind, a benign, forgiving, optimistic nationalism. We forgot about neighbours who stole our electricity wires and leaders who stole our oil money. We all became, for that moment, Nigerians who had contributed to the vanquishing of the world. Still, football nationalism, for many of us, often expands past Nigeria, and into the rest of Africa. I do not ordinarily much care for football or for excessive nationalism, but whenever Nigeria plays a major game, I find myself undergoing a transformation. I kick the air as I watch. I scream. I pray. I will the universe to make us win. And the idea of an ''us'', a collective identity, becomes unquestionable. Sometimes the boundary of this identity widens, as it did during the 2006 World Cup when Nigeria did not qualify. And so, for one intense day while Ghana played the United States, I became Ghanaian. I watched with my Nigerian best friend Uju, hugging each other and dancing when Ghana finally won. "Some of our boys started playing this game without shoes," Uju said proudly. "Our boys" were, of course, the Ghanaians. Thankfully, Nigeria qualified for this year's tournament. , I will watch the Nigeria-Argentina match with that cautious optimism that is the default position of many Nigerians about our national team. (Our boys are good, we like to say, but the management and organisation of our team is terrible.) I would be thrilled if we win all our matches but I would not despair if we don't, because I would then aim my hope, borne of an expanded nationalism, at our other boys, from Ghana, Ivory Coast, Cameroon and South Africa. World Cup 2010: USA coach Bob Bradley reveals Capello's influence. The USA coach, Bob Bradley, has revealed he spent time in Italy studying Fabio Capello's techniques when the England coach managed Milan. Bradley, preparing his side to face England in their World Cup Group C opener on Saturday, said he developed his own coaching knowledge during visits to several top clubs, including Manchester United. Bradley's thirst for knowledge has paid off with the USA reaching the final of the 2009 Confederations Cup, knocking out Spain and only losing the final 3-2 to Brazil after leading 2-0. Bradley said: "I have enjoyed the opportunity to get to certain clubs, to see how things work. Without a doubt there have been some managers who, when you get to know them a bit, have been great in being open. "The times I've been at Manchester United, I appreciate the way Sir Alex Ferguson has set a whole tone within the club ... I also watched AC Milan a great deal in their great years which Fabio Capello was part of. That even included taking a college team to Milan at one point. "I have certainly seen his work over the years, whether it was Milan, Real Madrid, Juventus, England. His career speaks for itself. For me, I learn something every chance I have to see how these people work." Bradley said he expected to face a well-organised England team on Saturday. "Whenever you come up against teams that are managed by top managers like Capello, you expect they will be well prepared, tactically and mentally right. He sets a good tone with his team about how they need to play, how they go about their business. "For me the most impressive thing when you look at Capello's career is how he has adjusted to the different teams, to the talents, tweaked things. When he took over at Milan, he kept a lot of the good things but tweaked it a bit with the number of games Milan were playing so the physical side could be managed. "Capello's quote to me on many occasions was 'when you make wine, the grapes aren't always the same'. At first, I thought he was on about wine but I realised he was trying to tell me something about football!" USA striker Jozy Altidore, meanwhile, has won his battle to be fit in time for the game, Bradley confirmed. Altidore missed the weekend win over Australia in Johannesburg while recovering from an ankle problem, but the 20-year-old has resumed full training. Bradley said: "The injury cost him a couple of days training but, from the start, we knew it was very minor. The fact he is back in regular training means he is available for whatever role we choose on Saturday." Altidore could have a key role to play against England as he was the USA's leading scorer in their qualifying campaign with six goals. Edson Buddle and Robbie Findley, though, also staked their claims to start by impressing as a partnership against Australia. Meanwhile, Bradley is also optimistic that key defender Oguchi Onyewu will be able to complete 90 minutes against England if called

World Cup 2010: Witch doctor exorcises Harry Kewell's 'curse'. If Australia perform well at the World Cup, they may want to thank a witch doctor and their prime minister, Kevin Rudd, for combining to exorcise the 'curse' affecting Harry Kewell. Rudd passed on a goodwill greeting to Kewell at the insistence of the South African healer Bishop Isaac Nonyane, who has claimed the PM's best wishes would exorcise the "malicious spirits" plaguing the 31-year-old. An injury to Kewell's groin has dominated Australia's build-up to the World Cup, with Pim Verbeek, the coach, desperate to have the forward fit for Sunday's opening match against Germany. "No one has bewitched him," said Nonyane – who has claimed to be communicating with his dead grandmother – as he held a Bible and a picture of Kewell. "But it is because of malicious spirits, and the main intention of those malicious spirits is to hurt him. "A get-well message from the prime minister will do. Let the guy [Kewell] know that the whole Australian community rallies behind him to get well, and that they do believe in him, to get well." World Cup 2010: Divided loyalties. On the face of it, England's opening World Cup match against the United States gives me the rare sporting privilege of watching a game I can't lose. I was born in Uganda to an American father and an English mother, and hold passports to both countries. (I've investigated the possibility of Ugandan citizenship, but the Ugandan government doesn't permit it at the moment, though it may in the future.) I've happily, perhaps even cynically, exploited the benefits of dual citizenship most of my life: going down the fast-track queues at immigration; voting on both sides of the Atlantic; studying in the US on a scheme for British nationals; reaching the shortlist of an award that's restricted to American writers; cheering on the Dream Team in Barcelona 1992 and the English rugby team in 2003. Do I feel English? Do I feel American? I'm a bigamist who is in love with both his wives. Occasionally I dislike them both. But why choose between them? True, when I try to talk with an American accent it comes out like a strangled version of Cliff from Cheers (my father grew up in a suburb of Boston). But it's at least as convincing as the accents of those notable Americans Rupert Murdoch, Sir Anthony Hopkins and, for that matter, Henry Kissinger. It has always seemed obvious to me that nationality is a fiction, anyway. The nation state is a bogus construct: our real ties are with families and neighbourhoods. My children are Anglo-Welsh-Americans of French-Canadian and Italian extraction. But, basically, they're from Tooting. Being Anglo-American is not like being, say, Anglo-German or Russian-American. It's not overtly divisive. Wars between my two nations come along very rarely; there is a probably unhealthy degree of agreement over foreign policy; and sporting events (a proxy for war) are rarely significant enough to cause major trauma. Each country dwells in a sphere of sporting influence (baseball and gridiron; football and cricket) that never really overlaps with the other. But a US-England football/soccer match with stakes as high as these is a profound and troubling thing for hyphenates like me. We want to believe we can love both countries and want both to succeed. That clearly won't be possible come Saturday evening. My first inkling that a dual allegiance wasn't an infinitely feasible state of affairs came when I needed to get a passport for my newborn daughter. I learned then that people and institutions are not as comfortable with my ambiguous status as I am. It turns out I'm not as American as I think. While I hold a US passport, I can't prove I've been physically present on US territory for five years of my life, which means I can't easily pass on my citizenship to my children. My long residence in the UK has attenuated my Americanness. Two years ago, at the sharp end of a bureaucratic process administered from that impregnable and joyless bunker in Grosvenor Square, I got a glimpse of America as it must appear to o

World Cup 2010: Concern mounts for Australia over Tim Cahill neck injury. Tim Cahill and not Harry Kewell is now Australia's biggest injury concern ahead of their World Cup opener against Germany on Sunday night in Durban. While Kewell, whose much-talked about groin injury has dominated Australia's build-up to the 2010 World Cup, was able to join in an intense two-hour training session last night, Cahill was a little more than a spectator. The Everton midfielder is still recovering from a neck injury sustained during the first half of Saturday's 3-1 loss in a warm-up match against the United States and, while the rest of Pim Verbeek's squad engaged in a full-scale practice match, Cahill was having shooting practice alone on the side of the pitch. • Follow the Guardian's World Cup team on Twitter • Sign up to play our great Fantasy Football game • Stats centre: Get the lowdown on every player • The latest team-by-team news, features and more The sight of Cahill – by far Australia's most prolific scorer in recent years – being unable to join in training just five days from the Socceroos' opening game is a major concern. With 20 goals from 40 appearances, Cahill has scored more than any other member of the Australia squad and famously netted the first World Cup finals goal for Australia four years ago when his two late strikes against Japan secured a memorable 3-1 victory in the opening game. But while there is now some doubt over Cahill for the opening game in Durban on Sunday, Kewell seems to have won his fitness battle, just as Verbeek predicted all along. The 31-year-old, whose late goal against Croatia four years ago secured Australia's passage to the knockout stages of the World Cup for the first time, trained strongly last night and looks set to be ready to face Germany. Kewell missed both of Australia's warm-up games against Denmark and the United States but appears to have timed his return to fitness to perfection. Australia's other big injury concern for their third World Cup finals appearance following 1974 and 2006 in midfielder Brett Emerton also joined in full training and also looks to have overcome his recent calf problem. Tim Cahill and not Harry Kewell is now Australia's biggest injury concern ahead of their World Cup opener against Germany

World Cup 2010: Madjid Bougherra confident Algeria can tackle England. Algeria will play the first match of their third World Cup against Slovenia on Sunday but their players already have an eye on their second Group C match, against England. The Rangers defender Madjid Bougherra said today that his experience in British football would help him to deal with the likes of Wayne Rooney when Algeria meet England at the Green Point Stadium in Cape Town next Friday night. The centre-back, who has played for Crewe Alexandra, Charlton Athletic and Sheffield Wednesday, has also played European football with the Scottish champions. "It can help me," said Bougherra of his experience in Britain. "I play in a big team, I played Champions League so I have learned a mentality change – I want to win every game." • Follow the Guardian's World Cup team on Twitter • Sign up to play our great Fantasy Football game • Stats centre: Get the lowdown on every player • The latest team-by-team news, features and more Rangers fans voted him their player of the year after his first season at Ibrox, 2008-09, in which the Glasgow club won a league and cup double. "It is British football," he said of such experience, "so this can help me with England." Algeria's new captain for Sunday's match, Antar Yahia, yesterday suffered an injury scare just over 48 hours before the match against Slovenia. The defender was given the captain's armband after Algeria's manager, Rabah Saadane, dropped Yazid Mansouri because of poor form. However, the 28-year-old Yahia, who has expressed a desire to leave the relegated Bundesliga side Bochum this summer, injured his left ankle in training after a collision with the squad's third‑choice goalkeeper, Raïs M'bolhi, and limped off before the session ended. Algeria suffered another blow when the striker Rafik Djebbour aggravated a foot injury, which he had sustained yesterday, in training today. He is a major doubt for the game against the Slovenes. Algeria, who qualified for the 1982 and 1986 World Cups, made this year's tournament at the expense of the six-times African champions, Egypt. "We will play each game like a cup final," Saadane said. World Cup 2010: Germany juggernaut ready to roll against Australia. The most decorated country in Europe they may be but not many have expected much from Germany since they won their last trophy at Euro 96. Few pundits had them as a pre-tournament pick, even though their recent record is as good as it gets without winning a trophy: a World Cup final in 2002, a semi-final spot at home in 2006 and a European Championship final in 2008. Expectations are low – most fans would be happy enough with a place in the quarter-finals, maintaining a record of reaching at least that stage in every finals since 1954. But the rest of the field will be concerned at the bullish talk of Germany's captain, Philipp Lahm, who ahead of today's opening fixture, against Australia in Durban, has announced that Germany can improve on their impressive showing in the last two tournaments. "I think this is the best German team in which I have played," said Lahm, who at 26 will become his country's youngest World Cup captain after replacing the injured skipper, Michael Ballack. "There is even more confidence in the team because we did so well in the warm-up games, which was not the case back in 2006." Germany arrived in Pretoria on the back of three wins in a row, 3-1 against Bosnia-Herzegovina and 3-0 triumphs over Malta and Hungary. As well as hitting form at the right time, Germany have youth on their side. Joachim Löw's squad have an average age of just under 25 – their lowest at a World Cup since 1934. "We are a young and hungry team," Lahm said. "It's noticeable at every training session [that] the team trains with great enthusiasm and tempo. Today we have more players who will go on the attack during a 1-1 draw. We have more hands‑on types, not so typically German." Lahm, who has been enjoying a career revival at Bayern Munich, added: "We really want to start with a win. It will give the young team even more security. We can set the world alight, but winning the first game is absolutely imperative." Germany's captain, Philipp Lahm, has said he believes his young team can triumph even though few pundits back them

World Cup 2010: Samuel Eto'o on his incredible journey. "It's incredible," Samuel Eto'o says as a dazzling smile lights up his usually serious face and he bangs a table in exultation. "I've always had this dream of playing in the World Cup in Africa and it's about to become a reality. When I'm in South Africa it will be the same as if I'm back in Cameroon. I've always said that, even before Cameroon, I belong to Africa. I might live in Europe but I sleep in Africa." Eto'o speaks in impassioned torrents of French, sounding as poetic as he is fiery while explaining how his extraordinary journey merges with the wider story of Africa's first World Cup. After making his international debut the day before he turned 15 Samuel Eto'o Fils left Cameroon for Spain in 1996. He travelled from his hometown of Douala to Madrid, shivering in short trousers when he arrived at Barajas airport, lonely and frightened. "It seems incredible now," Eto'o exclaims again, reaching for his favourite word, "but then my whole life is incredible!" • Sign up now and play our great Fantasy Football game • Get the lowdown on every player with our stats centre • All of the latest team-by-team news, features and more • Follow the Guardian's World Cup team on Twitter now His trial led to a contract with Real Madrid which ultimately entailed little more than him being shunted from one Spanish club to another. Eto'o was loaned out to Leganés and Espanyol and Mallorca before, finally, the last of those clubs bought him in 2000. Four seasons on, having won the first of three African Player of the Year awards, Eto'o moved to Barcelona. Despite suffering terribly from racism, Eto'o established himself as one of the world's great footballers as he helped Barcelona win three La Liga titles and two Champions League finals. A few weeks ago, having been transferred last summer to Internazionale as the Italian club were offered Eto'o and €46m (£38m) in exchange for Zlatan Ibrahimovic, he celebrated his first season in Serie A by winning the treble – including yet another Champions League title as his work-rate and discipline proved crucial to José Mourinho's tactical masterclass. "That's why I'm so proud to be African in this World Cup," Eto'o says. "Like most Africans I had to work much harder and show much deeper belief than others. I started with nothing and reached the level I'm at today. All I had was football and God's help. But I made it and now I'm going home, to Africa, where we can show a different face to the world. "Most people only see Africa in terms of poverty and war, famine and disease. But this World Cup gives us the chance to show something different. I think the whole world is going to be really surprised by Africa. This could be the best World Cup in history." World Cup 2010: Italy happy believing the world is against them | David Hytner. When Fifa asked Patrick Vieira to hand over the World Cup trophy to Danny Jordaan, the head of the local organising committee, at last Thursday's pre-tournament concert in Soweto, it seemed like a nice enough feature of a star-studded event. Africa is not blessed with an abundance of football world champions and the invitation to Vieira, the Senegal-born France midfielder who helped win the trophy in 1998, struck a symbolic chord. Not in Italy nor, more specifically, at the Azzurri's training base in Centurion near Pretoria. Within the ranks of the world champions there was surprise and indignation at a perceived slight. Why was a member of their victorious team from 2006 not chosen for the honour? "Vieira didn't win that cup, Italy did," said Giancarlo Abete, the president of the Italian Football Federation. "It was a gaffe by Fifa and they must offer an explanation. We received no formal invitation. If we had been asked to send an official delegation, I would have had no problem in responding positively." Fifa did ask Andrea Pirlo to attend but the Italy playmaker was receiving treatment for the calf injury that has plunged his World Cup into jeopardy. Italy hope he can return for the final Group F fixture against Slovakia. Abete and his fellow directors were astonished that Fifa did not extend an alternative invite. The diplomatic incident was the latest illustration of the Italians' feeling that the world is against them. Some might call it paranoia but Marcello Lippi and his players have felt compelled to batten down the hatches in the face of criticism and scepticism, particularly from their countrymen. The Italian media gives the team little chance of retaining the World Cup and ringing up a record-equalling fifth triumph – the squad is too old and key players are past their best, is the view – while even politicians have spoken out against Lippi. They have questioned his ways and means while, in what several players have claimed is political grandstanding, they have complained about the financial bonuses on offer. Lippi's squad are on £198,000 a man from their federation to win the tournament. The Azzurri, however, revel in a siege mentality and if the grounds for the current one pale when compared to the calciopoli scandal that prompted their ranks to be closed at the 2006 World Cup, the result is the same. Italy intend to use the slings and arrows as motivational therapy. They kick off their campaign against Paraguay tomorrow night. "Whenever a World Cup comes around," Lippi said, "everyone wants to get in their say. Last time, everyone joined in as we moved along but this time, we're not going to allow anyone to jump on the bandwagon." Lippi has the skin to absorb criticism and he shrugged off the Vieira controversy, although he did wonder why the player "decided to do that. World Cup 2010: Italy not too old to win, says Marcello Lippi. Marcello Lippi has hit out at critics who have claimed his Italy team are too old to win the World Cup. The head coach is unhappy with the scepticism over his squad – even in Italy – and has asked for the team to be given a chance to prove themselves. "Yes, we have old players but with age comes charisma and the experience of playing in big games. I think we have the right mix of young and old players," Lippi said. "We have nine players from 2006 – that's less than 50%. I've never seen any team that wins the World Cup show up four years later with 23 new players." While Lippi has kept the 36-year-old Fabio Cannavaro and 33-year-olds Gianluca Zambrotta and Mauro Camoranesi, he has also brought along three 23-year-old defenders – Salvatore Bocchetti, Leonardo Bonucci and Domenico Criscito. The average age of the squad is 28 years and nine months. "Whenever a World Cup comes around everyone wants to get in their say," said Lippi. "Last time everyone joined in as we moved along but this time we're not going to allow anyone to jump on the bandwagon." With Andrea Pirlo likely to miss the entire group phase due to a left calf injury, Italy is without its most talented midfielder. "Single players don't win World Cups. Let's be clear. There are no longer any superstars in Italy and it's not like anyone I left home would alter the skill aspect," said Lippi. The 62-year-old coach also said that the squad could contain a "new" Paolo Rossi – the striker who scored six goals in the last three games at the 1982 World Cup. Giampaolo Pazzini, an unsung forward who scored 19 goals for Sampdoria this season, has already been compared to Rossi. "Let's see," Lippi said. "Who knows, maybe we'll discover someone here who can really carry us on their shoulders." Marcello Lippi has hit out at critics who claimed his team were too old to retain the World Cup

Holland World Cup 2010 fan's preview: No easy route after group stage. The Dutch had a plan in the 2006 World Cup: it was to win Euro 2008. That didn't work out, but since then Bert van Marwijk has been building on the positives (crushing wins against Italy, France and Romania) and learning from the setbacks. He's a pragmatic coach who understands that style sometimes has to be sacrificed – a long way from the mantra of Johan Cruyff and co. The return of Mark van Bommel has added some occasionally illegal, but always required, steel and grit, and the defence was watertight in qualifying. There's no easy route once past the group stage though. I'd expect a quarter-final defeat. Men to watch: Attacking options abound: Wesley Sneijder, Arjen Robben (hamstring allowing), Rafael van der Vaart, Robin van Persie and Klaas-Jan Huntelaar. And breaking through at just the right time are two superb young attackers: Eljero Elia and Ibrahim Afellay. Weakest link: The defence is of less concern than normal, but the keeper is the worry. The current cream of the crop, Maarten Stekelenburg, is more of a boring Edam than a really good quality Gouda. There are no viable alternatives. The coach: Bert is no glory seeker. His goal is to organise the firepower and keep the peace between the superstars in the squad and the less well-paid. Best chant: "Hup Holland" ("Go Holland"). Least-liked opponents: Germany and Portugal. TIPS Winner: Spain – full of top-class players. Golden boot: David Villa (Spain). England: They won't struggle in the group stage, but will lose in the quarter-finals. England lack depth of talent, and endeavour and sweat aren't enough. The best to be hoped for is a dignified defeat.
