World Cup 2010: Marcello Lippi confident Italy can see off Slovakia. Italy can take their inspiration from England. After a pair of toothless draws, Fabio Capello got the result that he needed against Slovenia to move England into the last 16 and his old friend Marcello Lippi intends to copy him. The early stages of Italy's campaign here in South Africa have been almost identical to England's. The Azzurri's 1-1 draw with Paraguay was not a performance to set the pulses racing and their subsequent 1-1 draw against New Zealand prompted soul-searching and criticism back home. • Follow the Guardian's World Cup team on Twitter • Sign up to play our great Fantasy Football game • Stats centre: Get the lowdown on every player • The latest team-by-team news, features and more Italy, though, are famously slow starters. In 1982, in one of their four World Cup-winning years, they scraped through the group phase with three draws and Lippi noted that the same return would suffice once more if Paraguay were to beat New Zealand in Polokwane. The manager and his players, though, do not want to leave anything to chance, regardless of the omens. When they play the World Cup debutants Slovakia at Ellis Park in Johannesburg, they will look for victory in Group F. Confidence is high that the result will be theirs. As England did, Italy, the past masters of tournament football, take heart from their destiny being in their own hands. And, like England, they also reason that they are due an improved performance. "We haven't done what we're capable of but we can still kick-start our World Cup," Lippi said. "We haven't been tormented by the wait for this game, the confidence is there." Lippi said the two goals his team have conceded so far have come from the only efforts on target against them. Although worryingly they were both from free-kicks, he said his team has looked compact. The problem has been in the creation of chances and, with the playmaker Andrea Pirlo not yet fit enough to start in the wake of a calf problem, Lippi is considering an attacking reshuffle. Claudio Marchisio has been a disappointment and he is expected to be dropped, with Riccardo Montolivo assuming his attacking midfield role and Gennaro Gattuso coming into the team in defensive midfield. Up front, Giampaolo Pazzini and Antonio Di Natale could replace Alberto Gilardino and Simone Pepe. "Our best is yet to come and our first win would change everything for us," said the captain, Fabio Cannavaro. "We have no fear because that only drives you to make mistakes." Cameroon 1-2 Denmark | World Cup 2010 Group E match report. Cameroon tonight became the first team to be knocked out of the World Cup after a wild night of attacking thrills in Pretoria. But they, along with most of the crowd here, will be scratching their heads and wondering quite how it happened. Paul Le Guen's side threw everything at Denmark, created much the better chances — they had 23 shots to 13 — and forced Thomas Sorensen into a series of desperate saves. They went ahead early through Samuel Eto'o and had most of the possession afterwards but it was all to no avail. Two fine goals on the counter, from Nicklas Bendtner and Dennis Rommedahl, knocked the stuffing out of them and with Japan and Denmark, who both have three points, playing each other in the final group game Cameroon are out. Cameroon arrived in Pretoria riven by feuding following Le Guen's decision to deploy Eto'o on the right and omit Alex Song for the team's defeat to Japan. This time round Le Guen – pressured by government officials and Eto'o himself – reversed his decisions and, with Eto'o in the centre, Song guarding the back four and Achille Emana brought in to bolster the attack, Cameroon looked far better for it. They attacked from the off and went ahead after 10 minutes after an eyes-wide-shut mistake from Christian Poulsen, who passed blind to the right from the edge of his penalty area to Eyong Enoh, who crossed for Eto'o to slide past Sorensen. As the Cameroonians celebrated, Le Guen clapped and pumped his fists on the sidelines, but he cut a lonely figure. Denmark, having lost their opening match to Holland, also picked a team intent on attacking, with Jesper Gronkjaer brought in for Thomas Enevoldsen and Jon Dahl Tomasson playing behind Nicklas Bendtner in a 4-2-3-1 formation. But this sacrificed their defensive solidity, and they were repeatedly exposed whenever Eto'o and Emana ran at them. Emana should have made it two when he ran on to a defensive clearance and smashed it just wide. Their defence was creaking but at the other end Denmark had chances too in what quickly proved to be the most open game of the tournament. Early on, Rommedahl took a pass from Christian Poulsen, cut in from the right and shot over. Then Bendtner turned Nicolas Nkoulou to collect Rommedahl's pass only for his close-range shot to be smothered. But it was still a minor surprise when, shortly after the half-hour, Denmark equalised. From just outside his own penalty area Simon Kjaer hit a spectacular 70-yard pass over Benoît Assou-Ekotto and into Rommedahl's path. Rommedahl took one touch and crossed to Bendtner who slotted home from close range. By this stage both teams had already given up trying to defend. In the space of two minutes before half-time Rommedahl's shot was smothered, Tomasson smashed over from 12 yards, Eto'o hit the post and Emana missed a one-on-one. World Cup 2010: Portugal v North Korea - as it happened. Preamble Hello. During the final series of The Sopranos, as he meticulously burned his bridges with the rest of humanity, Tony Soprano tersely opined that "'Remember when' is the lowest form of conversation". You simply cannot preview this game without rewinding to the highlight of the 1966 World Cup: the amazing quarter-final between these two sides at Goodison Park. North Korea, who had already dumped Italy out, roared into a 3-0 lead in the craziest of atmospheres before being well and truly Eusebioed. He scored four times in a 5-3 win, but failure comes no more glorious than North Korea's. Cris Freddi, these islands' premier football historian, nailed it by with the simple description: "a unique match". Today has no chance of matching that, but North Korea played extremely well against Brazil and could feasibly take points off either Portugal or Ivory Coast. Yet while Portugal will need to play well just to win the game, they must also keep half an eye on goal difference. If they win today and lose to Brazil, and Ivory Coast beat North Korea – all entirely conceivable results – goal difference will decide who finishes second in the group. Good evening everybody and welcome to our coverage of this Group H encounter between Spain and Honduras at Ellis Park in Johannesburg, writes minute-by-minute reporter from King's Cross bunker. You probably don't need me to remind you that both teams need to win tonight, what with tournament favourites Spain having been shocked by Switzerland, while Honduras were beaten 1-0 by the swashbucklers of Chile, who might have scored 10 or 11 if their strikers hadn't been so profligate in front of goal. Having beaten the Swiss earlier this afternoon, Chile are sitting pretty on top of the group with six points from two games, with Switzerland behind them in second place on three, while Spain and Honduras have yet to get off the mark, but have tonight's game in hand. An emphatic win for either side will catapult them above the Swiss on goal difference. Some match pointers with which you can bore your friends down the drinker • Xavi (102) and Xabi Alonso (101) were the only players to attempt over 100 passes (including crosses and corners) in the opening round of games • If Honduras score against Spain, it will be 28 years to the day since their last World Cup goal • No side have ever won the World Cup after losing their opening game in the group stage • Honduras' pass completion rate of 63% was the lowest in any of the first round of group games • Spain's defeat against Switzerland was the first time they have lost with Andrés Iniesta in the starting line-up Team news we've culled from the wires Fernando Torres returns to Spain's starting line-up for tonight's match against Honduras, taking the place of Andres Iniesta. Torres came on as a substitute in the 1-0 defeat to Switzerland, and returns to the starting 11 for the first time since having right-knee surgery in April. Spain coach Vicente del Bosque is also giving Jesus Navas his second start, with the speedy winger replacing David Silva in a more attacking line-up. World Cup 2010 live blog: 21 June | Sean Ingle. Welcome to guardian.co.uk's daily live World Cup blog, wherever you are in the world ... Our hope is that this blog will provide all of the following: breaking news, predictions, pontifications, colour from our 13-strong team in South Africa, plus lots of pointing outwards; to your comments below the line, to the best things we've seen on the web, to various World Cup randomania. Our plan is to update the blog from from 8am-6pm UK time, however the posts will come faster between 9am until around midday, when our minute-by-minute reports will kick-in. As there's no point in duplication from that point on, we'll post the best bits of the minute-by-minutes, and bring you updates from our writers in South Africa and fans' networks members across the globe. What's coming up today: Press conferences and training sessions for almost every team, plus three intriguing games: Portugal v North Korea (12.30pm), Chile v Switzerland (3pm), Spain v Honduras (7.30pm). What a day yesterday, eh? There was: 1) John Terry trying to take on Fabio Capello in Camp England ... and getting slapped down quicker than a roll of dough in the hands of an expert pizzaioli. 2) The France squad's extraordinary decision to refuse to train. 3) New Zealand, the 2000-1 outsiders, holding out for a draw against World Champions Italy. 4) And last night's tutti-fruity Brazil v Ivory Coast match ... 8.20am: The big news of the past 24 hours, to English ears at least, is John Terry's straight talking at the English press conference yesterday morning ... and his subsequent failure to follow through at the team meeting with Fabio Capello last night. As Paul Hayward reports: This has been the biggest demonstration of England player power since Italia 90, when Bobby Robson came under pressure to switch to a three-man defence after an unconvincing start - and late tonight Capello's players appeared to have won concessions on the timing of the team announcement and improving lines of communication. Robson maintained to the end that the sweeper system was his idea and that he would never have allowed himself to be dictated to by players. Conversely Terry broke ranks alone: raising the possibility that he mistook Cape Town for Cobham, the Chelsea training ground where he wields so much power. Terry's gamble, on Father's Day, was to display himself as the big daddy of this squad and take the discord to the public. At the meeting itself Terry was persuaded to take a back seat. Earlier, he had said: "Whether he [Capello] starts it or finishes it, the players can say how they feel and, if it upsets him, then I'm on the verge of just saying: 'You know what? So what? I'm here to win it for England. He's feeling the same, the players are feeling the same and, if we can't be honest with each other, then there's no point in us being here. You can't hold grudges. If I say something tonight, and I probably will, and a few others will, then I'm doing the best

World Cup 2010: Chile v Switzerland - as it happened. Afternoon all. Interesting, very interesting (as Barry Davies once said). And I don't think I'm the only one utterly intrigued by this fixture. The suped-up Ford Fiesta that is Chile v the slightly-battered but rugged Swiss Land Rover. Chile were superb in their first game against Honduras, their full-backs attacking with vim and vigour, their midfielders tricksy and direct. There usn't a Player Of The First Round of Matches Award, but if there was I'd have handed it to Alexis Sanchez, who was more refreshing than a shower in lemon juice (and almost as zesty). Coach Bielsa likes to employ a 3-3-1-3 and they'll need to get players forward to break down a Swiss side that produced (still) the shock of the tournament so far by beating Spain, despite having 0.43% of the possession. The question is whether the Swiss look to land a knockout blow and risk a slightly more adventurous outlook, or whether there'll take a 'it was good enough for Spain, it'll be good enough today' approach. Although, to be fair, they played with two up front against the Spanish, and were discliplined in extremis against a team with clear technical superiority. Perhaps we shouldn't confuse work-rate and pragmatism with negativity. The teams are in: Chile (3-3-1-3):-Claudio Bravo; Mauricio Isla, Waldo Ponce, Arturo Vidal; Gary Medel, Carlos Carmona, Gonzalo Jara; Matias Fernandez; Alexis Sanchez, Humberto Suazo, Jean Beausejour. Switzerland (4-4-2): Diego Benaglio; Stephan Lichtsteiner, Stephane Grichting, Steve Von Bergen, Reto Ziegler; Valon Behrami, Gokhan Inler, Benjamin Huggel, Gelson Fernandes; Alexander Frei, Blaise Nkufo. Referee: Khalil Al-Ghamdi (Saudi Arabia) So both sides see the return of important strikers. Chile's Suazo was the top scorer in South American qualifying, while Alex Frei is Switzerland's all-time top scorer with 40 goals from 73 caps and his personal website's photo album contains this picture, which I find myself inexplicably fond of. If you haven't already watched it take a bit of time pre-match to enjoy our brick-by-brick reconstruction of England v Algeria. Rooney's fan rage is particularly good. And speaking of child-like joy, I'm going to an sports day this evening. In the park, With some friends. Egg-and-spoon race, beanbag-on-the-head race, digging-up-childhood-trauma-of-coming-last-and-getting-sympathy-applause-from-parents race … there'll all be there. Any last-minute suggestions to add to the programme? Pre-match email dept. "Good to see Alex Frei back for the Swiss," writes Dave Cisowksi. "He's got the best theme song of all time. Bang bang!" Ah, once upon a time I might have liked that. Now it's just noise. You know you're getting old when German thrash metal doesn't do it for you anymore. And on a sports-day theme, here's another bit of music from Hani Zaccarelli. Well, actually it's Roots Manuva, but you know what I mean. Clearly I need to get my hands on a multicoloured parachute f

Anschluss. Conexion. Stitch-up. A selection of guttural-sounding foreign words (German, Spanish, Mockney) but today they all mean essentially the same thing: a mutually beneficial pact of non-aggression. Uruguay and Mexico are, to be fair to them, in an impossible position here. Through their own prior excellence they find themselves both only needing a draw to go through. Unsporting, perhaps, to settle for a point; but also irresponsible not to do just that if it's still 0-0 after an hour or so. Teams go for the draw all the time. Can we really deny these two the right? Still, it all represents more terrible news for France, who need to win and improve their goal difference by 3 relative to Mexico. So at least it's not all bad. 2.34pm: It is a shame in another way too: this could have been - and might still be - a cracker. Mexico, excellent in midfield but perhaps short of a top class striker; against Uruguay who have two. 2.35pm: The teams are in: Mexico: Perez, Osorio, Rodriguez, Moreno, Salcido, Torrado, Marquez, Guardado, Giovani, Blanco, Franco. Uruguay: Muslera, Maxi Pereira, Lugano, Victorino, Fucile, Arevalo Rios, Perez, Pereira, Forlan, Suarez, Cavani. Referee: Viktor Kassai (Hungary) 2.38pm: Blanco starts for Mexico, which should make for a fun 15 minutes or so before he needs a lie down. Mexico will probably stay in a mobile 4-3-3. Uruguay most likely the usual rugged and pacy 4-4-2. 2.41pm: Lee James notes sagely :"With all this talk of an Anschluss style fix do you think that would show Mexico in a particularly cowardly light? They have a frustrating record of always reaching the second round despite fluctuations in the quality of their team- they got knocked out by Argentina (and Maxi) at the last world cup didn't they? Surely they can't be so afraid of getting beat by Uruguay in an open game that they are willing to settle for the second round again this time." Hmm. But still. It's all about the getting there. Cowardly is a bit rough too. They're in this position because they've been bold. 2.43pm: Matthew Judd muses: "Surely there is enough motivation for these two to compete in order to avoid Argentina next round?" And Julia, who has also attached a photo, writes: "I found you in my friends list. I'm guess I added you at the one of social networks. I live in New York, USA. Search for new friends. like to travel and like visit new places. I wanna visit England. Probably this summer. I hope to hear from you and see more pictures soon. Kisses." Which is all fine. But what about Javi Hernandez's enviable goal record? Eh, Julia? 2.52pm: Tim Ellis notes: "Doubt Mexico will play to draw. I don't think it's in their chemistry. And a draw for Mexico would mean facing Argentina in the round of 16. Do you think that the

World Cup 2010: Uruguay v Mexico - a game made for defences | Lazar Treschan. The opening World Cup games may have left something to be desired. But the second set of matches, at least so far, have been nothing short of wonderful. Now things are going to get even more interesting, as teams will fight tooth and nail to stay alive and get into the last 16. The Group A situation is particularly intriguing. Here's how things currently stand: Uruguay 4 points, + 3 goals Mexico 4 points, + 2 goals France 1 point, -2 goals South Africa 1 point, -3 goals In today's final group games Uruguay play Mexico, and South Africa play France. Since the bottom two are both three points behind the top two, if Uruguay and Mexico draw, they both automatically qualify for the second round. The only way that South Africa or France can qualify is if either Uruguay or Mexico loses and by such a margin that the loser ends up with a worse goal difference than the winner of South Africa v France. Maybe Uruguay and Mexico might want to pass the ball back and forth for 90 minutes, ending in a not-so-hard fought draw. Let's remember the 1982 match between West Germany and Austria. As Algeria had already played their final group game, it was clear that the Germans needed a 1-0 victory to get through to the next round. That same result would also keep Austria in the top spot, so they allowed the Germans to score after 10 minutes, and then the two teams proceeded to kick the ball around pointlessly for 80 minutes. The match, played in Gijón, Spain, drew a lot of negative attention, and is often referred to in Germany as the "Schande von Gijón" (the Shame of Gijón). As a result, all final group games are now played simultaneously. Could we see such a boring pass-around in Uruguayv Mexico? It would not even need to be as scandalous as letting one team score; the two sides could just decide on their own not to attack and risk the loss that might kick them out of South Africa. It's possible but perhaps not practical. Whoever comes in second place in this group has to face Argentina in the next round. Whoever wins the group gets to face South Korea, (or possibly Greece or Nigeria). That's a big difference. The South Koreans are good but they are no Argentina – not even close. Maybe each of them secretly wants to play Argentina, at least a little bit. The only thing as satisfying for Uruguay as winning the World Cup would be beating Argentina, our arch-rivals, in any round. And while Argentina do look like they could score at any moment, their defence is horrible. And Mexico have a reason for revenge against Argentina, seeing that they lost to them in the last 16 four years ago, thanks to a flukey, if lovely, goal from Maxi Rodríguez. There's actually some recent bad blood between Uruguay and Mexico, too, which could undermine any desire to collaborate. Teams: Ghana: 22-Richard Kingson; 4-John Pantsil, 8-Jonathan Mensah, 5-John Mensah, 2-Hans Sarpei; 6-Anthony Annan, 23-Kevin-Prince Boateng, 13-Dede Ayew, 12-Prince Tagoe; 21-Kwadwo Asamoah, 3-Asamoah Gyan. Referee: Carlos Simon (Brazil) Preamble: So it comes down to Ghana again. The country that is often described as the first in black Africa to gain independence is the continent's last representative in the World Cup (yes, Ivorians, I am reluctantly counting you out). And to go farther they will have to best a European power again – well, get at least a draw with Germany. And this is a battle that, for the first time in World Cup history, will pit brother against brother: both Kevin Prince-Boateng of Ghana and Jerome Boateng of Germany start today. I am told by devout Dungeons & Dragons players that this is reminiscent of the gory internecine clashes between Orcs and Demons. More learned folk tell me that it evokes Edgar and Edmund's baleful feud in King Lear. Some other chump is bound to mention Oasis. 7:21pm: Ghana, in my opinion, have been at times glorious in this tournament, at times galling. Perhaps they're poor decision-making under pressure can be excused by their age - this, on average, is the youngest team in the tournament, after all. But it's still annoying that they have very often chosen to pass when they should shoot and shoot when they should pass. Had they kept cooler heads they would have clobbered Australia in the last match and practically be into the enxt round already. Let's hope they are not so profligate tonight: I'm looking at you, Mr Prince-Boateng, the worst culprit so far (along with Chile's equally excitable/greedy Mark Gonzalez). 7:24pm: Out stride the teams to a mighty din. National anthems: The camera lingers on Prince-Boateng during the Ghanaian one, and he has his hand on his heart, his eyes firmly closed eyes and his face rigid with concentration ... or nerves. What a moment for the lad, all the same, playing in the World Cup against the country of his birth and his brother for the country of his father. We celebrate this tournament but it is really an anachronism, isn't it? Making a man choose like that wouldn't be right in anything more significant than a football festival. 7:29pm: "Please help!" simpers Francesca Cannas. "Some of the German players have this plastic strip down the sides of their shirts and some don't! I noticed the same thing on Argentina's kit yesterday. Why?! Does anyone know? It's driving me nuts." 1 min: Ghana get the game under way and make their intentions clear from the start, tearing forward with gusto. World Cup 2010: Mexico shed fear factor ahead of Argentina showdown | Amy Lawrence. For Chileans who have suffered the tragic consequences of an earthquake and tsunami recently, the gift of something to cherish was there to be seized. A crowd of 50,000 descended upon Santiago's main thoroughfare on Friday night to honour Chile's progress in the World Cup. They were the last of what has been a clean sweep of South American teams, with all five contenders from the Conmebol region reaching the last 16 of the tournament with barely a hiccup. Argentina won their group with maximum points. Brazil, Uruguay and Paraguay topped theirs without losing a game. Chile won plenty of friends as they went for the jugular, only to end up behind Spain on goal difference. Unlike their European cousins, there have been no shambolic exits, no shamed faces, no bad-tempered uprisings and no apologies to report. Even Diego Maradona has been as sweet as pie. Considering not a single South American side made it as far as the semi-finals at Germany 2006 (which comprised an all European quartet) this new show of dominance has puffed up the chests of those involved. The iconic Uruguayan schemer Enzo Francescoli thinks the difference is the dual experience South American players have of top-level football on their own continent during a gruelling qualification campaign allied to regular club football in Europe. "The players are used to a different standard of football, with different tactical movements and different marking styles. South American football is another thing altogether," he reckons. If you include the progress of Mexico and the USA, teams from the Americas make up almost half of the last 16. Unbridled confidence is a common theme. So too is a commitment to go for it. Although Brazil might not yet be winning as many marks for aesthetic impression as the world has come to expect, it has been refreshing to see Uruguay, Paraguay, Chile, Mexico and Argentina take the field with the attacking vibrancy and fluency that comes with a three-pronged attack. Even in their undiluted misery back home in France and Italy, it would take a footballing spoilsport of the highest order not to want to cast an eye over the rendezvous between Argentina and Mexico at Soccer City in Johannesburg this evening. History may teach Mexicans not to be overly optimistic, but the health of their football is such they do wonder when they can unlock their vast potential. Their football industry is one of the highest ranked in the world in terms of revenue and income. Outside Europe, support for the domestic league is surpassed only by that of Brazil. They have always been importers of talent and now they are exporting, too. It has a thriving TV business and acts as the gateway for broadcasts to South and North America. Around 20,000 Mexicans travelled to South Africa, despite the cost of most tours (an average of $13,000 – £8,640) being well beyond the reach of all but the very rich. Javier Aguirr

World Cup 2010: Diego Maradona serious about Mexico challenge. All the usual elements were there - acknowledgement of the opposition's strengths, discussion of the state of the squad and the need to focus on the task in hand - but once again the eccentric streak of Diego Maradona caught the attention at the Argentina manager's press conference ahead of this evening's last-16 encounter with Mexico. Before addressing a packed audience in Pretoria, Maradona clambered over reporters to bear-hug his friend and former Napoli team-mate Salvatore Bagni, who is working as a pundit at the tournament in South Africa. "Even knowing him as I have done all these years, Diego always does something to surprise you," said Bagni. The surprise thus far has been Argentina's serene progress through the tournament. Despite predictions of an implosion after a difficult qualifying campaign and constant scrutiny of Maradona's management style, the albicelestes strolled through Group A with maximum points to set up a repeat of their second-round meeting with Mexico in 2006. On that occasion, Argentina required a Maxi Rodriguez rocket in extra time to go through, and while Maradona said his side will not underestimate the challenge this time around, he believes they are well equipped to progress. "Mexico are a team who you need to respect and we are going to do that," he said. "But they should also respect us and our history. They have a number of players who concern us. If [the Argentinian-born striker Guillermo] Franco doesn't play, that's better because they won't have headers. But we have our match to play, we have our players to field. "We know the match is going to be tough, but with the team we have and the players we have we can move forward. The present situation is great because we've fulfilled our duty to win the first matches and from here on we start building physically and mentally for the next step." Argentina are likely to be without the centre-back Walter Samuel, who was injured against South Korea in the group stage - "If he is not 100%, he won't be able to give us the help we need," said Maradona - with Nicolas Burdisso expected to continue in his place against the Mexicans. Despite their form thus far (as well as a perfect record they are also the joint top-scorers with seven goals), Maradona attempted to downplay the hype - while also taking a pop at his former detractors. "I keep on saying the others are the favourites," he said. "We're here to fight. We're in good shape, although of course there's always a few small things to solve within the squad." "But what makes me angry is the lack of respect towards the players. People were saying we were a disaster, [that] we were the worst team they've ever seen, and now they are saying we are an excellent team. We have the same players as before, so you shouldn't believe what everyone says." Diego Maradona said that his side will respect Mexico but he believes that Argentina can continue to prove their doubt

World Cup 2010: Mexico's great love-hate relationship with Argentina. When Mexico's president, Felipe Calderón, published an analysis last week of the drug wars that bleed our country, it came as no surprise he referred so much to the US. After all, it's part of our culture to point the finger at Uncle Sam whenever something bad happens to us. If we're an underdeveloped country with an everlasting economic crisis, child obesity, industrial pollution and a shameful political class, we think it's because of them. Of course that's only perception. What's for real is that sometimes they treat Mexican immigrants as criminals, and that over 150 years ago they took from our country the modern-day states of California, Arizona and New Mexico in the infamous Mexican-American war. Those are some of the main reasons why the "Star Spangled Banner" is booed everytime the USA play in the Azteca Stadium. The others have to do with football. Over the past years the US soccer team has been our biggest rival; our nemesis; the team we love to hate so much that it seemed impossible there could be another one. But then came Argentina. We've never fought a war against them. Neither have they invaded us, or taken a square foot from our territory. In fact we've always had a good relationship. Argentinians make us feel at home when we visit Buenos Aires and never behave like Idaho spring-breakers when they come to Cancún. Besides, there are thousands of "porteños" who live in Mexico City. Some of them own excellent Argentinian restaurants, others work as creative divas in advertising agencies. If they're women and they're pretty, they get work as actresses or models; and if they play football a little better than the Argentina-born Guille Franco, they might get Mexican citizenship and play for our national team in the World Cup. Nevertheless, we cannot stand their football team. We just can't. How could we when La Albiceleste has beaten El Tri over and over again? Back in 1993, when the best Mexican side of all time reached the final of the Copa América, Argentina beat us 2-1. Later on, in 2005, when Mexico were playing the best football in the Confederations Cup, they kicked our ass in the semi-final, and a year later they did it again in the second round of the World Cup. On Sunday, when both teams face each other for the 10th time in five years, millions of Mexicans will be praying for revenge. But even the Virgin of Guadalupe can't do much when they have Lionel Messi and Gonzalo Higuaín on their side, while we have Franco on ours. So we can expect that Argentina will win, as usual; Mexico will be looking for someone to blame, as always; and the USA will remain in second place among the teams we love to hate, forever. World Cup Daily: Magnificent Mexico, forlorn France, and awesome Argentina. James Richardson's joined in the studio for this edition of World Cup Daily by Barry Glendenning (oh yes, he's back), Amy Lawrence, and Gregg Roughley to look back on a day of such exciting football even Greece scored. We begin by analysing France's lacklustre defeat by Mexico. Has a team ever played less for their manager than Les Blues did for Domenech this evening? Your answers on a postcard, please. Next up for the French of course is the hosts. Niren Tolsi from the Johannesburg Mail & Guardian tells us about the mood in South Africa now that Bafana Bafana's hopes of qualifying for the second round seem all but lost. Also in the podcast, we purr over Argentina's demolition of South Korea in the day's early kick-off. Higuain may have got the hat-trick, but the game was all about the magnificence of Lionel Messi. But can Diego Maradona's men go all the way with such a poor back line? Finally, we hear from Owen Gibson in Cape Town ahead of England's game with Algeria, and Raphael Honigstein brings us the latest from the Germany camp. Tweet us, and we'll see you here tomorrow for what will have been our special live show in front of an audience. How exciting... And if you want to watch Maradona juggling a ball to a bit of Eurodisco, look no further. Argentina turn on the style against South Korea, while France falter against Mexico. Plus, the latest from the England camp, and a preview of Germany's game with Serbia

World Cup 2010: England and Germany put old stereotypes behind them. This is the England-Germany conflict that finally kills the myths. The English are meant to tiptoe into Bloemfontein today fearing German tournament know-how and death by penalty shoot-out and the Nationalmannschaft are supposed to be cocksure that history is in their corner. The English inferiority complex and the German sense of destiny have run their course here in South Africa. Fabio Capello's ageing squad have soldiered through too many big Champions League and domestic tests to believe Germany are invincible in World Cup competition. England's streetwise players lack the capacity to see themselves as slaves to the past. In this spirit comes a poignant retraction from Franz Beckenbauer, who had been taunting Capello's players from the get-go. "Kick-and-rush" team was Der Kaiser's first provocation, then he said England had blown it by finishing second in their group. "I apologise because I like England and I like English football," Beckenbauer now says. "Maybe it was a reaction because I was disappointed [with England's performances] and maybe in a bad mood." These mind games are archaic. Germany's greatest sportsman realised in the end that the time for playing on old insecurities has passed. Today's is a very modern confrontation: "a derby", as England's Jamie Carragher puts it, but not one freighted with the post-war imagery of raddled mother country versus a super-competent master. Not on the pitch, at any rate. "I think this is a type of game which both teams will approach with a great deal of respect," says Joachim Löw, the Germany coach, who has stayed true to Jürgen Klinsmann's liberalising principles. "But while there will be respect you won't find fear in anyone's eyes, neither on the English or German side. Both teams will act with courage and while we have a young team, we will be able to hold our own. You can say it's youth versus international class, Germany v England, and we're looking forward to it." Youth versus "international class" is the new reality in Anglo-German relations, though Löw is excessively kind to express England's qualities in those words. Germany reached the semi-finals of the last World Cup and the final of Euro 2008. They are the ones with international class. What he means is that Premier League wealth collides with the German faith in self-renewal: in coaching, education, technique, skill. The Football Association problem-solve by buying Capello and five Italian aides. Germany return to teenage energy and feed five players from last summer's Under-21 squad into the senior set-up. Part of the beauty of this last-16 encounter is that both cultures arrive with a strong self-image, a confidence, admittedly, that was not apparent in England's 1-1 draw with USA or the soporific 0-0 against Algeria, which exposed Capello's players to lifelong ignominy, even disgrace. World Cup 2010: England are better than Germany, says David James. David James has dismissed fears that Germany have a psychological stranglehold over England and insists Sunday's second-round tie in Bloemfontein will be won long before a penalty shoot-out "because we are a better team". The Germans have progressed further than England in every World Cup finals since 1966, though Fabio Capello will take heart from the reality that his players have largely gone unscarred by previous defeats inflicted by the old enemy. Rather than dwelling on defeat on spot-kicks in 1990 and at Euro 96, his squad have drawn inspiration from their friendly success over Joachim Löw's side in Berlin in November 2008 when the visitors' makeshift side triumphed 2-1. "I'm genuinely confident with the team that penalties won't be an issue," said James. "Why? Because I think we're a better team than Germany. We played them in Berlin and beat them 2-1 in a game which we should have won more comfortably. They're a strong side, ranked sixth in the world, but we are not fearful of them at all. We are confident we can beat them. We have got through the qualifiers, the group stage, into the knockout, and we are confident we can make the British happy by beating Germany. We will respect them, but they are beatable. "The manager picked a squad of 23 based not just on footballing ability but also on their mental capabilities. As a group of players we're not sitting around bringing up the romantic side of things, saying: 'Look what happened this time we played Germany, or what about this time?' The one comment that has been made is: 'What about Berlin? We beat them 2-1.' So there was an achievement that a large nucleus of this group of players have been involved in. We're talking about our achievement." Only four of Capello's current squad started England's last loss to the Germans, under Steve McClaren at Wembley in August 2007, but only Frank Lampard, James Milner and Joe Cole of those expected to be involved at Free State Stadium have never beaten these opponents. The likes of Steven Gerrard, Ashley Cole and Emile Heskey can even conjure memories of the 5-1 World Cup qualifying victory in Munich in 2001. Capello welcomed Ledley King back to full training at Royal Bafokeng today following a groin complaint, but is expected to field an unchanged line-up in Bloemfontein with Matthew Upson retained at centre-half, Jermain Defoe up front and Milner on the right of midfield. Jamie Carragher, restored after a one-match ban, could yet come into his thoughts at centre-back, though the Italian is inclined not to disrupt a winning side. "All the players trained and everyone is fit to play," said Capello. "It is a good feeling. If they train, they are fit to play, so I have more options. I have to choose. Four players reveal what they remember about England v Germany. Sir Geoff Hurst 30 July 1966 Wembley England 4 West Germany 2 (aet) It happened more than 40 years ago but hardly a week goes by even now without me being reminded about some aspect of the day England won the World Cup. The victory generated a whole range of emotions from jubilation and patriotism to anger and frustration, depending upon your loyalties. The German fury about the third goal has diminished over the years, but they've never forgiven us. I have to admit that I had a bit of sympathy for them because they genuinely believed it didn't cross the line. It's a long time now since England's only World Cup victory and it remains an abiding source of pride to me that I was part of that historic occasion. Terry Butcher 4 June 1990 Turin 1-1 (pens 3-4) We just about scraped through to the semi-finals but once there we played our best football. We should have won but it went to penalties and, as is often the case, the Germans had the edge. We had beaten West Germany 3-0 in Mexico in 1985 and felt we could do so again. That belief was there even after we went 1-0 down, so when Gary Lineker equalised nobody on our side was surprised. We had chances to get a second goal but it never came. There were a lot of tears in the dressing room afterwards but once we got home we realised that actually we'd achieved something special. Andreas Brehme 4 June 1990 Turin 1-1 (pens 3-4) I remember feeling that this could be a real classic. There was no sense of a rivalry as such – I had never played them in a tournament before – but it was obvious they were going to cause us some problems on the night. England had a lot of momentum and great players, and we knew it would be a really tough game. I remember it as a very tense, even match that could have gone either way, with plenty of chances, people hitting posts and so on. I don't really remember too much about the penalties apart from the overwhelming feeling of relief after going through. Stuart Pearce 26 June 1996 Wembley 1-1 (pens 5-6) When I look back at Euro 96 and through the team that we had, you talk about leadership and that side was so full of leaders. Paul Ince, Tony Adams, Alan Shearer, Gary Neville, Gareth Southgate, the list goes on. You can name Gerrard, James, Lampard, Terry, it is like Euro 96. We'd have won the tournament if we'd won the penalty shoot-out. We didn't, and went out to the best team. That's life, it hurts and the pain never mellows. But I still think about that game, and the 1990 semi-final, because I draw experience from them. I have to make sure the lessons learned are passed on. World Cup 2010: Germany plan for penalties before England match. Fireworks greeted Germany's squad when they arrived back at their base in the dusty nowhere land outside Pretoria after beating Ghana on Wednesday night – and, despite the lateness of the hour, there was warm applause from hotel staff too. But among Joachim Löw's youthful squad no one was carried away with a narrow 1-0 win. As the centre-half Arne Friedrich put it yesterday: "I think we're self-critical enough to realise that such a performance won't be good enough against England. At the back we allowed far too many opportunities and further forward we didn't create enough chances." Manuel Neuer, Germany's skyscraper of a keeper, agreed: "We didn't play to the best our abilities. We were under intense pressure and we need to step up against England." • Follow the Guardian's World Cup team on Twitter • Sign up to play our great Fantasy Football game • Stats centre: Get the lowdown on every player • The latest team-by-team news, features and more That process has already begun. That will almost certainly come in the form of an email detailing where every England player likes to place his penalties. But such confident talk was rarely heard yesterday at the Velmore Hotel, where Germany are encamped for this World Cup. There were no chests puffed out or arrogant soothsaying – just the admittance of the obvious: Sunday's game is likely to be close. "At the moment England haven't really shown what they are capable of but I'm convinced they remain one of the favourites," the Germany assistant coach, Hansi Flick, said. "Any team that has star-studded players is to be reckoned with and they have learned a lot from Fabio Capello. World Cup 2010: Chile 1-2 Spain | World Cup match report. Spain avoided becoming the third of European football's anciens régimes to be toppled at this World Cup – and a potential clash with Brazil in the last 16 – by overcoming Chile in a thrilling and highly tense encounter. Despite going two goals up through David Villa and Andrés Iniesta, and facing a side reduced to 10 men when Marco Estrada picked up a second caution by tripping Fernando Torres, they endured some nervy moments after Rodrigo Millar pulled a goal back. But the game petered out as both sides realised that, with Switzerland struggling to overcome Honduras, they would go through together if the status quo was preserved. Chile now face Brazil in Pretoria on Monday evening, while Spain – as group winners – meet Portugal in Cape Town on Tuesday. "I think we've overcome a very difficult time," the Spanish coach, Vicente del Bosque, said. "We were very disturbed emotionally by the defeat to Switzerland but we are more optimistic now and I'm sure that will be reflected on the pitch." With Iniesta returning on the right after recovering from a thigh injury, Spain switched David Villa to the left and allowed Fernando Torres to lead the line. It was the first time in the tournament that Del Bosque was able to play his first-choice front three and suddenly the old Spain, the team that had lost only once in 49 matches before the World Cup, were back. And Chile were in the firing line. Two chances were created in the opening five minutes, both for Torres. A header on tip-toes went over the bar; a shot from Joan Capdevila's punt forward was blocked by Gonzalo Jara on the stretch. But Chile did not flinch and Iker Casillas had to leap high from a standing start to paw away Alexis Sanchez's lob. It was a fast, free-flowing game – and hot-tempered with it. Gary Medel picked up a yellow card which will rule him out of the Brazil game after a foul on Sergio Busquets. Waldo Ponce was lucky to see only yellow when he pushed at Torres's shins with his studs in full view of the referee, Marco Rodríguez, in a foul that carried echoes of David Beckham's sending-off against Argentina in 1998. And Marco Estrada was cautioned for sliding through Busquets. Worse was to come. At this stage Chile's quick one-twos and fast breaks were troubling Spain but halfway through the first half another rush of blood to the head – this time from the goalkeeper, Claudio Bravo – led to Spain taking the lead. Bravo charged out of his area to stop Torres running on to Xabi Alonso's pass but his sliding block fell only to Villa who – composure personified – looked up before curling a shot first time from the touchline into the unguarded net. But Chile, urged on by their raucous fans, kept pushing forward. One moment summed up the thrilling nature of the first half as Piqué held his head in his hands after nodding Xavi's corner over the bar – only to realise that Chile had launched another thrilling counterattack. World Cup 2010: Slovakia v Italy - as it happened | Scott Murray. It may seem daft to say of a country that's won the tournament four times, a record bettered only by Brazil, but Italy have done more than their fair share of suffering at the World Cup. In 1950, a team decimated by the 1949 Superga air disaster understandably decided to travel to South America by boat. Knackered and out of shape after a two-week cruise, Italy immediately lost their first game against Sweden, a result which effectively put them out at the very first hurdle. It was Italy's first defeat in a World Cup match, and the first-ever by any World Cup holder. (Uruguay hadn't bothered to compete in 1934 and 1938.) They then flew back home. In 1954, they were knocked out by Switzerland. 4-1. In 1958, they were sent packing by Northern Ireland. In qualifying. The 1960s were a comic tour de force. In Chile in 1962, two Italian journalists wrote pieces comparing the host country to a midden – not particularly tactfully, as there had been an earthquake two years previously killing 6,000 people – and so the home team responded to the slight by hoofing the Azzurri around like old socks in the infamous David Coleman baiting Battle of Santiago. The finals of 1966 brought the ignominy of North Korea and Pak-Doo Ik, the team met upon their return from England by an airport-full of radged-off supporters wielding sizeable boxes of assorted ripe organic produce. Italy reached the 1970 final, but embarrassed themselves in the match. Coach Ferruccio Valcareggi, whose "staffetta" relay policy of giving playmakers Sandro Mazzola and Gianni Rivera one half each had got them through the quarters and semis in a flurry of goals, touched cloth when it came to the crunch. In the final, Rivera didn't come on until four minutes from time; Brazil tonked them 4-1 in a second-half rout. In 1974 Italy at one point fell behind to Haiti, going out in the first round; in 1978 Holland did for them in what was effectively the semi-final with two preposterous long-range strikes. Then there are the penalty years – painful and unlucky defeats to Argentina in 1990, Brazil in 1994, and France in 1998 – and of course Ahn Jung-Hwan's golden goal for South Korea in 2002. It's not been plain sailing, is the point here. And now this: draws scraped against Paraguay and New Zealand. New Zealand! Italy need to win to be sure of going through, and that ain't a shoo-in. Slovakia, then. They've been pretty negative so far, but manager Vladimir Weiss's son - the imaginatively monickered Vladimir - has been a revelation down the right wing. Robert Vittek and Stanislav Sestak have looked lively enough up front, too. A Slovak win isn't beyond the realms at all; could this be another addition to Italy's surprisingly long list of World Cup catastrophies? Kick off: 3pm. The stadium: Ellis Park, Jo'burg. After steady performances against Cameroon and Holland, Japan needed only a point from their final Group E match, against Denmark, to qualify for the second round. Despite that, very few observers gave them a second thought as possible qualifiers for the last 16. Danish fans interviewed on Japanese television before the game were unanimous in their expectation of a routine victory – an opinion that was shared by most of the European media. Afterwards Honda said he would be satisfied only if Japan progress further. "I had expected to be really jubilant if we won today," he said. "Why is that not the case? Good afternoon everybody and welcome to our minute-by-minute coverage of this Group F encounter between Paraguay and New Zealand, which is being played at Polokwane, World Cup 2010's fourth highest match venue at 1,229m. Having provided the two biggest upsets of the tournament to date, New Zealand are on a hat-trick today, and could eliminate group leaders Paraguay if they beat them and the result of something interesting happens here. Possible Group F permutations • If Paraguay win or draw against New Zealand, they will qualify for the last 16. • If Paraguay win, they will top the group. They will also be guaranteed first place in the event of a draw, as long as Italy fail to win their game against Slovakia. • If Paraguay draw and Italy win, top spot will come down to goal difference. • If Italy beat Slovakia they will qualify. If they draw, they will still qualify as long as New Zealand fail to defeat Paraguay. Their fate will come down to goals scored if New Zealand draw against Paraguay. •If New Zealand beat Paraguay, they will advance. If they draw, they can only qualify as long as Italy draw against Slovakia and they finish with more goals scored than La Nazionale. •If Slovakia beat Italy, they will be guaranteed progress providing New Zealand do not overcome Paraguay. If Slovakia and New Zealand both win, the European side will be tied with Paraguay on four points and goal difference will decide who goes through. Two nations divided by a common language and a long-gone empire both advanced to the round of 16 today as Portugal and Brazil tested the patience of their referee, Benito Archundia of Mexico, after a thoroughly mean-spirited goalless draw whose few scoring chances went mostly to the European side. The 2006 World Cup – Ghana's first – was one thing, he said, but Angola and Togo had qualified for that; this was something more concrete. World Cup 2010: How Germany planned a flair revolution | Paul Hayward. Mr Lurve, as they kept calling him at the pre-match press conference, promises a "cut-throat fight" when Germany face Argentina in Cape Town. Here, in a combustible World Cup quarter-final, is the perfect test of the new German identity. Still strong in spirit, but now thrilling to the eye, the Nationalmannschaft want to be the most entertaining side in South Africa. Joachim Löw's employers are not declining any compliments. "It was very encouraging to see that victory against England in Bloemfontein was not necessarily due to the old 'German' virtues of running, fighting and grinding out a result," Wolfgang Niersbach, the DFB's general secretary, says, "but rather to a superiority of skills that, thankfully, even many England fans were ready to acknowledge." This response to the Guardian's questions about Germany's stylistic transformation under first Jürgen Klinsmann and now Löw conveys the bright new mood of England's nemesis. There might seem a predictability about the urge to laud the German system as Löw's men prepare to confront Diego Maradona's Argentina but the Bundesliga's output of young talent shames the Football Association and the Premier League. "Ranking and results are not the only criteria. We have to look at our style, our play, our intentions," Löw says. "We thought about what football we wanted to display, what philosophy we wanted to follow, and how we'd implement that. We had an emotional match against England and after the 4-1 win we were enthusiastic. But the joy over that win lasted for a short time only. We know there's more to come. We want to go further." It is a testament to Fernando Torres's character that, despite another stinker against Paraguay on Saturday night, he braved the mixed-zone scrum to patiently answer yet more questions about his lack of form during this World Cup. But for all his insistence that his game is coming together, Spain again looked more cohesive when he retreated to the bench. One of the more telling statistics from Spain's grimy win concerned shots at goal. With Torres, they mustered four attempts in 56 minutes; without him there were 11 in the 34 minutes that followed, including David Villa's late winner. "David has been very important for us, the key for Spain," Torres said afterwards. "He is scoring important goals, he gives us the opportunity to keep looking forward thinking we can go all the way. It has been his World Cup." The same, alas, cannot be said for Torres, whose sub-prime performances are clearly causing him anguish. "It is a difficult tournament for me because my fitness is not the best," he said. "I am feeling better but I still have to improve, obviously. I didn't arrive in the best moment, especially in terms of fitness especially after two surgeries, but I have to accept my role in this tournament." Those words were spoken with fluency that has so far eluded him on the pitch, but he must have noticed how much more telepathically attuned Spain were with Villa in the centre and Andrés Iniesta, Xavi and Cesc Fábregas creating lovingly crafted triangles behind him. World Cup 2010: Germany and Uruguay commit to race for third place. The World Cup's third-place play-off has often carried an air of the game nobody wants to play, a meeting of losing semi-finalists with at least one eye on the flight schedule home and little to play for beyond the hope of a slightly milder feeling of anticlimax. Tomorrow evening's third-place match in Port Elizabeth, between Germany and Uruguay, will at least carry personal significance for a number of players. Three, Diego Forlán, Mesut Ozil and Bastian Schweinsteiger, are in the running for Fifa's Golden Ball award, for the player of the tournament, which will be announced on Sunday. • Watch brick-by-brick recreations of the best games • Relive the action with our Twitter replay interactive • Immerse yourself in our World Cup slideshow • The latest news, features and more on the final Thomas Müller, the Germany striker who scored twice against England, is one of three men nominated for the young player award. All the above are set to start in a match that will provide Germany with the opportunity to finish third at the World Cup for a second time running. For Uruguay, third place would be their best finish since 1950 and the striker Luis Súarez, who returns from suspension , says this is a match with a significance far beyond the personal. "For this squad, the World Cup is not over yet. Now what's left is to play to the death for third place," Súarez said. These sentiments were echoed by his manager, Oscar Tabárez. "We have no guarantee we will win but we will prepare ourselves for a fight to the death," he said. "The challenge for Uruguay is to finish third in the World Cup. I know Germany and they will make it very difficult for us, so we must fight hard to win." While Fifa will be delighted to hear its pre-final match receiving such passionate avowals of commitment, the subplot of individual honours will be intensified by Súarez and Forlán having an outside shot at securing the Golden Boot, for the tournament's leading scorer. Uruguay's strikers have four goals each. Two more tomorrow and a blank in the final for David Villa of Spain and Wesley Sneijder of Holland may be enough to earn either man the coveted award. Germany's Miroslav Klose, who is a doubt for the match thanks to a back injury, has also scored four but he has a bigger prize to aim for: one more World Cup goal would make him the tournament's all-time joint top scorer, with Ronaldo of Brazil. It remains to be seen whether such personal incentives can inspire a third-place match to remember. Fifa will at least be hoping for a happier climax to the race for the Golden Ball than the last tournament provided. Four years ago in Germany Zinedine Zidane of France was voted player of the World Cup, an award that was announced shortly after he was sent off for headbutting Italy's Marco Materazzi in the final. Germany and Uruguay have a host of reasons to make tomorrow's World Cup third-place play-off in Port Elizabeth

World Cup 2010: it's Holland v Spain in Woking. Woking, which not even Woking would consider a flashpoint, could have a bloody Sunday. How so? Unusually for a British town, it has large colonies of both Dutch and Spanish people. And it's Woking, Surrey. Who knew? I stand outside the International School of London in Old Woking Road, looking for Dutch fans packing heat. These fathers are IT consultants and oil men at Royal Dutch Shell. "The whole community is dressed in orange," says Marco Damhuis, the headmaster. "We will watch the game in the conference room at the Talbot Inn in Ripley. We will turn Ripley orange." Another man says he is going to the pub in Egham, so Egham might be orange too. "2-0!" shouts a grandfather from his car. "No, I make a mistake. 3-0!" "I am nervous already," says a tall, thin man. "During the 1978 Holland v Argentina World Cup final I remember crying my eyes out and my mum trying to console me and my dad saying: 'I feel like crying too.'" The score was Argentina 3 Holland 1. Did I know, asks his friend, that the Dutch national anthem contains the lines "To the king of Spain I've granted/ A lifelong loyalty," because the Netherlands used to belong to Spain? "We might have to reconsider that," he says. The odd thing is that the Dutch, in Woking at least, do not seem to hate the Spanish. They hate the Germans, in a downbeat way. "It is a disappointment that we are not going to play Germany," says another. "We were looking forward to having revenge." For losing the 1974 World Cup final, he explains: West Germany 2 Holland 1. "It has been a long time since there was something this exciting," he says. "There was a Dutch player at Wimbledon and we got very excited but that was a long time ago." When pressed for the tennis player's name he can't remember. So where do I find the enemy the Dutch don't seem to mind? I head for Barcelona, the tapas bar in Goldsworth Road. Any Spaniards? "No," says the bartender, glancing at her colleague. "I'm English, he's French and the kitchen's Hungarian. We are only going to be a tapas bar for another two months anyway. Then we are going fusion." It's the same in the Mediterranean Food Emporium opposite: "I'm Greek and she's Italian. Next door [in the Cafe Continental] they are all Portuguese." So I go to the newsagents. Spaniards? "I don't know of any," say the Englishman. "We had a hairdresser here who is Spanish but he shut the shop and went missing." World Cup 2010: Dirk Kuyt outlines Holland's plan to rattle Spain. Dirk Kuyt has accused Germany of playing with fear in their semi-final defeat by Spain and insists the Dutch will not make the same mistake when the sides collide in Sunday's World Cup final in Johannesburg. The Liverpool forward suggested the Germans had been intimidated by the European champions, with the Dutch convinced there are weaknesses in the Spanish set-up which can be exploited as they attempt to secure the trophy for the first time. Holland go into the final unbeaten in 25 matches and bolstered by 14 successive wins, in qualifying and the tournament to date, in the World Cup. "We are not afraid at all," said Kuyt. "You could see the Germans were afraid of Spain. They didn't try to attack. We are going to attack and then you will see weaknesses coming to the surface. If you play like the Germans you are definitely going to lose. We don't have players who are afraid and we don't have players who feel small against the big opponents. There is respect, but not fear." The Germans struggled to impose themselves on Spain, who monopolised possession for long periods before finally chiselling out the game's only goal 17 minutes from time. The Dutch will not allow themselves to become as becalmed while the Spanish enjoy the ball and will draw inspiration from their recovery in the quarter-final win over the much fancied Brazilians as they seek to succeed where the celebrated Holland sides of 1974 and 1978 could not. "Dutch football is always very strong and, in every tournament we play, we show we have quality," said Kuyt. "But, in this tournament, we have shown we have more than just quality. We also have belief. We believe we can beat every opponent in front of us. We have the patience to do that. Against Brazil we were 1-0 down, but we still had the conviction that we could win. "Against Uruguay in the semi-final they equalised and, in tournaments in the past, we would have gone on to lose it from that point. But this time we stayed focused. We believed in our own qualities. We kept our positions and, in the second half, were the only side that deserved to win." The Dutch coach, Bert van Marwijk, has sought advice from the likes of Johan Cruyff and Ruud Gullit over the course of the tournament, and Rudi Krol has been among his coaching staff, but he is adamant his side are capable of making their own history with victory on Sunday. World Cup 2010: Holland v Spain. Previous meetings Surprisingly, the two teams have played each other only nine times, with each side winning four and one draw. But the Dutch have had much the better of recent meetings: Spain's last win came way back in 1983, since when Holland have come out on top three times out of four. Expectations The two sides share a desperation to win what would be their first-ever World Cup. "We are so close to writing history," said Dirk Kuyt. "This is the third time we have reached the final. Now we have to win it. We felt before the tournament we had the quality in the team to win it. We are so close. There is nothing bigger than the World Cup." Spain's Gerard Piqué says: "It's a great chance for us to make history and win the World Cup. Other squads have the experience of winning the World Cup in the past, but it is our dream." Key clash Mark van Bommel has drawn praise and rage in almost equal measure with his performances during the tournament, providing an effective shield in front of his defence if a little prone to committing niggly fouls. The 33-year-old will need to ensure that he keeps his discipline iron tight against a side that loves to pass the ball across and around the area he normally polices, and avoid getting sucked into failed challenges on the likes of Xavi. The managers The 59-year-old Vicente del Bosque has spent the vast majority of his adult life as an employee of Real Madrid, and has just one, brief experience of life outside his country – an unproductive year at the Turkish club Besiktas. The Holland coach, Bert van Marwijk is 19 months younger than his managerial opponent and considerably more itinerant. He and also had one unhappy spell outside his country, at the Bundesliga club Borussia Dortmund. Both coaches took charge in 2008, since when Del Bosque has largely stuck with an already successful side, but Van Marwijk has made extensive tactical changes. Sunday 11 July, 7.30pm Soccer City, Johannesburg

World Cup 2014: Croatia profile – Ivan Rakitic. This article is part of the Guardian’s World Cup 2014 Experts’ Network, a co-operation between 32 of the best media organisations from the countries who have qualified for the finals in Brazil. theguardian.com is running previews from four countries each day in the run-up to the tournament kicking off on 12 June. Each summer, the Swiss border town of Moehlin really comes alive for a day. That is the day when Moehlin’s most celebrated son, Ivan Rakitic, brings a whiff of top European football into the otherwise sleepy place, tucked safely among the canton of Aargau’s wooded hills just south of Germany. Rakitic owns the local club NK Pajde, whose name can most accurately be translated from Croatian as “chums” or “buddies”. They play in lower Swiss divisions and are entirely a product of a family affair: Ivan’s dad is the chairman, his uncle is the director of football and his brother the player-manager. Ivan himself is the sugar-daddy. “Yes, that’s about right – but it’s also a hobby of mine,” he laughs. “Most players there are my old friends, neighbours, relatives … These are the people who I played with as a kid, they are my lifelong buddies. We have a family atmosphere at the club and like to hang out on the football pitch. I still sometimes play, or even train with them whenever I get the chance.” Once a year in the off-season, Rakitic gets his other friends, some of them Switzerland and Croatia international players, to come to his hometown and play an exhibition game against Pajde. The word on the streets of Moehlin is that he even got some of his old pals – like Germany’s Manuel Neuer, who was his team-mate at Schalke – to chip in for the club’s budget. His birthplace means a lot to him. It was here that he first fell in love with his parents’ home country, watching Croatia play at the 1998 World Cup. “When Robert Prosinecki scored for us against Jamaica,” Rakitic told FourFourTwo, “I was so happy that I ran onto the balcony and climbed the fence in euphoria. To be fair, our flat was only on the first floor, so it couldn’t really end up tragically if I fell over...” It was also here that, when Rakitic Jnr was 16, that his father, Luka, had a very serious and potentially life-changing conversation with a distinguished guest – a member of José Mourinho’s coaching staff who tried to woo Ivan to Chelsea, offering Luka a job in London. By that time, his son had already been a standout in Basel’s youth ranks and the family kindly rejected the offer, but not without much consideration. And it was also here that Ivan informed his dad of his decision to represent Croatia – the player later said the family never tried to force him into making the choice, but that his dad shed “tears of joy” upon being told what it was. This article is part of the Guardian's World Cup 2014 Experts' Network, a co-operation between 32 of the best media organisations from the countries who have qualified for the finals in Brazil. theguardian.com is running previews from four countries each day in the run-up to the tournament kicking off on 12 June. Mario Mandzukic Mandzukic apparently never wanted to do anything other than play football. When he was choosing which high school to go to, his father Mato later revealed, Mario went for whichever was closest to his home. The idea was not to waste too much precious time on going there and back, instead spending it on the nearby playground where he used to kick the ball for hours every day. The type of education he received in that school was very specific: apart from being a professional footballer, Mandzukic is also a ceramic tiler. It seems that he hasn't changed much. Whenever asked about his hobbies, Mario claims he has none. "My private life would bore you," he once said. "I feel best spending time at home with my family and girlfriend." He's also been very successful at keeping his partner, Ivana, away from the media. They never appear in public together, but hers is one of two names written on the boots he wears on the pitch. Oddly enough, the other is the name of his dog. 'My pug Leni makes me happy' – was his only comment about it. Robert Kovac – assistant manager Many footballers date and marry models, but it's usually one of the biggest stars in the team who has the most famous WAG. In Croatia's case – it's the assistant manager. Anica Kovac was the first runner-up in the Miss World 1995 beauty pageant and she married Robert Kovac six years later. They have three children. But Robert – who is not only the assistant, but also the younger brother of the Croatia manager Niko – can also say he married a hometown sweetheart. The curiosity of Cameroon and the World Cup is that the country is both Africa’s highest achiever and its worst under-performer. It is tragic that corruption and farce have prevented Cameroon from capitalising on the success of their first two World Cup adventures, in 1982 and 90. Giorgio Chiellini is not malicious, the way that he is portrayed. It seems implausible now, given his starring role in Liverpool’s strongest title challenge for 24 years but, only nine months ago, Raheem Sterling was at risk of becoming another of England’s lost talents. That charge was dropped, but two court cases inside 12 months prompted the Liverpool manager to issue some harsh advice. Sterling listened, as he thankfully has done with guiding figures throughout his life, and his career, club and country have benefited. World Cup 2014: Colombia to show spirit against Greece despite injury list. Colombia’s injury list is no excuse for underperforming on a World Cup stage their coach, José Pékerman, has made clear by insisting Radamel Falcao’s absence would not derail their campaign and had strengthened the squad’s spirit. Pékerman’s message on the eve of Colombia’s opening game against Greece on Saturday appeared to be for his players’ ears as much as for supporters back home, with the squad weakened by injuries to influential members including the veteran defender Luis Perea and the key midfielder Edwin Valencia, as well as Falcao. Colombia’s Argentinian coach described the Monaco striker’s failure to recover from a cruciate ligament injury in time for the World Cup as his saddest day in the job. Twelve days on, however, he claimed the team had moved on, has an enviable array of attacking options available and a camaraderie epitomised by Falcao’s and Perea’s decision to accompany the squad in Brazil. “That day we were suffering and it was not something that was said to make a good impression; it came from inside,” said Pékerman. “This is a serious group, it is a family. Perea and Falcao will be with us. They could have gone on holiday but they decided to come with the group because they are never away from us and a part of us. This has always been a close-knit team, a family, and in any family you have to deal with problems. “We have dealt with our problems and now we are not thinking about what is behind us but the potential of this team. “Any team in the world would love to have Falcao as its striker but we are strong, we can deal with this and with the game against Greece. You will see for yourself. “To play for the national team requires a different mentality and the players have accepted that. We have so many good strikers and they must know the coach loves them and will not abandon them. We have never lost our solidarity in the face of something so difficult.” Pékerman spoke highly on Friday of Víctor Ibarbo, the Cagliari striker who did not feature in the qualifying campaign but has impressed in the recent friendlies, although the manager is expected to deploy Sevilla’s Carlos Bacca alongside Teófilo Gutiérrez against Greece in Falcao’s absence. He added: “Colombia and Latin American teams in general have had difficulties against teams like Greece in the past. We think in America there is only one way to play, with beautiful moves, but you have to be competitive. Colombia will try to play its own way, its own style, but when you change players they bring their own characteristics to the team.” Colombia’s José Pékerman described cruciate ligament injury to the Monaco striker as ‘saddest day’ but vowed Colombia will show their strength against Greece

World Cup 2014: England’s threat from Paulo Wanchope’s Costa Rica. Paulo Wanchope remembers Jim Smith throwing things about in the Derby County dressing-room. He remembers Paolo Di Canio, his West Ham United strike partner, giving pre-match pep talks to his own legs. And he remembers life on the end of a yo-yo at pre-Abu Dhabi investment Manchester City. English football, however, will mostly remember Wanchope for a single moment – when he sprinted from halfway at Manchester United, away from Nicky Butt and inside Phil Neville and Roy Keane before, silkily and instinctively, rolling his studs across the ball to open up the shooting chance. Gary Neville could not challenge in time and, in a blur of rubber-legged brilliance, the Costa Rican had guided it low into the far corner beyond Peter Schmeichel. It was April 1997, Wanchope was 20 and it was his debut for Derby. “Wanchope treated Manchester United’s defence … with absolute contempt,” thundered the local radio commentator. Derby won 3-2 and the goal would be voted by supporters as their favourite at the club’s 125-year celebrations in 2009. “When I go back to England, everyone still talks about that goal, which is a great surprise to me given that many years have gone by,” Wanchope says. “When I first got to England and I said: ‘I’m from Costa Rica’. Everyone was saying: ‘Where is that? You mean Puerto Rico? They play football in Costa Rica?’ So it was good for me to start my career in Europe like that. It’s something that I will always have in my heart.” Wanchope now looms into view for English football once again. As the Costa Rica assistant manager and, from the perspective of these shores, surely the most recognisable name in the set-up, he will attempt to spring another surprise in the World Cup group that contains Uruguay, Italy and England. He finished off his A licence this year at St George’s Park, alongside his old City team-mate Shaun Goater, and he plans to return for the Pro licence next year. The dream is to coach in England. But Wanchope is consumed by the project with Costa Rica, having joined Jorge Luis Pinto’s staff two-and-a-half years ago. The teardrop tattooed under his left eye reflects some tough times that Felipe Salvador Caicedo Corozo has been through during his life, especially when he was a boy; but he also says that when he sees himself in the mirror, it doesn’t let him forget where he came from. As a boy he played football with his friends in the streets of Guasmo, a poor and dangerous neighbourhood located in the south of Guayaquil in Ecuador. They would play without shoes, in the streets and with shabby balls, but always with the big dream of becoming a professional footballer. When he was nine, he started to play for Rocafuerte FC and – once he had got used to playing in football boots – gradually his football level began to rise dramatically and he was soon one of the most important players in the youth teams. Concrete proof of this came when he was called up to the under-17 Ecuador side and good performances in the World Championships caught the attention of some big teams in Europe. When he was 16, the French club Lyon appeared to be the most interested in him; everything was almost ready but then FC Basel of Switzerland nipped in and signed the young ‘Felipao’. It was a difficult decision for him to leave Ecuador but, after receiving the support of his family, he packed his bags and began his European dream. Ogenyi Onazi appeared to be just another unknown player in the backwaters of the Nigerian football system until fate played a part in his emergence in the autumn of 2009. Onazi was part of the Nigeria team who hosted the Under-17 World Cup, a tournament that also featured some of the planet's best new kids on the block, including Neymar, Philippe Coutinho, Isco, Koke and Mario Götze. Always competitive at junior levels (Nigeria won the 2013 Under-17 World Cup), Nigeria's preparations for that competition on home soil had been a study in how not to prepare a team, as the selection process was bedevilled by the removal of 17 members of the squad after they failed the MRI test that had been introduced by Fifa to curb age cheating. A new coach, John Obu, was forced to call up several replacements, including a pair from a church-sponsored team in Lagos called My People which was set up by senior prophet TB Joshua – they were the striker Sani Emmanuel and Onazi. At first, it was the latter who made more of an impression as he was named the tournament's best player having scored five goals on Nigeria's progression to the final, where they were beaten 1-0 by Switzerland. World Cup 2014: Hulk says he will be fit to face Mexico despite injury. Brazil striker Hulk limped out of a training session on Sunday morning but later said his injury was not serious and he expects to be fit for Brazil’s World Cup match against Mexico on Tuesday. The Zenit St Petersburg striker was playing for Brazil’s first string against a second string team in a training match, but limped off the pitch after scoring. He received treatment on the touchline but was unable to continue and the training complex. “I felt a niggle and came off as a precaution,” he told reporters shortly before the squad were due to fly to the northern city of Fortaleza. “I am good, I am happy and I hope to be 100 percent in two days for the next game. I hope it wasn’t anything serious, I feel good.It’s a normal pain. If I feel anything at all I come off to treat it.” Hulk had a similar issue ahead of the opening 3-1 win over Croatia last week, but played 68 minutes before being replaced by Bernard. The striker has been a regular starter under Luiz Felipe Scolari, and has personal reasons for particularly wanting to play in Fortaleza. “I am from the northeast and my family is going to be there,” he said. “It is a game that I don’t want to miss. I want to play and I am going to be ready, God willing.” Should Hulk be unable to start against Mexico in the Group A game, Ramires looks the most likely to step into Brazil’s starting eleven. The Chelsea midfielder moved into the first-string training side after Hulk’s departure, and impressed after coming on against Croatia.
